episode_summary.source_notes: Pilot episode. Father (Vietnam medic) accuses ER doctors of killing his daughter who came in with sore throat.
episode_summary.summary: Suzanne Morton dies in ER after being given meperidine by drunk Dr. Auster, which fatally interacted with her phenelzine (antidepressant). Dr. Ekballa Raza initially suspected but proven innocent when investigation reveals he wrote correct drug (acetaminophen) and falsified chart under pressure. Raza faced public scrutiny and career threats as foreign doctor before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: If the toxicologist finds meperidine in Suzanne Morton's body, you'll be lucky if you're only deported.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Raza confesses to white-out falsification: 'I wrote meperidine without thinking. But I gave her acetaminophen... when they look at me here, they see an Indian or a Paki... I have to be twice as good as everybody else just so they will think I am as good as everybody else.'
person_rows.notes: Raza was accused of causing Morton's death through prescribing meperidine. He admitted to falsifying records with white-out to avoid narcotic paperwork but insisted he gave acetaminophen. Investigation later proved Dr. Auster (Chief of Medicine) actually administered the fatal meperidine while drunk. Raza faced deportation threats and career damage as foreign doctor trying to prove competence. Eventually cleared and offered job by Chief of Medicine before trial revealed Auster's guilt.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows Laura di Biasi shoots two young men on subway after perceived threat. Michael Jones (aka Mosquito Mosket) dies; Darnell Chenault paralyzed. Case revolves around self-defense vs. premeditated revenge.
episode_summary.summary: Laura di Biasi, a former dancer turned hospital technician, shoots two young men (Darnell Chenault and Michael Jones/Mosket) on a subway after they allegedly threaten her. She claims self-defense; prosecution argues premeditated revenge stemming from a prior subway attack that ended her dance career. Jones/Mosket dies. The case focuses on di Biasi as defendant throughout—no falsely accused innocent parties are identified. Prosecution eventually offers a plea deal when new information about Mosket's criminal history creates reasonable doubt on murder charges. No person in this episode meets the criteria of being publicly accused then proven/strongly implied innocent.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete with full courtroom scenes and investigation details.
episode_summary.summary: Jack Curry is arrested and prosecuted for the murder of Bobby Holland, a gay man with AIDS. Curry admits to pulling the trigger but claims it was assisted suicide - that Holland wanted to die rather than suffer from AIDS. The prosecution argues Curry committed murder regardless of Holland's wishes. While Curry was initially suspected of serial murder (similar cases in LA and San Francisco), all three victims had AIDS and evidence suggests Holland may have wanted to die (he trashed his own apartment, paid off debts). However, Holland also had an appointment to discuss AZT treatment, suggesting he wanted to live. Ultimately, the jury acquits Curry of murder/manslaughter but convicts on reckless endangerment (a misdemeanor). No one in this episode is falsely accused and later proven innocent - Curry did pull the trigger on all three victims, the question was whether it constituted murder or assisted suicide.

episode_summary.episode_title: Kiss the Girls and Make Them Die
episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Paige Bartlett is strangled in her apartment. Two men had sexual contact with her that night: Steve Feinstein (ponytail boyfriend who dumped her) and Ned Loomis (ex-boyfriend who was with her later). Feinstein is initially suspected but has a solid alibi. Loomis is convicted of her murder. Steve Feinstein was publicly accused of murder, questioned by detectives multiple times, and labeled a suspect despite his alibi, causing exposure at his workplace and among his social circle before being cleared.

person_rows.episode_title: Kiss the Girls and Make Them Die
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'Did you ever hear about a new disease they got called AIDS?' Logan also calls Feinstein's magazine 'garbage' and says 'You don't know much about women, do you?' when questioning him. The detectives show clear contempt throughout.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives repeatedly visit Feinstein at his workplace/studio where he publishes 'Cyber Funk' magazine. A woman is present during questioning: 'Woman: Steve? / Feinstein: I'm kind of busy right now. / Woman: You keep me waiting and I won't be interested. / Feinstein: It'll just have to wait. / Woman: Steve? Oh-hh, a foursome? / Feinstein: They're cops.' Later: 'You got it. / Logan: And you never wanted to see her again? And you told her? / Feinstein: And you didn't sleep with her? / Logan: I just did it to shut her up. / Feinstein: Why didn't you tell us? / Logan: Just to shut us up?'
person_rows.notes: Feinstein was the initial primary suspect based on being seen arguing with victim and leaving bar with her. He had an alibi (dinner with investors from 9:30pm-3:30am verified by Eleanor Phipps and others). Despite alibi, detectives returned multiple times and questioned him publicly at his workplace in front of colleagues/associates. DNA testing proved he had sex with victim earlier in evening but his alibi placed him elsewhere during murder. Detectives showed clear hostility and moral judgment throughout questioning. No apology given once cleared. Qualifies as harm because he was publicly accused at his place of business, exposing him to colleagues despite having provable innocence via alibi.

episode_summary.source_notes: Classic Law & Order format with clear investigation pivot from Willie Tivnan to Janet Ralston and Gil Himes
episode_summary.summary: Alan Ralston is murdered in a parking garage. Willie Tivnan, a Black man with a criminal record, is arrested after his fingerprints are found on a crack pipe at the scene and Janet Ralston identifies him. However, detectives discover evidence pointing to Janet Ralston and her lover Gil Himes as the actual killers who planned the murder for insurance money. Willie is eventually cleared, but only after being publicly accused, arrested, and held on $100,000 bail.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Come on, where did you unload the credit cards? ... You may just have bought yourself a ticket out of here.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Judge: Willie Tivnan. The charges are murder in the second degree, attempted murder, and assault in the first degree. ... Bail is set at $100,000. ... Stone later uses Willie's testimony to prove Janet Ralston shot her husband.
person_rows.notes: Willie was smoking crack in the stairwell when Alan Ralston was murdered. His fingerprints on the crack pipe and Janet Ralston's false identification led to his arrest. He was held on $100,000 bail and publicly arraigned. Defense attorney Elizabeth Strickland believed he was innocent. Willie's truthful account of the light going out BEFORE the door opened (contradicting the theory he shot Ralston) helped prove Janet Ralston was the actual killer. Willie admitted to eight separate muggings but consistently denied the murder. No apology is shown despite his innocence being proven and his testimony being crucial to convicting the actual killers.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript available. Episode involves a conspiracy to murder Charles Halsey, who was serving as a bagman for corrupt officials. Two young black men initially accused.
episode_summary.summary: Councilman Charles Halsey is stabbed and later dies. Two black teenagers (Simonize Jackson and Tremaine Lewis) are initially arrested for the attack after being found with his possessions. They confess to robbery but claim Halsey was already wounded when they arrived. Investigation reveals Halsey was a bagman in a municipal corruption scheme. Anthony Scalisi (mob enforcer) actually stabbed Halsey on orders from corrupt officials (Deputy Commander Jefferson, Borough President Conti, Councilman Van Damme). The two teenagers were innocent of the stabbing, only guilty of robbery of an already-wounded man.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'Hey... shut up. We don't dig being dissed by lightweights.' Then threatening about Spofford youth facility: 'I'm not talking about a year at Spofford. Going... Going... Gone.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Simonize and Tremaine tell detectives: 'Look, he was already cut when we got there. We took him off, but we didn't do nothing to him. He started to choke. He was just lying there bleeding. It was that white cat in the Benz.' Later pleads to 'Possession of stolen property, six months.'
person_rows.notes: Jackson and Lewis were guilty of robbing Halsey's possessions after finding him already wounded, but innocent of the stabbing/murder. They confessed under pressure but maintained they didn't stab him. Their account was corroborated by truck driver Tony Hallowell who saw Scalisi at the scene. Pleaded to possession of stolen property.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Max yells 'Drop the gun, Tremaine!' during arrest. Earlier Logan threatens: 'If the councilman croaks, you guys move up to the bigs... murder two.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Lewis arrested at gunpoint in his apartment. Both suspects tell police about 'that white cat in the Benz' who 'got out as soon as the truck pulled off.' Trucker Tony Hallowell corroborates: 'there were two guys at the rear of the Jag... guy reaches in, pulls out a fur coat and hands it to the other guy' who gets in Mercedes.
person_rows.notes: Like Jackson, Lewis was guilty only of robbery of possessions from already-wounded Halsey, but innocent of the stabbing. Their testimony about the white man in the Mercedes (Scalisi) proved accurate. Pleaded to possession of stolen property, six months.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete with full courtroom and detective scenes.
episode_summary.summary: Irv Diamond dies after a heart attack while with prostitute Jolene Curtis. Driver Cookie Molina removes Diamond from the hotel, dumps him in Central Park, and hits him with a bat to fake a mugging. Investigation leads to Laura Winthrop's high-end prostitution ring 'Poppy Escorts.' Jolene is HIV-positive, raising reckless endangerment concerns. Cookie testifies against Winthrop in exchange for a walk. Winthrop ultimately pleads to involuntary manslaughter. No person was falsely accused then cleared - Diamond's death investigation correctly identified those responsible (Cookie, Jolene, Winthrop). The case focused on prosecuting the actual perpetrators of the prostitution ring and Diamond's death.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Officer Freddo Parisi shoots Tommy Richardson, a Princeton student, claiming self-defense during a drug deal. Investigation reveals Richardson never had a gun—Parisi planted his weapon after the shooting. Silky Ford, who sold the gun, is initially misidentified but protected by Parisi's threats. Partner Davis covers for Parisi until confronted with evidence. Richardson is publicly portrayed as a drug dealer during investigation/trial, destroying his reputation as a community hero. Parisi commits suicide before verdict.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Silky testifies: 'Tommy turns to say something, and the cop shoots him. Tommy didn't have no gun. Freddo takes my gun, he wipes it on his shirt—twice...and puts it in Tommy's hand.' Also: 'When they brought the Richardson boy to the morgue, he had $3,000 on him. The transaction never took place. He never bought Silky's gun.'
person_rows.notes: Tommy Richardson was killed by Officer Parisi who then planted a gun on him to justify the shooting. The accusation (that Tommy drew a gun on police) was fabricated by Parisi placing his own weapon in the victim's hand. Tommy's reputation as a Princeton honors student and community benefactor was destroyed when his drug dealing activities were revealed during the investigation and trial. The public exposure occurred through media coverage ('Did you know Tommy Richardson lectured against drugs? Can you make some kind of a statement?') and courtroom proceedings. Though Tommy was involved in drug dealing (partially_involved in that crime), he was completely innocent of the accusation that killed him—attempting to shoot a police officer. His death and the destruction of his heroic reputation in the community constitute severity 4 harm.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Freddo at Silky's cell: 'He says if I tell anybody, he'll kill me. Says it's my lucky day...Keep your mouth shut, or you're dead. Even if I burn him, his partner'll get me.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Davis initially identifies Silky in lineup but softly: 'Could be...could be number three. I'm not sure enough for an ID.' Greevey notes: 'It's hard to believe that his gun was there and he wasn't.' Later Silky arrested: 'You have the right to remain silent.' Silky threatened by Parisi to stay silent about witnessing the shooting.
person_rows.notes: Silky Ford was briefly suspected of being present at the shooting scene when Davis and Parisi soft-identified him in a lineup, though both knew he was the actual witness who could expose Parisi's cover-up. Silky was arrested on weapons charges. Though Silky was guilty of selling the gun and drug dealing, he was innocent of participating in any shooting or drawing a weapon on police. The key harm was Parisi's direct death threats to keep him from testifying, plus his arrest. Exposure was primarily within law enforcement (police_only), but the arrest and death threats from a cop constitute material harm (severity 3). Eventually cooperated after being set up on drug charges and offered a deal.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript of Law & Order S1E9. Episode focuses on child abuse case involving Dierdre 'Didi' Lowenstein. Both parents are accused and tried; mother Carla confesses and pleads to manslaughter, testifies against husband Jacob who is convicted of murder.
episode_summary.summary: No qualifying persons found. Both Carla and Jacob Lowenstein were accused of murdering their daughter Didi, and both were ultimately convicted (Carla of manslaughter via plea, Jacob of murder). Neither was proven or strongly implied innocent. The episode's focus is on the tragic death of an abused child and the prosecution of both parents responsible for her death. No false suspects or wrongly accused individuals appear in the transcript.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript analyzed for false accusation patterns
episode_summary.summary: Artist Victor More dies during a sexual asphyxiation session. Commissioner Henry Rothman is charged with manslaughter after his fingerprint is found on a Polaroid taken during More's death. Evidence shows Elizabeth Hendrick, a wealthy patron, was present and ordered Rothman not to save More. Rothman initially testifies against Hendrick, then recants under her influence and commits suicide. Hendrick is ultimately arrested based on Polaroids Rothman left behind. No individuals were falsely accused and later proven innocent; the investigation correctly identified the perpetrators.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows a 16-year-old girl claims to have been raped by white police officers. Investigation reveals she fabricated the story to get permission for an abortion (she was pregnant by boyfriend Jordan Hill). Congressman Eaton uses the case for political purposes. FBI rape kit comes back negative. Girl and mother eventually admit the hoax.
episode_summary.summary: Astrea Crawford, 16, claims white police officers raped her and wrote racial slurs on her body. Two uniformed officers (Teal and Rudolph) are publicly named by Congressman Eaton. Investigation reveals the rape never occurred - Astrea fabricated the story because she was pregnant by boyfriend Jordan Hill and wanted an abortion but feared her father's reaction. FBI rape kit negative. Officers Teal and Rudolph are cleared with substantiated alibis. Case becomes politically charged with Congressman Eaton using it to attack the justice system, despite knowing or suspecting it was false.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cragen: 'The two uniforms Eaton named have substantiated alibis for the time in question.' Earlier, Eaton publicly names them: 'Teal and Rudolph, a couple of night shift uniforms from 7-3.'
person_rows.notes: Officer publicly named by Congressman Eaton in press conference/public rally setting. Has substantiated alibi proving innocence. Exact consequences to career/reputation not specified but public naming by elected official in racially charged case constitutes significant harm.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cragen: 'The two uniforms Eaton named have substantiated alibis for the time in question.' Earlier, Eaton publicly names them: 'Teal and Rudolph, a couple of night shift uniforms from 7-3.'
person_rows.notes: Officer publicly named by Congressman Eaton in press conference/public rally setting. Has substantiated alibi proving innocence. Exact consequences to career/reputation not specified but public naming by elected official in racially charged case constitutes significant harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Abortion clinic bombing; Mary Donovan dies delivering bomb she thought would be set at night; revealed she was actually seeking abortion
episode_summary.summary: Anti-abortion activists Rose Schwimmer and Celeste McClure conspire to bomb Chelsea Women's Choices clinic. Mary Donovan, believing she's delivering a bomb for the cause, dies in the explosion. Investigation reveals Mary was actually pregnant and seeking an abortion under a false ID provided by her boyfriend Patrick Dunn. Patrick is briefly arrested as co-conspirator based on fingerprints on the fake ID, but he was actually helping Mary get an abortion, not plant a bomb. The episode explores the abortion debate while prosecuting Schwimmer and McClure for murder.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Patrick: 'I'm not going to jail, Kevin. I can prove I wasn't involved.' Kevin: 'Mary wasn't involved either. Shut up! Shut up!' Stone: 'Hey!' Kevin: 'All he wanted was for her to be a martyr instead of a sinner. She didn't go there to bomb the clinic. She was pregnant. She was there to have an abortion... That morning I gave her the ID and the money so that she could... I pleaded with her to at least let me take her. I pleaded.'
person_rows.notes: Patrick's fingerprints were on Mary's false ID card, leading to his arrest for conspiracy to bomb the clinic. In reality, he gave her the fake ID and money so she could get an abortion anonymously. He was trying to help her avoid shame with her family, not plant a bomb. He pleaded with her to let him accompany her. Exposure appears limited to police interrogation room with Kevin present; no indication of public exposure at work or to family beyond Kevin.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Officer Rennick, a corrupt cop who was skimming money from drug busts and killed dealer Ricky Powers, attempts to murder his partner Officer Sandoval on a rooftop to silence her. She kills him in self-defense. Brutus Walker is initially suspected of killing Rennick but is later cleared. A 15-year-old girl, Ravina Price, is briefly suspected when her fingerprints are found on the murder weapon. The episode explores police corruption and the breakdown of the partner bond.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Profaci: 'If it was you and walker in an alley and no one else was there, he didn't have a gun and was gonna give himself up... I'd shoot him in the face.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone presents evidence to Walker and Simpson showing Rennick killed Ricky Powers, not Walker. Walker says 'Hundreds of brothers wanted him dead!' Stone eventually reveals tape proves Sandoval killed Rennick in self-defense, clearing Walker of cop-killing.
person_rows.notes: Walker was guilty of throwing Ramos out the window and killing drug dealer Ricky Powers under coercion from Rennick, but was innocent of killing Officer Rennick. The transcript shows widespread assumption he killed the cop, leading to manhunt and arrest. He faced potential execution ('dead man' if stays on street). Detectives openly discussed shooting him on sight. He was arrested on screen for Rennick's murder before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Max: 'Where'd you get the gun, Ravina?' Ravina: 'Don't touch me! Don't you touch me! I don't let no man touch me.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Greevey: 'Great, 15-year-old high school girl. Some neighborhood kid. She says she picked it up in back of the building.' Max: 'She got no motive to shoot a cop.' Greevey: 'Problem is, her prints are on the gun. Brutus walker's aren't.'
person_rows.notes: 15-year-old girl found the gun and picked it up. Her fingerprints on the murder weapon led to her being questioned. She became distressed during interrogation, pulling a gun (the murder weapon) in fear before being talked down. No indication the accusation spread beyond the detectives, but she experienced coercive interrogation and significant distress. Cleared when detectives realize she had no motive and simply found the weapon.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Three young men (Metzler, Hanauer, and Ryan Cutrona) are accused of gang-raping reporter Monica DeVries. The case is dismissed due to victim's repressed memory and lack of DNA match. Investigation reveals a fourth perpetrator, Timothy Pruiting, who actually committed the rape while the three younger defendants primarily held the victim. Ryan Cutrona, the youngest and most reluctant participant, eventually testifies against Pruiting. The three younger men are re-arrested for rape (holding/accessory roles), but they are factually less culpable than initially accused of being the primary rapists.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Any jury that sees those tapes will send you up for a lot more time than your buddies'll do. / I can only work with what I have on hand, and if your client plans to martyr himself with a heavier sentence, that's not my problem.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Ryan is 17, terrified, held at gunpoint in court by co-defendant. Initially accused as equal participant in gang rape. Later testimony reveals: 'We really didn't think he'd go through with it... We just thought we'd set him up, he'd back off, get scared.' Evidence shows he was present but Pruiting was the actual rapist.
person_rows.notes: Ryan is partially_involved because he was present and held the victim, which is criminal. However, he was initially accused as a primary rapist (equal to Metzler/Hanauer), when the actual rape was committed by Timothy Pruiting. The harm from being publicly accused of gang rape exceeds his actual culpability (holding/accessory). He's 17 with no record, terrified, and threatened in open court. The prosecution initially treats him as equally guilty, causing severe legal/reputational harm disproportionate to his role. Eventually becomes state witness against Pruiting with reduced charges.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: We weren't able to leverage Hanauer, or Metzler, and the younger kid is not forthcoming.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Metzler confesses 'we really didn't think he'd go through with it' about Ryan. Recording from police car: 'We didn't do her. He got off on it... All we did was hold her, maybe some other stupid stuff, but we're not riding down for Pruiting.' Narcotics says Metzler has been 'running for [Pruiting] for months.'
person_rows.notes: Metzler is partially_involved as an accessory who held the victim during Pruiting's rape. Initially charged as equal participant/co-rapist in gang rape. The actual penetration/primary assault was committed by Timothy Pruiting. Metzler's criminal conduct (holding victim, being present) is real but distinct from the rape itself. Public accusation as gang rapist causes legal/reputational harm exceeding actual role. Eventually case dismissed then re-filed with more accurate understanding of roles.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: We weren't able to leverage Hanauer, or Metzler.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Recording from police car with Metzler: 'Because if he is, I'm not taking the fall, and neither should you. We didn't do her. He got off on it... All we did was hold her, maybe some other stupid stuff, but we're not riding down for Pruiting.'
person_rows.notes: Hanauer is partially_involved as accessory who held the victim during Pruiting's rape. Initially charged as equal participant in gang rape. The actual penetration/rape was committed by Timothy Pruiting. Hanauer's role was holding the victim and being present - criminal but not the primary rape offense for which he was publicly accused and indicted. Case dismissed then re-filed with corrected understanding of each defendant's role.

episode_summary.source_notes: This is Part 1 of a two-part episode. Resolution likely occurs in Part 2.
episode_summary.summary: Isaac Skolnick is brutally beaten in his candy store over a bootlegged cigarette conspiracy. Edgar Hoover, a homeless man, is initially accused but later cleared when the real attacker (Joe Pilefsky) is identified. Hoover dies shortly after. The case expands into organized crime involving the Masucci family. Harvey Beigal cuts a deal to testify about a separate murder (Russell Mackey), which allows him to walk on the Skolnick assault conspiracy despite being the one who ordered it. The Mackey case collapses due to perjured testimony from Pilefsky (part of Masucci's strategy), and Beigal's plea bargain stands despite the outcome.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: GREEVEY: 'There is no way he could be charged with any crime let alone serve any time.' DEFENSE ATTORNEY: 'I'm sure you gentlemen are familiar with the word "incompetent."' GREEVEY: 'Yeah, well, from where I sit he's competent enough to do 3½-to-10 years.' Later cleared when HOOVER says: 'I saw a guy come out the back in the alley. Mean looking. Fancy suit. Like Donald Trump.' He later IDs Pilefsky: 'That's the guy. Etched in stone.'
person_rows.notes: Hoover was found with $112 from the register and was initially treated as a suspect. He was homeless and mentally ill, making him an easy target. However, he consistently maintained he saw someone else and later identified Pilefsky. He was cleared but died shortly after in a hotel from alcohol, which the detectives had provided. His exposure was limited to police interrogation room with his defense attorney present. No public exposure or material harm beyond the interrogation itself, so severity is 1.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete. This is Part 2 of a multi-part episode dealing with organized crime.
episode_summary.summary: Following a failed prosecution of mob boss Frank Masucci for murder (set up via perjured testimony in Part 1), Stone pursues various members of the Masucci crime family. Laurel McGinty, a buildings inspector, is accused of bribery and threatened with prosecution to flip on Harvey Beigal (Masucci's brother-in-law). She is proven innocent of the primary crime narrative (the murders) but guilty of the bribery she's accused of. Joe Pilefsky is coerced through threats and a staged prison incident into cooperating. The episode culminates with Masucci being murdered, orchestrated by his own sister Katherine Beigal.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: The DA's subpoena is a powerful weapon. If I have to I will question every landlord of every building you inspected over a decade. One of them, I assure you, will turn you in to protect his own good name.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone: 'If you testify you get immunity. If you don't, you go to prison. I'm sure the former is preferable, don't you think?'
person_rows.notes: McGinty is actually guilty of bribery, but she is innocent of any involvement in murders or organized crime beyond being bought off by Beigal. She is threatened and coerced into testifying. Given she is guilty of the crime she's accused of, she does not qualify as a false accusation victim under the rubric.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'I got this friend over at The Post, hasn't had a hot story for months. I bet it would be a big boost to his career if I gave him an exclusive on how Joe Pilefsky ratted out Frank Masucci. What do you think, Max? Old Joe here would be breathing another, what, five, maybe six days? Nah, three tops.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Pilefsky is convicted of assault. Police then threaten to plant a newspaper story saying he ratted on Masucci, and stage a 'Judas kiss' from D'Agastino in prison yard to make it appear he's cooperating, forcing him into witness protection.
person_rows.notes: Pilefsky is guilty of the assault he's convicted of. He is also guilty of perjury from Part 1. He does not qualify as falsely accused. His coercion by police is notable but he is not innocent of his charged crimes.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: An 11-month-old baby is killed and his 12-year-old brother critically wounded in a drive-by shooting. Police initially suspect the older brother Gregory Winters of drug dealing, but he is proven innocent. The real crime was a botched contract killing by drug dealer Michael Ingrams against real estate agent Ned Kay, who stole $300,000 from him. The 14-year-old hit man went to the wrong apartment (315 Jacobs Place instead of 315 Jameson Place).

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Max: 'We need a motive. Was Gregory involved with... with what? Drugs?' Denise: 'Wednesday and Thursday nights, Saturday mornings, Gregory plays basketball. Monday and Tuesday nights, he got choir practice. Gregory ain't perfect, but he ain't into no dope. I can guarantee you that.' Later, Coach confirms: 'Gregory Winters was a classic point guard... Kid was the brains of this team... He thought those guys [gang members] were lame.' Max later says: 'I'm sorry.'
person_rows.notes: Gregory is a 12-year-old shooting victim who falls into a coma. While he's unconscious and unable to defend himself, detectives theorize to his grieving mother that he might be a drug dealer, causing her additional pain. The accusation is made in front of his mother, who forcefully defends him. The coach and further investigation prove Gregory was a good kid focused on basketball and choir. Max offers a partial apology ('I'm sorry') but it's brief. The harm is the emotional distress caused to the mother and the insult to Gregory's character during a traumatic time.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-trial
episode_summary.summary: Jose Urbano, a drug dealer and parolee, is shot at a church fair. Initially, Hector Cartagena (Urbano's associate) is suspected and arrested. However, evidence later reveals that Nicky Guzman shot Urbano in revenge for raping Guzman's fiancée Lucy Rivers. Alicia Rivers (Lucy's sister) is also arrested for conspiracy but charges are dismissed. Both Cartagena and Alicia are publicly accused but later cleared. The transcript ends mid-trial for Nicky Guzman.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Max: 'Your ass is grass, hombre. You're on parole. You can't hang with felons. Even dead ones.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives arrest Cartagena based on witness Anita's statement that 'Hector said Jose was no good to his friends. He was mad, he wanted to kill him.' Later released when evidence points to Nicky Guzman.
person_rows.notes: Cartagena arrested publicly at his residence with backup. Threatened with parole violation. Released when investigation shifted to Nicky Guzman. Has extensive criminal record but proven innocent of this murder.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Bailiff: 'The charges are murder in the second degree and conspiracy in the first degree.' Judge later dismisses: 'In the interest of justice, I'm dismissing on the girl.'
person_rows.notes: Lucy Rivers' sister, works for church. Arrested publicly in front of family for allegedly luring Urbano from church fair. Charged with murder 2 and conspiracy. Held on $100,000 bail she could not afford. Charges dismissed by judge after grandmother's testimony revealed Nicky Guzman as actual shooter. Stone used her arrest strategically to pressure grandmother into testifying. Legal Aid lawyer Shambala Green noted this was done 'to force her to talk.'

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial and arrest of Petrovich.
episode_summary.summary: Karl and Evelyn Jarmon are murdered in their home. Their sons Greg and Nick are arrested based on fingerprints on the murder weapon, powder residue, financial motive ($10M inheritance), and weak alibi. The boys had history of abuse from their father. Later investigation reveals business partner Alex Petrovich orchestrated the murders as part of Russian organized crime scheme to take over Jarmon Printing. Charges against the sons are dropped; Petrovich is convicted.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, publicly named as suspect, trial by media, social ostracism ('People stare on the street. Far as a lot of people are concerned, we'll always be guilty.')
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detectives press both brothers hard in interrogation, take paraffin tests, fingerprint analysis. Max to Captain: 'nine times out of ten, in the house...kids kill parents.' Logan challenges lack of evidence but Max pushes: 'In my book, top of the hit parade.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Greg to Stone after charges dropped: 'You know what it's like? People stare on the street. Far as a lot of people are concerned, we'll always be guilty.'
person_rows.notes: Greg's fingerprints were on the shotgun (legitimately from skeet shooting that day). Powder residue from same activity. He made the 911 call finding his parents dead. Despite lack of solid evidence connecting him to the actual murders, he was arrested publicly. Stone eventually realizes the sons are innocent and drops charges when Petrovich is identified as the real killer through witness testimony and organized crime investigation.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, publicly named as suspect, trial by media, social ostracism ('People stare on the street. Far as a lot of people are concerned, we'll always be guilty.')
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Max pressures both brothers throughout interrogation. When they provide alibis, Max dismisses: 'The alibi is nothing. Nobody at the game ever saw them leave.' Stone notes: 'They were sure enough a week ago to make an arrest.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Nick to Stone after arrest is explained: 'You know what it's like? People stare on the street. Far as a lot of people are concerned, we'll always be guilty.' Earlier, Nick explains the family history: 'Mr. Jarmon was beating Gregory with a cane. Nick had to put a stop to it. And so he hit him.'
person_rows.notes: Nick had previously broken his father's jaw defending his younger brother Greg from a beating with a cane. This history of violence against the abusive father made him appear guilty. He was arrested alongside Greg based purely on circumstantial evidence (weak alibi, motive of inheritance, family dynamics). Charges dropped when real killer Petrovich identified. Stone acknowledges: 'The system makes mistakes' and promises: 'We are not going to re-indict.'

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows Law & Order S1E20 'The Troubles' involving IRA member Ian O'Connell and federal prisoners.
episode_summary.summary: A federal prisoner (Ahmed Mustafa) is murdered while in transit. Initially both surviving prisoners (Savino Montez and Ian O'Connell) are suspects. Montez is later found hanged in his cell, initially staged as suicide but proven to be murder by prison guard Patrick McCarter on O'Connell's orders. O'Connell, an IRA member held for 5 years without charges, is eventually prosecuted for Mustafa's murder after being granted asylum. Multiple people experience serious consequences from accusations before being cleared or shown innocent.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Max: 'Pay up!' Logan shows Montez's sheet: 'Twice, murder two. Both by strangulation.' Coroner on Mustafa: 'Crushed larynx, broken windpipe, strangulation.' But later McCarter testifies: 'The organization told me to shut the Cuban's gob before he talked about O'Connell killing the Arab.' Montez was killed to prevent testimony, implying he was not Mustafa's killer.
person_rows.notes: Montez was a federal prisoner with violent history, making him initial prime suspect for Mustafa's murder based on MO match. However, McCarter's testimony reveals Montez was killed specifically to prevent him from testifying that O'Connell killed Mustafa, strongly implying Montez was innocent of Mustafa's murder. He was murdered (severity 4) as consequence of being a potential witness against O'Connell.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete with full courtroom resolution.
episode_summary.summary: Drew MacDaniel is found in Central Park missing a kidney, which was illegally harvested and transplanted into Joanna Woodleigh. Dr. James Reberty and nurse Elaine Hale are arrested for the crime. While Reberty initially tries to shift blame to Hale, both are eventually convicted. Phillip Woodleigh (Joanna's father) is also prosecuted for conspiracy after evidence shows he knowingly paid for the illegal organ procurement. No innocent persons are falsely accused in this episode - the investigation correctly identifies the perpetrators from the start.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows a case about evidence tampering in a money laundering prosecution. Three bankers were acquitted after computer discs showing drug money trails were erased. Investigation reveals conspiracy involving Congressman Wilson, Chief O'Farrell, Officer McCrory, and Detective Shearer.
episode_summary.summary: Three bankers accused of money laundering were acquitted after evidence (computer discs) was tampered with. Captain Cragen was investigated and publicly suspected by IAD but proven innocent when he wore a wire that implicated Chief O'Farrell. The actual conspirators were O'Farrell, Congressman Wilson, McCrory, and Shearer.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: IAD: 'How long they been in there? About an hour. Internal Affairs?' Max asks what gives, Cragen responds: 'These guys taking a run at the whole precinct? No, just me.' Later, Stone threatens: 'I'm going to have you up on departmental charges, friend.' Cragen proves innocence by wearing wire that gets O'Farrell's confession.
person_rows.notes: Cragen was under formal IAD investigation with colleagues aware. He faced threat of indictment and departmental charges. Property clerk sign-in logs showing his presence two days in a row made him primary suspect. Stone explicitly threatened to indict him to pressure cooperation. Cleared when he wore wire proving O'Farrell's guilt. Stone acknowledges afterward: 'You did your best. I had to push you. I had no choice.'

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript involves Det. Max Greevey's murder investigation and subsequent coerced confession by Det. Mike Logan.
episode_summary.summary: Detective Max Greevey is murdered before testifying in a grand jury corruption case. His partner Mike Logan tracks down and coerces a confession from Daniel Magadan Jr. at gunpoint in an alley. Though Magadan confessed and was ultimately convicted, the confession was coerced under threat of death. The episode focuses on the legal consequences of Logan's conduct and the admissibility of evidence derived from the coerced confession. Magadan is convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to 25 years to life.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript analyzed per rubric
episode_summary.summary: Edward Cullen and his girlfriend Alexandra Beckett are murdered. Doug Phillips, Alexandra's ex-boyfriend, is arrested based on prints, motive (jealousy), and phone records. He is later cleared when evidence shows Melanie Cullen (Edward's estranged wife) committed the murders. Phillips was publicly arrested and indicted before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LOGAN: 'With a nicer apartment than yours.' CERRETA: 'Hey, listen, everybody's a suspect until they're cleared.' LOGAN: 'Your girlfriend liked a lot more than the view.' LOGAN: 'Hey... up yours, pal.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: STONE: 'Okay, we'll dismiss on the boyfriend. Pick her up.' Earlier: 'Douglas Phillips, you're under arrest for the murder of Edward Cullen and Alexandra Beckett.' Court scene: 'Not guilty. Your Honor, these are vicious murders committed by a privileged young man...' Judge: '$200,000.' Later dismissed when Melanie Cullen arrested.
person_rows.notes: Phillips was arrested, bail set at $200k, publicly accused in court of being 'a privileged young man who considers himself beyond the norms of society.' Charges dismissed when new evidence pointed to Melanie Cullen. No apology shown on screen. Police were verbally aggressive during interrogation.

episode_summary.source_notes: 19-year-old actress Priscilla Blaine dies from amitriptyline overdose (200+ mg, 8+ tablets). Her mother Elizabeth Blaine pushed her into pornography to fulfill her own failed acting dreams. Priscilla left a desperate taped monologue for her mother a week before death. Mother is convicted of criminally negligent homicide.
episode_summary.summary: No falsely accused innocent persons are identified in this episode. The investigation consistently focuses on the victim's mother, Elizabeth Blaine, who is ultimately convicted. While the legal theory shifts from suicide to accidental overdose during trial, no other suspects are pursued or publicly accused then cleared. This is a case about parental pressure leading to death, not mistaken identification.

episode_summary.source_notes: Nathan Robbins stabbed to death during street robbery. Christian Tatum ('Lemonhead') initially accused, later cleared. James Polesky convicted.
episode_summary.summary: Nathan Robbins is stabbed to death outside a coffee shop. Christian Tatum ('Lemonhead'), a homeless man with severe mental illness, is arrested when police find the victim's ring in his jacket pocket. He claims his friend 'James' did the stabbing. Investigation proves James Polesky committed the murder; Tatum accepts plea deal and testifies against Polesky. Tatum suffers severe mental deterioration from trial stress and anti-psychotic medication, ending up institutionalized at Bellevue in a 'rubber cell.'

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, jailed, forced medication causing severe side effects, mental deterioration, institutionalized in psychiatric facility
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Stand up. Your psychiatrist tells me that you have moments of rational thought. Now we're gonna go back in there, and we'll take care of you... and you... are gonna have one of those moments.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone pressures Tatum to testify despite his terror: 'James is going to kill me, don't you understand?! He's gonna slice me! He's gonna feed me to the pigeons!' Final scene reveals his fate: 'So does Lemonhead... rubber cell at Bellevue.' Dr. Olivet earlier warned: 'The man's on anti-psychotics... which leaves him nauseous, listing to one side, and in danger of choking to death on his own tongue... the guy's hurting, and I don't think he can survive another circus.'
person_rows.notes: Tatum was homeless and severely mentally ill (psychotic, not psychopathic per Dr. Olivet). Initially arrested for murder based on ring found in his jacket. He claimed it was given to him by James Polesky. Police/prosecution coerced his testimony despite his deteriorating mental state. Though he accepted a plea deal (rob two) and testified against Polesky, the process destroyed him. He ended up institutionalized at Bellevue in a 'rubber cell' - a life-altering consequence. The harm (arrest, detention, forced medication, mental deterioration, institutionalization) directly resulted from being wrongly accused of murder, even though he was later proven innocent and the real killer (Polesky) was convicted.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony.
episode_summary.summary: Parents Ted and Nancy Driscoll are charged with manslaughter after their daughter Dinah dies from untreated strep throat due to their religious beliefs forbidding medical treatment. The case focuses on their Church of All Saints faith in spiritual healing. There are no falsely accused innocent parties—the parents are the defendants throughout, and while the prosecution struggles with proving their guilt, they are never cleared or replaced by other suspects. The housekeeper Mrs. Amado and church practitioner Sharon Barlow are witnesses, not accused persons.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at final cross-examination
episode_summary.summary: Amy Newhouse is attacked and loses her pregnancy. Initially, detectives suspect her boyfriend Chris (a former disbarred lawyer turned cabbie). Evidence later points to her boss David Alcott, with whom she had an affair. Alcott is arrested and publicly tried for murder, then assault. Eventually it's revealed Amy and Chris orchestrated the entire scheme: Chris was the actual father (not Alcott), they blackmailed Alcott, then staged the attack themselves to file a civil suit. The transcript cuts off during Amy's cross-examination about whether they knew the legal definition of murder in NY.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: David Alcott, you are under arrest for the murder of the unborn son of Amy Newhouse. [...] Excuse me, you just can't walk in without an appointment! [...] This is absurd, Your Honor. These charges... [arrested publicly at his law office, tried in court, $100,000 bail set]
person_rows.notes: Amy and Chris fabricated the entire case against Alcott. They knew Chris was the father (confirmed by doctor 12/19), but made Alcott believe the baby was his to blackmail him. Chris staged the attack on Amy (his alibi was proven false via odometer records). Alcott was arrested publicly at his office in front of colleagues, tried publicly for murder/assault, faced $100K bail, had assets frozen, wife filed for divorce. Though murder charges were eventually dismissed on statute technicality (fetus age), he endured full public prosecution. The harm to his reputation, marriage, finances, and career was severe and lasting despite being completely innocent of the attack.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: I know how it is, Chris. She ticks you off, so maybe you lose your temper. It happens all the time. Maybe I hit her once, but that was a long time ago [...] Hey, I know how tough it is raising a family. You're broke, you're young... The last thing you need...
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Chris was initially suspected but had an apparent alibi (cab fare to Kennedy airport). Detectives used coercive tactics during interrogation. Later proven he faked the alibi and was actually guilty of staging the attack.
person_rows.notes: Chris was initially a suspect but is EXCLUDED from person_rows because he was actually guilty of the crime (staged the attack on Amy as part of their fraud scheme). His false alibi was later exposed via odometer discrepancies. Including only for documentation purposes; not counted as falsely accused innocent person.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode involves a 30-year-old cold case; victim Tommy Keegan, age 8, killed in 1962; remains found in 1992/1993
episode_summary.summary: Two men (Dorfman and Conover) living in the same building as victim Tommy Keegan were suspected by police and neighbors after the boy's 1962 disappearance. Ed Conover faced public suspicion and a separate accusation of approaching a boy at a church youth group. Both men were innocent; the actual killer was Thaddeus Messimer, the father of Tommy's friend Julie, who pled guilty 31 years later.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Reverend: 'In the case of the missing boy? He was never charged... my understanding of the law, that makes him innocent.' Conover to detectives: 'In most people's eyes, a lot of them, he was automatically guilty.' Conover: 'suspicion almost destroyed Conover's life.' Messimer: 'Six months later... some kid, 14, 15, says Messimer made a pass. The boy who made the accusation, he was trouble... was wanting attention.'
person_rows.notes: Conover was initially suspected in Tommy Keegan's 1962 disappearance by police (Detectives McFadden and Shelby). Later accused of approaching a boy at St. Lucius Church youth group where he was a counselor. The reverend states the accusing boy 'was trouble' and 'wanting attention.' Conover describes how 'suspicion almost destroyed' his life. The exposure was community-wide (church, neighbors, employer Herald House). By episode's end, Messimer confesses to killing Tommy, fully exonerating Conover regarding the murder. The church accusation is also implied false based on reverend's and Conover's statements. Severity 3: career and reputation damage, though he survived into old age (now in nursing home).

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Lawlers: 'Two men... a couple. We wondered, but we thought it was awful to suspect them at first.' Messimers: 'We wouldn't want to speak ill of them, the way they were, but...' McFadden: 'You got a gay couple in the file? Two guys... 30s, 40s... lived on the block. Connors, Conway, something like that. The other guy... some German name. Couldn't make the case?'
person_rows.notes: Dorfman lived with Ed Conover in the same building as victim Tommy Keegan. Detective McFadden's notes from 1962 identify them as suspects ('gay couple in the file'). Neighbors (Lawlers, Messimers) suspected them based on sexual orientation. Died 9 years before episode (1983/1984), so not directly re-investigated, but was subjected to 1962 police scrutiny and neighborhood suspicion. Messimer's confession clears both men. Severity 2: public neighborhood suspicion and police investigation, though no arrest/charges found in transcript.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial verdict
episode_summary.summary: Andrea Fermi accuses fraternity members Ted Campion and Joel Holder of gang rape at a Halloween party. The case involves extensive investigation and trial, ending with both defendants acquitted. Andrea herself is not proven innocent of any accusation - she is the accuser/victim. Her ex-boyfriend Gary Burnham initially lies but cooperates after plea deal. No person in this episode qualifies as falsely accused then proven innocent with public harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: A bartender is killed in a hit-and-run. Investigation reveals his wife Jenna Kealey was having an affair with her 16-year-old student Roy Pack. Roy confesses to the murder, claiming Jenna manipulated him. Jenna claims she tried to hire a hit man (David Kaufer) but renounced the plan, and that Roy acted alone out of obsession. However, the prosecution discovers Kaufer was lying - Jenna paid him to pretend to be a hit man to manipulate Roy into committing the murder, creating a false renunciation story as insurance. Both Roy and Jenna are guilty - Roy of the actual killing, Jenna of conspiracy and manipulation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript of Law & Order S2E10. 53 people died in an arson attack on a social club (El Cielo). Investigation reveals conspiracy involving forged green cards.
episode_summary.summary: An arson attack on El Cielo social club kills 53 people. Police initially suspect Guillermo Ruiz, a drunk ex-boyfriend of a barmaid who threatened the club. Ruiz is brought in for questioning and his apartment searched, but his landlord provides an alibi (he was home asleep by 11:30). Investigation shifts to arson-for-profit targeting club owner Jose Rivera/Marcos, but Marcos dies in the fire. Police then identify Cesar Pescador through stolen green card use at hospital. Pescador confesses he was hired by Domingo Guerra to send a 'message' to Salvadorans who were threatening to expose Guerra's forged green card scheme. Guerra and INS agent James Collins are ultimately charged with the murders.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Listen. According to witnesses, you made a big stink last night around 11:00 p.m. What did you do after that? ... All right, let's go. ... Where were you all that time? ... Come on, Ruiz, think.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Landlord: 'Last night, sir, did you let Mr. Ruiz into the apartment?' Landlord: 'He said he lost his keys again... About 11:30. The news was just ending.' Logan: 'Do you know if Mr. Ruiz went out again last night?' Landlord: 'No way. That borrachon was dead to the world.'
person_rows.notes: Ruiz was the initial suspect based on witness reports that he was a drunk ex-boyfriend who threatened the club after being thrown out. He was arrested and questioned. His landlord provided a clear alibi that he returned home at 11:30 PM (before the fire at 1:00-1:15 AM) and was 'dead to the world.' No evidence of public exposure beyond the arrest itself. Police moved on to other suspects once the alibi was established.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end during tape playback
episode_summary.summary: Broadway producer Joshua Foster is found murdered, frozen for 5 years. Investigation reveals he was killed by his fiancée Leslie Hart and partner Gary Wallace after threatening to expose drug money funding their show. Mario Forcelli is initially investigated as mob-connected, but cleared. Both Hart and Wallace are publicly exposed through arrest, indictment, and trial.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Disposing of a body? That's obstruction and accessory to murder.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Logan and Cerreta confront Mario at his restaurant in front of staff: 'You guys don't know when to let up.' Detectives threaten him with obstruction charges for body found in his freezer. Mario responds: 'I freaked out a little bit, okay?' and 'This ain't ours. We didn't whack this guy.'
person_rows.notes: Mario Forcelli owns the restaurant where Foster's body was stored in a freezer. Detectives initially suspect mob involvement due to his family connections (father ran numbers, uncle under racketeering indictment). He was publicly confronted at his workplace and threatened with charges. Later proven innocent when brother-in-law Gabriel Hunt confesses to storing the body for the actual killer (driver Lloyd Danner). Mario had been in Curacao when body was discovered and had no knowledge of the murder.

episode_summary.source_notes: SVU episode, not original Law & Order. Transcript shows opening with SVU intro ('sexually-based offenses'). Victim Marnie Owens is a teacher murdered after sexual assault. Main suspect Phillip Montrose eventually indicted for manslaughter.
episode_summary.summary: High school teacher Marnie Owens is raped and murdered. Multiple people are investigated and publicly exposed before Phillip Montrose is indicted. Ethan Chance, a student, is wrongly accused and arrested publicly. Dr. Byron Marks is interrogated and exposed. Marcus Cole is questioned. All are later cleared when evidence points to Montrose, who eventually gets indicted for manslaughter (not murder) after testimony about victim's sexual history influences grand jury.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Making me run on my bad knee. I didn't do anything. Your mother's looking for you. You're under arrest.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Ethan arrested at teacher's memorial service: 'Fin, he's coming right at you. I got him. Move! Making me run on my bad knee.' Then read Miranda rights in public.
person_rows.notes: Ethan was a model student who owed his college scholarship to victim. Accused based on being last known person with victim and having her photo (given to him by Marcus Cole). Ran when he saw police at memorial because he felt guilty about calling her a 'whore' during their last interaction. Arrested publicly at memorial service with photo unit and crowds present. Mother had been searching for him. Police used coercive tactics in interrogation ('line up the dogs'). Later cleared when he explained Marcus Cole gave him the photo and his story about the tutoring session argument was verified.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You're lonely, you go to her apartment to get back with her. She lets you in. You gotta have her. But she ain't giving it up. She turns her back on you. You force her into the bedroom.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Byron: 'You said you met Marnie in a mentoring program. Sounds more like you were mentoring her. She came onto me. And you jumped right in. Till she dumped me. Yeah. That's pretty rough... You're lonely, you go to her apartment to get back with her.'
person_rows.notes: Surgeon at Bellevue. Initially lied about relationship with victim (claimed mentoring program, actually met at sex club). Brother identified him as victim's boyfriend. Police questioned him at work (workplace exposure implied). Subject to coercive interrogation tactics suggesting he forced himself on victim. His lawyer intervened. Later cleared when alibi verified - doorman confirmed he came home at 10pm and didn't leave until morning. Had to reveal affair and sex club activities to clear himself, potentially exposing marriage issues.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You saying I did it? You didn't like her. She wasn't gonna graduate me. News flash. You gotta show up to class to graduate. You think I'd kill the bitch over that?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives find Marcus on street corner where Ethan said he hung out. 'Flu better?' referring to his earlier excuse. Accused based on having the photo and motive (not graduating). 'You saying I did it? You didn't like her... You think I'd kill the bitch over that? Man, I'll take any test you want.'
person_rows.notes: 17-year-old student with criminal record (shoplifting, assault, drugs). Ethan identified him as source of victim's nude photo from internet. Had motive (victim wouldn't graduate him) and had spread photo to harass her. Detectives confronted him on street corner in his neighborhood. Willingly agreed to blood test, which presumably cleared him ('that was too easy' suggests test ruled him out). No formal arrest shown but was clearly under suspicion and questioned.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial/plea resolution
episode_summary.summary: Professional hitman Frank Kemp murders bookkeeper Janet Torrens (who was cooperating with FBI against defense contractor Charles Martine) plus two advertising executives (Craig Hovis and Peter Coyle) who witnessed the killing. Lawyer Larry Teasdale served as intermediary between Martine and Kemp. All three are arrested and eventually plead guilty after Kemp agrees to testify. No innocent persons were falsely accused - the investigation correctly identified all perpetrators from the start.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during final testimony.
episode_summary.summary: Lois Ryder is murdered in an apparent mugging. Detectives investigate husband Jonathan Ryder, who tries to frame the murder as the work of a serial mugger. Dr. Joel Friedman (Lois's lover) is initially suspected. Henry Willard, a mugger, is arrested after pawning Lois's jewelry but claims he found it in a dumpster. Evidence suggests Jonathan Ryder killed his wife and planted evidence to frame the mugger. Transcript ends mid-trial during Friedman's testimony about identifying the pin Lois was wearing.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Do you want us to tell you your rights?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Friedman at his apartment with his wife present: 'Who is it, honey?' Wife is present as they question him about affair and demand blood test. 'We'd been seeing each other... about five months.'
person_rows.notes: Friedman is Lois Ryder's lover. Detectives confront him at home in front of his wife about the affair. He cooperates, gives blood test proving he wasn't the father of recent sexual activity, and provides alibi (his wife came home 40 minutes after Lois left, not enough time to commit murder across town). Though cleared as suspect, his affair is exposed to his wife and he faces family consequences. Later reluctantly testifies at trial despite wanting to protect his marriage and reputation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: She's in pretty bad shape. You better hope she can't identify you. / You're a real specimen. Stupid, drugged and violent.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Willard pawned Lois Ryder's jewelry using stolen ID. Arrested violently. Claims: 'I found that stuff... In a dumpster. Penn Station somewhere.' Detectives pressure him: 'She's in pretty bad shape. You better hope she can't identify you.' When asked about missing pin from insurance photos, he insists 'There was no damn pin.' Detective Logan realizes: 'Suppose Mr. Congeniality in there is telling us the truth. He found the expensive necklace and bracelet in the dumpster, but not the $1,000 pin.'
person_rows.notes: Serial mugger with history of stealing jewelry. Pawned Lois Ryder's bracelet and necklace using stolen ID. Violently arrested and accused of her murder. His claim that he found the jewelry in a dumpster is initially dismissed but later appears credible when detectives realize the heirloom pin (which Jonathan Ryder kept) wasn't among the pawned items. Evidence suggests he's a red herring - he found jewelry Ryder planted to frame the serial mugger. While guilty of pawning stolen property and prior muggings, appears innocent of Lois Ryder's murder. Exposure limited to police/legal system (no indication of media coverage), but arrested and pressured.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Jaime Maser, age 15, shoots and kills his friend Robbie Fenwick with his father's .357 Magnum in what he claims is an accidental shooting during a 'trust' game. Investigation reveals a prior sealed juvenile shooting of Graham Campbell two years earlier with identical circumstances. Jaime is tried as an adult, initially acquitted by reason of mental defect (claiming Tracon intoxication), but then convicted of murder in the second degree for the Campbell shooting after the sealed record is opened and his mother testifies he threatened her with a gun saying 'Graham didn't believe me either.' No falsely accused persons identified in this episode.

episode_summary.source_notes: Serial killer case; multiple victims murdered before episode begins; focus on prosecuting Albert Cheney for Judy Bream murder
episode_summary.summary: Detectives investigate the murder of Judy Bream, strangled in her apartment. They connect her death to two prior murders and identify Albert Cheney, a bookkeeper with access to victims' information through doctors' files and a prior conviction for a similar killing. Cheney is arrested and tried. His wife initially provides a false alibi but later testifies against him after being charged as an accessory. No innocent persons are publicly accused or harmed by false accusations in this episode - the investigation correctly identifies the perpetrator from the start.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: A teenage girl at a Catholic rehab facility accuses a nun of sexual molestation. Investigation reveals the nun was innocent and the girl fabricated the story to discredit the nun. The real perpetrator was the male Executive Director who had been sexually exploiting vulnerable teenage girls at the facility. Sister Bettina was publicly accused, investigated, and arrested before being cleared when the truth emerged.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detectives treated her professionally during investigation and allowed voluntary surrender
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Maria admits fabrication: 'I just wanted them to get rid of her... Mr. Powell... me and him. She knew. She asked me about it. He'd think I told.' Stone files motion to dismiss after Maria recants and admits she lied to discredit Sister Bettina.
person_rows.notes: Sister Bettina was falsely accused by Maria Barragan who fabricated sexual molestation claims to discredit the nun. Maria feared Sister Bettina suspected her sexual relationship with Powell and wanted to eliminate her as a threat. The accusation led to formal arrest and prosecution before the truth emerged. Sister Bettina had actually protected the accuser by hiding evidence of drinking to prevent her expulsion. She was interrogated, took polygraph tests, was arrested (though allowed to surrender), and faced formal criminal charges. Her reputation in the religious community and at Haven House was severely damaged. Though cleared when Maria admitted the fabrication, there is no indication of formal apology from police or prosecution.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through the sting operation setup at end
episode_summary.summary: A baby (Henry Ward) freezes to death in an unheated apartment. His mother Jackie Ward is initially accused and arrested, but investigation reveals the building superintendent (Tirado) and enforcer (Turner) intentionally shut off heat to harass tenants on behalf of landlord Iris Corman. Jackie is cleared; Turner is convicted but gets mistrial; prosecutors pursue Corman. Jackie Ward was publicly accused, arrested, and held at Central Booking before being cleared when the true perpetrators were identified.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'She's responsible for that baby's death. Open and shut.' Logan to Jackie: 'In other words, you sat there and watched him freeze to death.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cerreta: 'Meantime she stays at Central Booking.' Jackie's lawyer: 'She's a good mother. She loves Henry.' Investigation shifts when they discover: 'The Department of Housing... the heat was turned off intentionally' and 'Iris Corman... Scum Landlord of the Year, three years running.'
person_rows.notes: Jackie Ward was arrested and held at Central Booking for her baby's death. Detectives initially believed she negligently caused Henry's death by leaving him alone in a cold apartment. Investigation proved the landlord's agents (Turner and Tirado) deliberately shut off heat to harass tenants, making Jackie a victim rather than perpetrator. Her sister Charlayne was present during questioning. No formal apology shown despite complete exoneration when true perpetrators identified.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through closing arguments
episode_summary.summary: Ezra Shore, a Jewish diamond dealer, is beaten and burned. Initial investigation focuses on four Black teenagers (Fontaine Robinson, Cyrus Tanner, Harley Monks, and Reginald Beggs) based on witness statements and Reggie's claim he saw his friends commit the murder. The teens are arrested and indicted, but Reggie recants on the stand, admitting he lied for 'juice' (street credibility). The case is withdrawn from the grand jury. Later investigation reveals Ezra's brother Isaac was laundering money for Joseph Tashjian, who actually killed Ezra when he threatened to expose the operation. Three innocent teens were publicly arrested, perp-walked, and indicted for a murder they didn't commit.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Officer: 'Turn around! Fontaine Robinson, Cyrus Tanner, Harley Monks, you're under arrest for the murder of Ezra Shore.' [...] Stone later withdraws the case from grand jury after Reggie recants: 'A witness changes his story on the stand, I had to withdraw the murder case from the grand jury.'
person_rows.notes: Arrested with dramatic perp walk scene. Reggie Beggs falsely accused him and others, claiming they beat and burned Ezra Shore. Reggie later admits he lied for street credibility. Actual killer was Joseph Tashjian. Charges eventually dropped but only after arrest, arraignment, and grand jury indictment.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Officer: 'Turn around! Fontaine Robinson, Cyrus Tanner, Harley Monks, you're under arrest for the murder of Ezra Shore.' Reggie testifies: 'Cyrus threw garbage on him. Fontaine lit him on fire.' Later recants: 'That man was already dead when I got there.'
person_rows.notes: Same circumstances as Fontaine Robinson. Falsely accused by Reggie Beggs of participating in murder (specifically throwing garbage on victim). Arrested, indicted, charges later dropped after witness recantation.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Officer: 'Turn around! Fontaine Robinson, Cyrus Tanner, Harley Monks, you're under arrest for the murder of Ezra Shore.' Defense attorney Langstrom: 'The blood under the victim's fingernail matches Harley Monks'. It also matches me and a couple million others in Manhattan.'

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'It proves you were in the alley at the time of the murder.' Cerreta: 'We have two people who swear you were bragging about it.' Logan: 'Yeah, it proves you stepped in something, Reg. And it stinks real bad and it's not going away.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Reggie: 'You hassling me and it's my face that's busted up.' [...] 'Caleb Shore says it was you who did the number on his old man.' Reggie: 'Oh, so I must be lying then.' [...] Caleb testifies: 'The son of a bitch said he liked burning him. He said I was next.'
person_rows.notes: Initially accused of murder based on his presence in alley (shoe print match) and street bragging. Caleb Shore violently assaulted him, breaking his nose/face. Police used coercive tactics, false evidence claims about shoe prints to pressure confession. He falsely confessed to witnessing murder for 'juice' (street credibility). Later recanted, admitting he only saw the aftermath. While he may have witnessed post-burning scene, he's innocent of the murder itself and was physically attacked based on false accusation.

episode_summary.summary: Tim Chong, a 17-year-old star physics student, is murdered. Police initially pursue gang-related theories due to his former White Tigers affiliation. Ron Borland is first arrested when his gun is matched to the murder weapon, but is later cleared when detectives discover his wife Marian and son Randy were the actual killers, motivated by academic competition between Tim and Carl Borland for a prestigious science prize. Ron Borland appears to have planted evidence on himself to protect his family.

person_rows.person_label: Ron Borland (fish market owner, Carl's father)
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Ronald Borland, you're under arrest for the murder of Timothy Chong. / Later: The People's motion charges against the previous defendant were dismissed. The People regret their error.
person_rows.notes: Ron Borland was arrested when his fingerprints were found on the murder weapon. He appears to have deliberately placed his prints on the gun to protect his wife Marian and son Randy, who were the actual killers. The case was dismissed against him and Marian and Randy were subsequently charged and convicted. His arrest was public and he endured the exposure of being charged with murder before being cleared.

episode_summary.summary: Councilman James Vogel is murdered after being blackmailed over his sexual orientation. The investigation reveals Harold Dwyer (a prisoner) and attorney Peter Colson ran a blackmail scheme targeting multiple gay men through prison pen-pal correspondence. While other victims (George Harris, Jay Lingard) were blackmailed, none appear to have suffered public exposure or material harm beyond paying the blackmail. The case centers on the actual perpetrators (Dwyer/Colson) rather than falsely accused parties. Edward Vogel (the victim's father) knew about the blackmail and paid it to keep his son's sexuality secret, but this doesn't qualify him as falsely accused of a crime.

episode_summary.summary: Marshall McFadden, a wealthy takeover specialist, is murdered. Simon Vilanis, a terminally ill former factory worker who lost his job, health insurance, and pension after McFadden's takeover, becomes the primary suspect when his stolen gun is identified as the murder weapon. He is arrested and charged. Investigation reveals the real killers were union enforcer Joey Palmieri (hired by his cousin Eddie Palmieri) at the direction of banker Dwight Corcoran, who paid $100,000 through McFadden's charity foundation to prevent McFadden from testifying about bank fraud. Vilanis is cleared.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cerreta: 'What do you say we go down to the station? Maybe somebody turned it in. Maybe you get lucky.' ... Judge: 'I'm going to enter a not guilty plea on the defendant's behalf.' ... Stone to Vilanis: 'But you didn't kill McFadden.' Vilanis: 'Nope. But I'd make a hell of a scapegoat. Me and my big mouth.'
person_rows.notes: Vilanis was arrested and indicted for murder based on his gun being the murder weapon and his public threats against McFadden. He is terminally ill with lung cancer from factory exposure. His gun was stolen by Joey Palmieri specifically to frame him. The real killer (Joey Palmieri) confesses in court, exonerating Vilanis. He insisted on representing himself pro se to 'have his day in court' to expose what McFadden did to workers. No apology is shown in transcript but charges are implicitly dropped when the real killer confesses.

episode_summary.summary: Photographer Julian Decker is murdered. Angela Brant, a model who worked as a prostitute through Decker, is accused and prosecuted for murder. Evidence includes her DNA matching vaginal secretions in victim's pajamas, her prints on murder weapon, lies about alibi. She nearly pleads guilty to protect her daughter. Eventually revealed that her 15-year-old daughter Tracy killed Decker after sexual relationship, and Angela attempted to take the blame. Angela is cleared and charges dismissed when father's testimony establishes her alibi.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, indicted, tried, perp_walk, media_exposure, daughter_learns_prostitution, reputation_destroyed, nearly_convicted
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone: 'We're dismissing on Mrs. Brant and arresting her daughter.' Brant arrested, bail set at $150,000. Green: 'I'll tell the jury she's on trial because prostitutes are easy targets. She was used by her johns, abused by her pimp, and desperate to support her daughter.' Brant willing to plead guilty: 'I've ruined my life, I don't want to ruin Tracy's, too.'
person_rows.notes: Angela is innocent of murder but guilty of prostitution. Her daughter Tracy killed Decker. Angela's prints were on scissors from finding body that morning during legitimate visit. DNA match was 80% (mother-daughter share DNA). Angela deliberately tried to appear guilty to protect her 15-year-old daughter, even willing to plead guilty to prison time. Husband initially told truth about alibi, then lied on stand per Angela's instructions. Prosecution nearly convicted innocent woman. Daughter was victim's sexual abuse victim (statutory rape) who became killer. Angela's exposure as prostitute to her daughter was direct consequence of murder investigation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Complex conspiracy case involving assassination of activist Marcus Tate. Multiple suspects accused and cleared.
episode_summary.summary: Marcus Tate, leader of African-American Congress, is assassinated. Mitchell Koblin arrested based on eyewitness testimony and gun possession. Trial reveals weak ballistics evidence and racial tensions. Koblin acquitted on murder but convicted on weapons charge. Post-trial investigation suggests possible conspiracy involving AAC insiders Roland Books and Otis Cook, but insufficient evidence for prosecution.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, perp_walk, indicted, public_trial, held_without_bail, acquitted_after_trial
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Jury verdict: 'On the last count: murder in the second degree. How do you find? Not guilty.' Also 'Not guilty' on attempted murder and one weapons charge. Convicted only on weapons possession third degree.
person_rows.notes: Koblin was accused of murdering Marcus Tate based on eyewitness testimony from Otis Cook and others identifying him as white male with gun. Ballistics could not match his gun to Tate's fatal wound, only to bodyguard Harlans (whom Koblin shot in self-defense). Jury acquitted on murder and attempted murder, convicting only on weapons possession. Post-trial investigation suggests actual shooter may have been AAC insiders. Koblin suffered arrest without bail, public trial, media exposure, but was ultimately vindicated on the murder charge.

Detectives initially investigate her father Curtis Milgram due to a violent past and insurance policy, but he has an alibi (speeding ticket 80 miles away). Tommy Beltran confesses and is prosecuted for murder two. Defense argues diminished capacity/crime of passion. episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom scene. Focus is on corporate liability for defective pacemakers causing deaths.
episode_summary.summary: Roberto Martinez, 18, dies when his pacemaker fails. Investigation reveals the device had corroded leads from a defective shipment. Initially Dr. Trevor Hellems is accused of unnecessary surgery and implanting a used pacemaker. Later, Jeffrey Suiter (who recycled/resold pacemakers with altered expiration dates) is arrested for murder. Both are eventually cleared or charges dismissed when evidence shows the real culprit was BioNorm Industries knowingly using defective components. The corporation used Steven Cleary (low-level technician) as a shield from liability.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Mrs. Martinez: 'Roberto said that when he was a rich doctor, he'd pay for Ana to go to Juilliard.' Fischel to ADA: 'Roberto didn't need a pacemaker... The thing craps out, and he's dead. That's assault and felony murder.' Hellems: 'And I guess I'll spend the next six years in court proving it.'
person_rows.notes: Hellems was accused by victim's family and lawyer Fischel of performing unnecessary surgery (could have used drugs instead) and killing Roberto through the failed pacemaker. He faces multimillion-dollar lawsuits. Later proven innocent when investigation shows the pacemaker failure was due to defective leads from BioNorm, not his surgical decision. He did implant a used pacemaker but tested it and believed it safe. The real cause was manufacturing defect unrelated to recycling.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Greevey: 'I know that three plus three equals murder two.' [Arrest scene] 'Jeffrey Suiter, you're under arrest for the murder of Roberto Martinez.' [Court] Judge: 'I'm dismissing with respect to the murder charges.' Stone: 'You can't fight with the FDA.'
person_rows.notes: Suiter was arrested and indicted for murder two after detectives discovered he altered expiration dates on recycled pacemakers. Bail set at $100,000. However, FDA expert testimony proved the pacemaker didn't fail due to battery expiration (Suiter's fraud) but due to corroded leads (manufacturing defect). Judge dismissed murder charges, acknowledging Suiter's fraud was not the cause-in-fact of death. Stone acknowledged: 'You can't fight with the FDA.' Suiter faced fraud charges instead. His arrest and indictment were public, causing reputational and business harm before exoneration on murder.
