episode_summary.summary: Dr. Alex Merritt is accused of sexually assaulting multiple female patients during gynecological examinations. The episode follows the prosecution of Merritt for raping Dr. Elizabeth Olivet, a police psychologist who deliberately returned to his office to gather evidence after hearing complaints from her patient Diane Perkins. Though initially convicted by a jury, the judge dismisses the verdict claiming entrapment. Merritt is later re-arrested after his nurse testifies and additional victims come forward. No false suspects are identified in this episode—the focus is entirely on prosecuting the actual perpetrator.

episode_summary.summary: Manuel Ortega and his wife are murdered at a restaurant. Investigation reveals Manuel worked as a travel agent booking flights for Colombian drug cartel members and hit men. Evidence suggests Manuel killed dealer Felix Arias (who harassed his wife), and the cartel sent hit man Javier Gaitan as retaliation. Gaitan is arrested, tried, and convicted of both murders. At his sentencing, victim's father Juan Christobal shoots Gaitan dead in the courtroom. Initially presented as grieving father, Christobal is later revealed to be a cartel operative who killed Gaitan to prevent him from cooperating with authorities. No innocent persons are publicly accused or harmed by false accusations in this episode - the investigation correctly identifies Gaitan as the restaurant shooter, and all other suspects (Manuel, Ramon) are actually guilty of their accused crimes.

Mary Kostrinski claims self-defense against attempted rape, but investigation reveals she was paid $30,000 by mob boss Jimmy Scanlon to kill Duff, who had been stealing from Scanlon. He's scared, Ben. He'll say whatever you want him to. ... These people are born gonifs, it's in their blood. ... He gladly gives you the only white man he knows. Defense argues Fabian (scared janitor) blamed 'only white man he knows.' Case initially weak - but Marietta LATER becomes cooperating witness and admits full guilt in smuggling operation. Including because: (1) initial accusation based on potentially coerced witness was questionable, (2) public workplace arrest caused immediate harm/exposure, (3) shows how prosecution initially relied on weak evidence before getting confirmation. However, since he ultimately WAS guilty and became cooperating witness, this is borderline for inclusion.

episode_summary.summary: Seven-year-old Samantha Silver disappears from a department store. Investigation initially focuses on Harold Zorn, a stock clerk with a prior sex abuse conviction, before discovering the girl was taken by her mother's allies who claim the father molested her. Gary Silver is accused of sexually abusing his daughter, publicly tried, and acquitted. The case involves coached testimony, disputed medical evidence, and a mother who wanted to flee the country. A former stock clerk with a sex abuse record is also publicly accused before being cleared.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Logan: 'You still wanted her, didn't you, Zorn?' Zorn: 'Harold, what was Samantha like when you talked to her?' [...] 'I asked her if she liked the giraffe. She pointed to the stuffed gorilla and said a woman at the register was buying it for her. That's all. I left.' Later: 'His alibi. It checks. They know him at the coffee shop. He was there when they were shutting down the store.'
person_rows.notes: Stock clerk at Spencer's with prior sex abuse conviction (pled down misdemeanor, no time served). Detectives confront him at his second job (chocolate factory) in front of coworkers about missing child. He explains he spoke to Samantha briefly then left for coffee break. Alibi confirmed by coffee shop witnesses. Despite clearance, he was publicly questioned at workplace about child abduction due to his record, causing exposure and likely job/reputation consequences.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Verdict: 'We find the defendant not guilty.' Stone earlier: 'She's talked to too many people. She can't remember what happened. Olivet prepped her six times. Six different answers.' Samantha on stand: 'I don't know what I'm supposed to say. I forgot.' Defense reveals: 'Your client and Mrs. Stark had other agendas. They've so muddied the water I don't know what a jury'll believe.' Mother wanted to 'leave the country... needed full custody.'
person_rows.notes: Broadway producer accused of molesting his 7-year-old daughter. Wife wanted full custody to move to France with boyfriend. Child's testimony completely contradictory across multiple interviews. Medical evidence disputed. Ramona Stark (psychologist witness) has history of appearing in 22 cases where 13 defendants were acquitted despite her testimony they were 'all guilty.' Wife's lawyer characterized as zealot. Jury acquits. Episode strongly implies coached/false accusation orchestrated by mother and activist psychologist to gain custody. Public figure subjected to criminal trial, massive media exposure, career destruction despite acquittal.

episode_summary.summary: Barbara Spiegelman, 61, is murdered. Her boyfriend Stephen Gregg is arrested, fingerprints found on knives in drawer. He pleads guilty to manslaughter (7-21 years) via plea deal with attorney Sally Knight. Post-plea, prosecutors discover Gregg's allocution contradicts forensics (he said victim was seated; ME says standing). Investigation reveals Kevin Doyle, Barbara's estate attorney, had stronger motive ($500K executor commission, $400K IRS debt). Doyle's fingerprints found at scene, his alibi false. Gregg's charges withdrawn; Doyle arrested and tried. Transcript ends mid-cross-examination, suggesting potential conspiracy between Doyle and Knight to frame/railroad Gregg. Gregg accepted plea despite innocence, likely coerced by incompetent/unethical counsel.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, indicted, perp_walk, jailed_pretrial, pleaded_guilty_under_coercion, accepted_7-21_year_sentence
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'the law says if you kill somebody, you can't collect under their will. So Carole and her husband will be spending that $2 million.' Briscoe: '$2 million would make my smile a lot bigger.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone/Robinette discover allocution inconsistency: 'You don't forget how you killed someone.' They withdraw charges after discovering Doyle's prints and motive. Robinette: 'Did you ever consider that Stephen Gregg took the deal for the wrong reason? He always maintained he was innocent.'
person_rows.notes: Gregg was Barbara's boyfriend (age gap relationship, she was 61, he was 38). Despite maintaining innocence throughout, he accepted man-1 plea (7-21 years) after his attorney Sally Knight told him prosecution's case was strong. Knight appears to have been referred by the actual killer (Doyle) and may have conspired to ensure Gregg took a plea. Gregg's allocution contradicted forensics (said victim seated; ME said standing), triggering post-conviction investigation. Charges withdrawn when Doyle's fingerprints found at scene and his $500K motive/$400K IRS debt discovered. Gregg suffered arrest, pretrial detention, public court proceedings, and accepted a guilty plea to a crime he didn't commit before exoneration.

He ultimately pleads guilty to Murder Two to protect his daughter from perjury charges and to seal the trial record. episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows police initially suspect Jonas Stark (nicknamed 'Skate') of murdering Dawn Bryan, a drug addict. Evidence (gun prints, motive) points to Stark. However, investigation reveals Dawn's mother Virginia Bryan actually killed her daughter (mercy killing at Dawn's request). Father Fred Bryan planted the murder weapon to frame Stark.
episode_summary.summary: Jonas Stark was falsely accused of murdering Dawn Bryan. He was arrested twice, subjected to interrogation, had a line-up conducted, and faced $500,000 bail for murder charges. His prints were on the gun clip because he had given the gun to Dawn for protection. The actual killer was Dawn's mother Virginia Bryan (mercy killing). Dawn's father Fred Bryan planted evidence in a sewer pipe to frame Stark. Grand jury ultimately indicted Virginia Bryan only for weapons possession (misdemeanor), not manslaughter.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan to Stark during first arrest: 'You hit Dawn's table while you were slapping her around, and then you put a bullet in her chest.' Briscoe: 'Your prints, genius. You left them on the gun clip.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone to Fred Bryan: 'I'm truly sorry for what happened to your family. But you want us to bend the law.' Context: After discovering Virginia Bryan was the actual killer and Fred planted evidence to frame Stark. Robinette: 'Fred Bryan planted the gun so the cops would find it.' Stone: 'These people are being canonized for their suffering. You're telling me this man killed his daughter [and framed Stark].'
person_rows.notes: Stark was arrested twice for Dawn Bryan's murder. First arrest had insufficient evidence (witness couldn't ID him, no weapon). Second arrest after his fingerprints found on gun clip retrieved from sewer pipe. Bail set at $500,000. He refused to plead guilty despite facing 25-to-life, insisting on his innocence and that he had given the gun to Dawn for protection. This turned out to be true. Dawn's father Fred Bryan planted the gun in the sewer pipe after reading in newspapers it was 'Stark's gun.' Virginia Bryan confessed to mercy-killing her daughter at Dawn's request. Stark faced public courtroom arraignment and was held on serious bail for a crime he didn't commit. Case was built around fabricated evidence (planted gun) combined with legitimate but misleading evidence (his prints on clip from when he gave her the gun).

episode_summary.summary: Lt. Janet Hagen is found dead in a hotel room after a Navy party. Ensign Walters confesses to accidentally killing her during a drunken encounter and is convicted by military court-martial. Detectives discover forensic evidence proving Hagen's fatal injury occurred 20+ minutes before Walters touched her. They identify Captain Bunker as the actual killer—Bunker had a history with Hagen from the Naval Academy where she filed a harassment complaint that derailed his career. Walters is proven innocent; Bunker is arrested and tried for murder.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: WALTERS: 'I was drunk. Everybody was. I saw her come out of a room. She nearly fell flat on her face. Then she walked the gauntlet. We went into a room. I put her on a bed. I was helping her off with her clothes and she started to vomit. Then she stopped moving. She could have hit her head. I don't know. I don't remember. Sir, I panicked. I don't know what exactly happened, but she was dead.' STONE in court: 'You thought she was drunk. She certainly appeared friendly, so you carried her into a bedroom.' WALTERS: 'Yes.' STONE: 'As a matter of fact, you confessed to committing that crime. Isn't that true?' WALTERS: 'Yes.' STONE: 'Do you have any recollection of ever striking Lt. Hagen?' WALTERS: 'No.' STONE: 'In the pre-trial inquiry conducted by the Navy, was there any evidence introduced to prove that it was you that struck her?' WALTERS: 'No.' STONE: 'In other words, you only thought that you had accidentally killed her.' WALTERS: 'That's right.'
person_rows.notes: Walters confessed under panic and belief he caused her death, but forensic evidence proved the fatal blow occurred 20-30 minutes before he encountered Hagen. The Navy court-martialed him for involuntary manslaughter based solely on his confused confession. He was convicted and sent to Leavenworth military prison. Stone later proved his innocence by showing the actual killer was Captain Bunker. Walters was cleared through the civilian trial that proved the timeline made his guilt impossible.

episode_summary.summary: Dr. Fay Walsh, a research scientist, is murdered in her university lab. Initially, animal rights activist Dirk Chesney is suspected due to threats and past radical actions. Later, evidence points to Professor Donald Walsh (victim's husband) and administrator Susan Boyd in a conspiracy. Walsh is tried and acquitted after Boyd's testimony falls apart on the stand. Eventually revealed that Boyd suffers from erotomania - a delusional belief she was having an affair with Walsh - and she alone murdered Fay Walsh to remove the 'obstacle' to her fantasy relationship.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Scene shows Chesney arrested fleeing his apartment with a shotgun. Detectives later: 'If you wanna keep him, you better find a different gun. Fay Walsh was shot with a .410.' Gun doesn't match. His acupuncturist alibi confirmed: 'Dr. Chen confirmed he had 25 needles in his can Sunday night.'
person_rows.notes: Animal rights activist with history of laboratory bombings. Had motive and painted walls at crime scene but wrong gun caliber and solid alibi. Released after gun and alibi verified. No indication arrest became public beyond police interrogation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detective to Walsh: 'What comes around goes around. And what goes around for murder is life in a little cage. But no one comes to let you out.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Walsh arrested and tried for conspiracy to murder his wife based on Susan Boyd's fabricated affair claims and her purchases of ammunition. Jury verdict: 'We find the defendant not guilty.' Later revealed Boyd suffered from erotomania and invented the entire relationship. Walsh consistently denied affair: 'I have never and will never have anything to do with Susan Boyd... You're a lunatic.'
person_rows.notes: Victim's husband accused of conspiracy to murder his wife based entirely on Susan Boyd's delusional belief they were having an affair. She purchased ammunition using fake ID, left incriminating voicemails to his answering machine, and testified against him. Public trial, arrest, indictment. Acquitted after her testimony collapsed. She later proven to have erotomania - invented entire affair. Walsh suffered arrest, public trial, loss of wife, false accusation while grieving. No police or prosecutorial apology despite complete fabrication.

episode_summary.summary: A computer virus inserted into Hogan-Hayes Clinic's blood analyzer causes insulin overdoses, killing 2 patients and putting 3+ in comas. Evidence against John Cook excluded due to illegal search; prosecutors pivot to charging father Robert Cook with conspiracy, hoping son will confess to save father.

Sifford said he got there at 12:30. That leaves you 30 minutes.' [...] Rodriguez breaks down: 'Okay, okay, 15 minutes! You satisfied?' Then clinic calls: 'Three more patients just went into hypoglycemic coma, just like the others.' Detective: 'Unless this guy is using mind control to kill people, I suggest you make your apologies in triplicate.'

Accused based on being alone with patients for disputed time period. Detectives apply aggressive interrogation tactics. Cleared when new victims appear while he wasn't on duty. Uh, from midnight to 6:00 a.m., Moe and Larry were poppin' eardrums at a club called Dreck. 150 witnesses.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Slugs in the vic's skull match the Beretta. His are the only prints on it. If I was gonna kill a guy, you think I'd do it for a Bonneville? / I think you'd do it for a shoelace. Your buddy's prints are on the gun. My guess is right now he's in the other room rollin' on you.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Their alibi sings, even if they can't. Uh, from midnight to 6:00 a.m., Moe and Larry were poppin' eardrums at a club called Dreck. 150 witnesses.
person_rows.notes: Arrested alongside Wayne Jeffers for murder after they stripped the victim's car. Cleared by same airtight alibi performing at club. Name not given in transcript but referenced as bass player and 'Moe' to Jeffers' 'Larry'.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: I don't know if they give out gold stars for loyalty in your country, but over here, all you get is a set of striped pajamas and the word 'schmo' stamped on your forehead. The world's not as big as you think it is, Tommy. We're gonna find your partner. The question you have to ask yourself, will he be this loyal to you?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: We know there were two. Just tell us who the other person was. Maybe we can work something out here. / I don't know if they give out gold stars for loyalty in your country, but over here, all you get is a set of striped pajamas and the word 'schmo' stamped on your forehead. [...] I thought we were just going to get the cards back. I never thought he'd kill his own brother.
person_rows.notes: Arrested for credit card fraud and murder of Nicolas Iliescu. Actually guilty of fraud but innocent of murder - his co-conspirator Alex Iliescu committed the murder. Police questioned him at workplace (furniture store) and used coercive tactics to pressure confession. Eventually identified Alex as the actual killer. Served as witness against Alex later.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Alex Iliescu, you're under arrest for the murder of Nicolas Iliescu. [...] I'm declaring the truck and all of the other evidence found in the garage inadmissible. [...] The case against Mr. Iliescu is dismissed.
person_rows.notes: ACTUALLY GUILTY - murdered his brother Nicolas. Arrested and charged but case dismissed due to evidence suppression (illegal search). Later murdered by his son Leon. Included for context only - NOT counted as falsely accused since he actually committed the crime.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode centers on Officer Rick Newhouse, a gay cop shot and killed during a drug bust when backup deliberately delayed responding.
episode_summary.summary: Officer Rick Newhouse, a closeted gay police officer, is killed during a confrontation with drug dealers when fellow officers deliberately delayed backup due to anti-gay bias. The prosecution charges three officers with murder two but all are acquitted despite evidence of intentional delay. A dealer (Lucho Martinez) was initially arrested for the murder but the case reveals the real cause was police homophobia. Officer Craig McGraw, also gay and Newhouse's partner, feared for his own safety.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You killed a cop, you dumb son of a bitch!
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Martinez arrested based on fingerprints on drug baggie at scene. He states: 'I didn't shoot him. Romero hit him in the leg. He was like an animal, man. He ran down, shot him again. He thought the cop was dead. Cop shot him.' Case shows Romero (the dead dealer) killed Newhouse with .38, while Martinez had .22. Martinez makes deal: 'Felony murder, 25-to-life. Drugs, 5-to-15. He didn't kill the cop. He had a .22. The cop was hit with a .38.'
person_rows.notes: Martinez was present at scene and participated in drug transaction, but did not kill Officer Newhouse. The .22 shells found were his, but Newhouse was killed by .38 from Romero (the dealer who died). Martinez made a deal to plead to murder two for 25 years, accepting responsibility despite not firing the fatal shot. His testimony about seeing two cops in a car nearby helped expose the real cause of Newhouse's death.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Kathy McKenna, a deaf university student, is murdered. Initially, her ex-boyfriend Paul Crandall is accused, arrested, and publicly charged based on threatening phone transcripts. After linguistic analysis reveals a hearing person wrote the threats, investigation shifts to Kathy's mentor Dr. Gordon Bryce, who killed her when she planned to leave for California. Crandall was publicly arrested and charged before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'Which lie do you want to start with? The one about the card game?' Briscoe: 'Hey, I want to believe you, Paul, I really do. But unless you can give us some answers, I have to assume the worst.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Rollins (defense attorney): 'In all my years of representing the disadvantaged I have never seen such a spectacle. As if Paul's deafness wasn't enough of a handicap, you hobble him with a public arrest.' Later: Stone tells Robinette to 'tell Crandall's attorney we're dismissing the charges against his client.'
person_rows.notes: Paul Crandall was arrested and arraigned for Kathy McKenna's murder based on threatening TDD transcripts. His attorney explicitly states this was a 'public arrest' that humiliated him. The charges were later dismissed when linguistic analysis proved the threats were written by a hearing person (Dr. Bryce), not Crandall who is deaf. His employer and the deaf community were aware of the accusation. The defense attorney's statement about the 'spectacle' of the arrest confirms significant public exposure and reputational harm, though we don't see specific consequences like job loss on-screen.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony. Rick Mason trial still in progress at end.
episode_summary.summary: Rick Mason, host of tabloid TV show 'Final Confession,' arranges on-air confrontation between child molester Dr. Joseph Vinton and victim Scott Fisher. Scott's estranged father Sid Fisher shows up and shoots Vinton dead on live TV. Investigation reveals Mason manipulated Scott into calling his father with the location, anticipating violent confrontation for ratings. Mason is prosecuted for manslaughter and facilitation. No innocent persons are falsely accused in this episode - Sid Fisher committed the murder he's charged with, and Rick Mason is the actual target of the prosecution who allegedly facilitated it.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end during defense closing argument.
episode_summary.summary: Roland Kirk, a homeless man with mental illness and crack addiction, is beaten nearly to death with rebar in an alley. Initial suspects include George Siddell (red hat homeless man) and Dr. Creighton (dentist who administered CPR). Investigation reveals Harold Morrissey beat Kirk in retaliation for mugging his wife earlier that evening. Both Siddell and Creighton were innocent of the attack but were publicly suspected/investigated.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You ever hassle with him? ... Now we're gonna want to talk to him later maybe. / (At station) People are gonna say you clobbered Kirk and took his card. / But we're gonna have to keep you here, until we can ask him.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: KELLEY: Come on. Hey, I'm walking. I'm cooperating. / LOGAN: You wanted a guy in a red hat? Meet George Siddell. ... (Later at station) BRISCOE: People are gonna say you clobbered Kirk and took his card. SIDDELL: No, I didn't. He gave it to me, a long time ago. LOGAN: Yeah. I'm sure he did. But we're gonna have to keep you here, until we can ask him.
person_rows.notes: Siddell was brought in because he wore a red hat (matching witness description) and had Kirk's Medicaid card. He was questioned and detained briefly but released when he explained he'd used the card to buy pills he then sold for wine. No public exposure beyond police interaction. Excluded from final count as harm was minimal (severity 1, police_only exposure).

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Search warrant execution at his home with wife present. Lawyer called. Blood found on slippers. Arrested and arraigned: 'Docket number 63181. People v. Steven R. Creighton. The charges are attempted murder in the second degree, assault in the first degree.' Bail set at $150,000. Defense attorney: 'The People's case is predicated on the testimony of an insane person.' Later dropped when Prosky identified Morrissey as attacker.
person_rows.notes: Creighton was innocent of the attack but guilty of failure to report (he administered CPR then left without waiting for paramedics or reporting, fearing lawsuit). He was arrested for attempted murder based on elderly witness (Mrs. Bundy) seeing him in bathrobe in alley, Kirk identifying 'bald woman' (Creighton is bald, wore bathrobe), and blood on his slippers. Search warrant executed at his home in front of wife. Formally charged and arraigned with $150,000 bail. Charges eventually dropped when Prosky identified Morrissey. Public exposure included: home search (family present), arraignment (public court record), and his lawyer had to defend him. Clear material harm from false murder charge.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: STONE: We could be looking at a conspiracy charge here. ... He acted as a lookout for Harold Morrissey. That's enough to make him an accomplice.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: STONE: Mr. Prosky, we could be looking at a conspiracy charge here. SIRKIN (lawyer): Based on what? Answering the phone? STONE: He acted as a lookout for Harold Morrissey. That's enough to make him an accomplice. PROSKY: But I didn't do anything. SIRKIN: Leon, please. STONE: I can see a light touch doesn't come naturally to you. What are you offering?
person_rows.notes: Prosky was threatened with conspiracy/accomplice charges for allegedly acting as lookout. He initially lied to police about calling paramedics and his role. He was NOT innocent of wrongdoing (he witnessed the attack, lied initially, and may have facilitated it by telling Morrissey Kirk's location). However, he was innocent of the PRIMARY accusation (conspiracy to commit attempted murder). The harm was the legal threat and having to come in with lawyer. Marked partially_involved because he did lie and may have enabled the attack, but the conspiracy charge itself appears to have been leverage. Borderline for inclusion - the actual wrong (lying to police, not helping) is different from the threatened charge (conspiracy).

episode_summary.summary: Julia Wood, a 19-year-old college student, accuses rock star C-Square (Clarence Carmichael) of rape after going to his hotel suite. The case proceeds to trial where C-Square is convicted of rape in the first degree. No innocent persons were publicly accused or harmed by false accusations in this episode. The defendant was found guilty of the crime he was accused of committing.

episode_summary.summary: Serial killer Arthur Tunney shoots four people (three fatally) with a sawed-off shotgun in racially-motivated attacks after his mother was killed by black teenagers. Police develop a profile with FBI help and arrest Tunney based on eyewitness voice ID, weapon match, and white supremacist literature. Defense attorney Horace McCoy challenges the evidence. No innocent persons are publicly accused; the investigation correctly identifies the perpetrator. Tunney is granted bail despite prosecution objections.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated during final arguments in appeal hearing
episode_summary.summary: Jonathan Keyes dies in his penthouse. After investigation, his wife Danielle is arrested and convicted of murdering him via insulin overdose. The episode focuses on the investigation and trial, with closing scenes showing the beginning of an appeal. No innocent persons are falsely accused or publicly harmed - the investigation correctly identifies the killer (Danielle Keyes). The housekeeper Helga and girlfriend Cathy Rogers provide testimony but are never accused themselves. Lance Keyes (the son) hires a PI to find evidence against his stepmother, but is not himself accused. Transcript ends mid-appeal argument, but conviction stands.

episode_summary.summary: Frank McKinnon, a building superintendent, is found dead with head trauma in what initially appears to be a burglary gone wrong. Investigation reveals his son Sean killed him and staged a break-in. The defense claims Frank was abusive and Sean acted in self-defense, but evidence shows Sean was actually the abuser—repeatedly beating his father and controlling the family. Sean's sister Maureen testifies against him about the pattern of abuse, while mother Catherine supports Sean's false narrative. No innocent persons were falsely accused with public exposure or material harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-sentence during Hendricks' testimony about apocalyptic beliefs.
episode_summary.summary: A car bomb in a Wall Street garage kills one person. Detectives initially suspect Assad Asalam, an Egyptian diplomat whose leased Mercedes was used in the bombing. He is questioned but has an alibi with State Department officials and reports the car stolen. The victim is identified as Wendy Berman, a 19-year-old runaway involved with Daniel Hendricks' Acherusian Temple cult. Hendricks is ultimately charged with felony murder based on kidnapping (through brainwashing), but the initial suspect Asalam was publicly accused and cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'Allah calls, I hear you people get antsy.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: FBI Agent Garrett arrives and says: 'His story checks. He was with Kenny, Dukes, and Fried of State all morning.' The detectives learn the car was stolen and Asalam was in meetings during the bombing.
person_rows.notes: While Asalam was brought in for questioning and subjected to an offensive religious comment by Briscoe, there is no evidence of public exposure beyond the police station or material consequences. The FBI immediately confirms his alibi. Excluded from count as exposure was police_only with minimal harm (severity 1, no public exposure, no material sanction).

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial and acquittal
episode_summary.summary: Phillip Swann was convicted 8 years ago of murdering Sidney Cohen based on accomplice Russell Bobbitt's testimony. When Cohen's body is found in a different location than Bobbitt testified, Swann gets a new trial, represents himself, and is acquitted. The episode focuses on Swann's guilt and his manipulation of the system, not on innocent people harmed by false accusations. While some witnesses invested with Swann and lost money, they were not accused of crimes. The only person who might qualify is Russell Bobbitt (the accomplice witness), but his fate is unclear at transcript end and he was likely complicit in some capacity given the evidence. No qualifying innocent persons harmed by false accusations are present in this episode.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through most of trial but cuts off mid-sentence during plea negotiation discussion.
episode_summary.summary: A 13-year-old runaway, Johnny Lasky, is found beaten to death. Investigation reveals he was killed by his 14-year-old foster brother Chris Pollit during a fight after a burglary attempt. Chris is tried as an adult; defense argues XYY chromosome syndrome made him genetically predisposed to violence. No false accusations or wrongly accused persons identified in this episode - the actual perpetrator (Chris) is identified early and remains the defendant throughout.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom proceedings and plea negotiation scene
episode_summary.summary: William Cooper, 56, owner of a meat distribution business, is shot to death in his office. Investigation reveals his wife Irina (26, Russian mail-order bride) was having an affair with Alex Nunez, a lab technician. Evidence shows Nunez killed Cooper after Irina arranged for her husband to be alone at the office. Both are arrested and tried. The case involves a coerced abortion, Cooper's controlling behavior toward Irina, and her desire to bring her son from Russia. No innocent persons were publicly accused or harmed by false accusations - the investigation correctly identified the perpetrators from the start.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-sentence during priest's visit to Stone.
episode_summary.summary: Mildred Bauer, 82, is found dead after an apparent burglary. Initially suspected burglar Maria Gonzales (the live-in maid) and her boyfriend Felix Ortega are cleared when evidence emerges that granddaughter Laura Bauer systematically starved her grandmother to death. Maria becomes a key witness against Laura, who is arrested for murder. The case reveals Maria was wrongly accused of burglary and faced deportation, while Laura is ultimately tried for depraved indifference homicide.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You want to find a maid, talk to other maids... Maybe we better talk this over down at the INS. You do have your green card, don't you?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney cross-examination: 'Miss Gonzales, you and your boyfriend, Felix Ortega, broke into Mrs. Bauer's apartment the day she died, didn't you?' Maria: 'We wanted to see if she was all right.' Defense: 'You were arrested for burglary, weren't you?' Maria: 'Yes.' Defense: 'Mrs. Bauer's silver candlesticks were found in Mr. Ortega's apartment, were they not?' Maria: 'Yes.' ... Maria later testifies she was scheduled to be deported yesterday and prosecution intervened.
person_rows.notes: Maria was Laura Bauer's grandmother's live-in maid who was fired Tuesday for giving extra food to starving grandmother. When she and boyfriend Felix returned Friday to check on grandmother and retrieve medicine, they found her dead. Initially suspected of burglary when silver candlesticks found at boyfriend's apartment. She claims grandmother gave them to her. Arrested for burglary, charges later dropped when prosecution realizes Laura is the actual killer. However, Maria faced deportation proceedings that were only suspended so she could testify against Laura. Laura herself called INS anonymously to get Maria deported, knowing Maria was a witness to the starvation. Cross-examination weaponized her arrest, the candlesticks, and deportation status to undermine credibility as witness.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detectives to Felix: 'You mind if we come in and look around?' Felix: 'You got a warrant? I know the law.' Detective: 'Look, we don't find anything, you're in the clear.' Other detective: 'Yeah, don't do us any favors. Hey, did we forget to mention? Your other girlfriend said hi.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives find $400 cash and package addressed to nephew in Dominican Republic at Felix's apartment. Detective sarcastically: 'Somebody been to the pawn shop since yesterday?' Felix: 'I work the trucks. I save my money.' Then candlesticks are identified by victim's friend. Felix has 'two burglary convictions' per detective discussion.
person_rows.notes: Felix came with Maria on Friday to check on grandmother after Maria was fired. They broke down door when grandmother didn't answer, found her dead, fled. Candlesticks found at his apartment. Has prior burglary record which made him automatic suspect. Police used threat of girlfriend Rosa's immigration status and mentioned his 'other girlfriend' to pressure him. Charges eventually dropped when real killer (Laura) identified. Exposure appears limited to police encounter and search, no indication of public/workplace exposure.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through verdict reading but cuts off mid-sentence at end
episode_summary.summary: Jason Bregman stages his own kidnapping with accomplices Watson and Navarro to extort $4 million from his father Sol. When the scheme goes wrong and Jason is shot, Watson and Navarro claim Jason was the mastermind. No innocent persons are falsely accused - Jason was complicit, and Watson/Navarro are guilty of the crimes charged (larceny, though acquitted of attempted murder). The case involves a spoiled son exploiting his wealthy father, not false accusations against innocent parties.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony but cuts off during final scene with Savitts' lawyer.
episode_summary.summary: Young woman Debra Elkins gives birth and claims her baby was stolen. Investigation reveals she promised the baby to multiple couples for money. Dorothy Baxter, a desperate infertile woman who believed she had a contract for the baby, kidnaps the infant from the hospital. Baxter is arrested but ultimately receives a suspended sentence. The episode focuses on private adoption fraud, with Baxter being the primary falsely accused party who suffers public arrest and prosecution despite being portrayed sympathetically as a victim of Elkins' scheme.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, prosecuted, pled guilty to reduced charge with suspended sentence, psychiatric treatment ordered, reputation damaged
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Dorothy Baxter arrested at her sister's house holding the baby: 'No, this is my baby though. I'm telling you.' She pleads guilty but says in court: 'we did everything that we were supposed to do. We gave her money, we took care of her... I want to know where it says that you need a backyard to love your baby.'
person_rows.notes: Baxter technically committed the crime (took baby from hospital) but the episode frames her sympathetically as a victim of Debra Elkins' fraud. She believed she had a valid contract and had paid substantial money. Stone and psychiatrist Olivet both express sympathy. The 'harm' is her arrest, prosecution, financial ruin ($60k+ spent on multiple failed adoptions), and emotional devastation. Classified as partially_involved because she did take the baby, but the primary accusation (that she's a dangerous kidnapper/criminal) is shown to be unfair - she's portrayed as mentally/emotionally broken by repeated adoption scams.

person_rows.consequence_detail: publicly questioned at home by detectives in front of family, threatened with job loss
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Briscoe: 'Well, there's a new mother with tubes up her nose who might disagree.' Logan: 'We saw your car. Your last fare didn't have a nosebleed.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Walinsky at his apartment holding his own baby. He protests: 'What are you doing with my son?' They accuse him of involvement in missing baby. Walinsky: 'That girl, she was sick. She told me to take her to the hospital. I swear to you, there was no baby.' Dispatcher earlier threatened: 'If he doesn't recover by tomorrow, you tell him, don't bother.'
person_rows.notes: Walinsky is quickly cleared but detectives confront him at home in accusatory manner while he's holding his own infant son. The dispatcher had already threatened his job for calling in sick. Public exposure was moderate (family present, job threatened) but brief.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Logan: 'You always let your girlfriends bleed half to death in a cab?' Briscoe: 'Listen, dumping that kid somewhere was gonna make your life a hell of a lot easier, wasn't it?' Shaw: 'You're wrong, man, all right? Just ask Debbie. You'll see.' Logan: 'If it was me, and I found out she was pregnant... man, I would've grabbed my hat and split.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Shaw arrested in hotel room with baby. Logan reads him Miranda rights. During interrogation, detectives suggest he abandoned the baby to make his life easier. Shaw protests his innocence and asks to talk to Debbie. Later cleared when Debbie confirms she left the baby with him intentionally.
person_rows.notes: Shaw was caring for the baby as requested by Debbie but was treated as kidnapper/accomplice. Arrested and subjected to accusatory interrogation. Detectives used insults and psychological pressure ('if it was me... I would've grabbed my hat and split'). Cleared once Debbie explained the situation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete. Episode focuses on Judge Joel Thayer stalking/threatening Janet Rudman and her child, then falsely accusing ADA Claire Kincaid of misconduct when she investigates.
episode_summary.summary: Judge Joel Thayer stalks Janet Rudman and threatens her child after their affair. When ADA Claire Kincaid investigates, Thayer retaliates by falsely claiming she made sexual advances toward him, resulting in her professional censure. Kincaid is later vindicated when evidence proves Thayer's guilt.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: THAYER: 'Ms. Kincaid was constantly asking about my marriage... She began to kiss my neck. I asked her to stop... She threatened to tell my wife that we were having an affair.' // KINCAID: 'He became very possessive, unreasonable... and when I wanted to break it off, he became threatening.' // 'I understand you've been censured.' Later proven false when Thayer allocutes to stalking crimes.
person_rows.notes: Thayer fabricated sexual misconduct allegations against Kincaid in retaliation for her investigation. She was censured by disciplinary committee (preventing practice of law) based on his false testimony. Later vindicated when Thayer's crimes were proven and he allocuted. The censure was a formal professional sanction with career-ending potential until lifted.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: LOGAN: 'So, Ronny, do you own a bicycle?' BLOCKER: 'Owned a Mongoose. Hey, those are silver, aren't they? Polished aluminum with full suspension. Somebody stole it.' // 'Blocker's got a perfect alibi. Fifty guests put him at his gallery from dawn to dusk on the day of the zoo trip.'
person_rows.notes: Blocker was investigated as initial suspect due to nasty note sent to Janet Rudman after being fired, ownership of silver bicycle matching witness description, and grudge motive. Detectives questioned him at his gallery (public workplace exposure). Cleared by airtight alibi - 50 witnesses placed him at gallery exhibition all day during the zoo incident.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode centers on accidental shooting of Angel Ramirez by Kevin Parker, who was trying to shoot gun dealer Juan Domingo. Multiple false suspects before the truth emerges.
episode_summary.summary: 14-year-old Angel Ramirez is shot and killed on the street. Initially appears random, but investigation reveals Kevin Parker (16) shot at gun dealer Juan Domingo (who had shot Parker's friend Ricky Morales) and accidentally hit Angel. Juan Domingo is falsely accused of Angel's murder before evidence points to Parker. Multiple students are questioned, Billy Wojak is arrested for holding guns in his locker. Case ends in mistrial after key witness Domingo is killed in suspicious circumstances.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives initially suspect Domingo of Angel's murder based on proximity and Mrs. Abel's identification. Later cleared when evidence shows Kevin Parker fired the fatal shot. 'Juan Domingo was there, too. Right next to him. We thought maybe you could tell us why, huh?'
person_rows.notes: Domingo is a gun dealer who actually did shoot Ricky Morales, but was falsely accused of killing Angel Ramirez. He was arrested for Angel's murder but this charge was dropped when Kevin Parker was identified as the shooter. Domingo later becomes prosecution witness but is killed before trial. His arrest for Angel's murder qualifies as false accusation harm, though exposure stayed within police/legal channels.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: 'If we find out this gun was used to commit a crime... you're looking at accessory to murder.' Detective threatens Billy in front of school officials and his mother.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Billy's locker is searched at school in front of Monsignor and security. Four guns found. Detectives threaten: 'If we find out this gun was used to commit a crime... you're looking at accessory to murder.' Billy taken to precinct for fingerprinting in front of school officials. Mother present: 'They don't wanna arrest you. Just tell them who the guns belong to.'
person_rows.notes: 16-year-old student who held guns for other students. Locker searched publicly at Saint Matthews school with Monsignor and security present. Threatened with accessory to murder charges in front of school officials and mother. Taken to precinct. Later cleared when Billy reveals the gun belonged to Ricky Morales and Kevin Parker. Though not formally charged, suffered public humiliation at school and coercive interrogation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during final scene with Prof. Munson testimony discussion.
episode_summary.summary: Florence Manning is killed by a mail bomb. Her estranged husband, physicist Edward Manning, is initially suspected due to divorce proceedings and his new girlfriend. Max Weiss, a struggling physicist, is arrested after bomb materials are found in his lab drawer. Weiss initially claims Manning hired him, but prosecutors discover Weiss had his own motive: Manning stole his scientific theory and sabotaged his grant application. The case reveals Weiss targeted Manning but accidentally killed Manning's estranged wife at their old shared address. Manning is also investigated for scientific theft/larceny but appears to avoid full cooperation to protect his reputation.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives question Manning at Hudson Polytech nuclear reactor lab, interrogate him about divorce and girlfriend, suggest physicists make bombs. Manning responds: 'Given enough time and money, I could possibly build you an atomic bomb. I know nothing of conventional explosives.'
person_rows.notes: Manning was the primary suspect due to bitter divorce, new girlfriend, and technical capability to build bombs. Detectives questioned him multiple times at his workplace (nuclear reactor lab) in front of colleagues. He was later cleared when evidence pointed to Max Weiss. However, Manning also committed scientific theft against Weiss, creating complex dual-victim scenario.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence at end
episode_summary.summary: Episode involves multiple murders on a single day. Ken Scofield is shot in Battery Park; Drew Washington is killed in a robbery; Scott Hexter becomes primary suspect in Scofield killing based on witness descriptions and parking tickets for tan Chevy in area. Hexter is arrested despite providing alibi (movies) he cannot verify. Evidence shows his car in Forest Park at time of Queens murder AND in Riverdale (hit-and-run) 10 minutes after Battery Park murder - physically impossible. Transcript ends before resolution, but strong implication Hexter may be wrongly accused. Also features Louisa D'Angelo who kills abusive husband (actual perpetrator, not false suspect). No other clearly innocent accused persons identified with sufficient public exposure/harm.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, publicly questioned at workplace (florist), interrogated 16+ hours by Queens police, re-arrested, jailed at Rikers without bail, media exposure threatened
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Because you're a sick bastard who gets his hee-haw's out of killing innocent people... They're gonna pitch a tent outside your mother's house and they're gonna dig up every piece of dirt... We'll keep you here 16 hours or 16 days. We don't care.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Evidence shows physical impossibility: 'Report on a hit-and-run last night in Riverdale. Tan Chevy, same plate as Hexter's, 10 minutes after Scofield was killed. Is it possible he was in both places? Sure, with a pair of wings and a good tail wind. Otherwise, he'd need at least 15 minutes to get up to Riverdale.' ADA Kincaid: 'I'm not convinced he's guilty of anything except being in the wrong place at the wrong time.' Hexter consistently maintains: 'I am so sick of this. Why can't you just believe me? I didn't kill anybody.'
person_rows.notes: Transcript truncated before final resolution, but strong evidence of innocence: (1) physical impossibility of being at two crime scenes 15 minutes apart; (2) no weapon found matching murder (.44); (3) ADA expresses doubt about guilt; (4) refused to confess despite extreme pressure; (5) mother vouches for character; (6) prior 16-hour Queens interrogation yielded nothing. Public exposure includes: detectives visiting workplace (florist on Woodhaven), arrest at work in front of colleagues, threatened media exposure ('6:00 news... pitch a tent outside your mother's house'), remanded without bail at public arraignment. Severity 3 due to arrest, jailing, public court appearance, workplace exposure, and intense interrogation tactics.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end; final dialogue cut off mid-sentence
episode_summary.summary: Benny Williams, business manager and father of baseball star Pat Williams, is beaten to death. Initially, investigators focus on Papa Doc (Henry Doinel), a bookmaker Benny owed money to, and Joey Lang, Papa Doc's enforcer. Papa Doc is arrested and charged with murder. However, evidence emerges that Pat Williams may have lied about his gambling activity and his whereabouts the night of the murder. The transcript ends abruptly during what appears to be a confrontation with Pat about the truth. Papa Doc appears to be innocent or at least wrongly charged based on developing evidence pointing toward Pat.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, arraigned, remanded without bail, publicly tried
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone to defense attorney: 'The entire case depends upon your client's testimony, not just the murder count.' Followed by discussion of dismissing all charges against Papa Doc because Pat Williams' testimony is collapsing.
person_rows.notes: Papa Doc is arrested, arraigned without bail, and publicly tried for Benny Williams' murder. The case relies heavily on Joey Lang's testimony that Papa Doc was the last person with Benny alive. However, investigation reveals Pat Williams lied about his gambling and has two unexplained hours plus phone calls to his father the night of the murder. Stone discusses dismissing all charges against Papa Doc. The transcript ends before resolution, but the dialogue strongly implies Papa Doc is being wrongly prosecuted and Pat may be the actual killer. Papa Doc's profession as a bookmaker made him an easy target, but the evidence increasingly points away from him.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode involves a hit-and-run case (Joshua Berger) and subsequent riot murder (Isaac Roberts). Complex racial tensions and legal maneuvering around both cases.
episode_summary.summary: Joshua Berger, a Jewish businessman, hits and kills 12-year-old Damon Fox in Harlem. Forensic evidence shows he was not speeding (20-25 mph). An eyewitness (Bobby) fabricated testimony claiming Berger was going 50 mph, likely coached by Reverend Ott. Grand jury refuses to indict Berger based on forensic evidence contradicting eyewitness. Berger pleads to leaving the scene. This triggers a riot where Isaac Roberts kills John De Santis (mistaken for Jewish). Roberts is tried for murder but jury deadlocks; DA declines retrial.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Berger testifies: 'Any middle-aged white person... who says that he's not frightened... no, make that terrified... to find himself alone on a city street... being followed by two or three black teenagers... is a liar.' Earlier: 'I was scared. That's why I'm here now.' Forensics expert: 'By comparing the coefficient of friction to the gross vehicular weight... he's doing 20, maybe 25.' Reverend Ott's eyewitness Bobby claims '50 mph' but grand jury refuses to indict. Stone: 'The forensic evidence contradicts an eyewitness account.'
person_rows.notes: Berger is accused of murder/manslaughter after hitting Damon Fox. Forensic evidence (skid marks, speed calculation) proves he was traveling 20-25 mph, not speeding. Eyewitness Bobby (later revealed to be coached by Reverend Ott) fabricated testimony claiming 50 mph. Grand jury hears both versions and refuses to indict for homicide. Berger pleads to leaving the scene only. However, he faces significant public exposure: appears before grand jury, questioned by prosecutors, leaves courthouse through rear door due to crowd threats, later testifies in Roberts trial where he's publicly confronted about his fear of blacks. His business reputation and community standing are damaged. The fabricated accusation triggers citywide riots. Severity 3 due to grand jury appearance, media exposure, community threats requiring police protection, and being central figure in racial controversy.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom scenes but cuts off mid-sentence at end.
episode_summary.summary: A 10-year-old girl, Wendy Sylvester, goes missing from her foster home. Police discover she was taken by Arnette Fenady, a volunteer poetry teacher who had previously been arrested for attempting to kidnap another child. Fenady believes she is rescuing abused children and confuses them with her own dead daughter, Crystal. The case examines the foster care system's failures and Fenady's mental illness. No innocent parties are falsely accused—Fenady is guilty of kidnapping, and the investigation correctly identifies her as the perpetrator. Wendy ultimately lies on the stand to protect Fenady, whom she sees as having saved her from an abusive situation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence. Final dialogue cuts off during legal discussion about Korey Burke's diary.
episode_summary.summary: Tennis prodigy Korey Burke is attacked at practice - her wrist broken, ending her season. Initial suspect Alan Lovitz (obsessed fan) is cleared by lineup. Marc Kenner confesses to being hired by rival player Alison Hall for $10k, but recants when his confession is suppressed. Prosecutors then suspect Korey's father Mitchell Burke orchestrated the attack to preserve endorsement money when Korey wanted to quit. Transcript reveals Korey has history of 'accidents' and sees psychiatrist. Final twist suggests Korey may have arranged her own injury to escape her father's pressure. Alison Hall arrested and charged despite weak motive. Transcript ends mid-legal argument about Korey's psychiatric diary as potential exculpatory evidence.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Korey Burke at lineup: 'No. I don't think it's any of them... No. He's not there.' Detective confirms: 'You're absolutely sure?' Burke: 'Yes.' Then released.
person_rows.notes: Obsessed fan with stalking history and threatening letter ('If you won't play for me, I hope you never play again'). Detectives bring him in based on letter and past incident trying to force into locker room. He voluntarily submits to lineup wanting victim 'to see me.' Victim definitively clears him. No indication exposure went beyond police. Released immediately after lineup.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone to Kincaid: 'We know who did it, but we have no evidence proving it.' Coach: 'The papers have ripped her apart. She's been punished enough.' Defense attorney Joan: 'Mr. Kenner's a jerk. Two months ago, I had to call the cops to toss him out of my apartment... Why? Motive's not part of the prima facie case. But nine times out of ten, lack of it translates into reasonable doubt.' Prosecutors shift theory to Mitchell Burke or Korey herself as actual perpetrators.
person_rows.notes: Tennis player ranked #82, accused of hiring Marc Kenner to attack rival Korey Burke (#5 ranked). Sole evidence is Kenner's confession obtained through police deception (fake forensics), which judge suppresses. Defense attorney correctly notes complete lack of motive - attacking Burke doesn't help Hall's career ('I beat Number 81, who cares?'). Prosecutors acknowledge 'crime doesn't make sense' and later develop alternative theories (Burke's father or Korey self-harm). Hall arrested on-court in front of media/crowd, charged with assault 1st degree and conspiracy, bail $100k. Media coverage ('papers have ripped her apart'). Transcript ends with prosecutors discovering exculpatory evidence (Korey's psychiatric diary showing history of self-injury), strongly implying Hall is innocent scapegoat. Career and reputation destroyed based on recanted confession from unreliable ex-boyfriend with criminal history.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at the end during Ann Madsen's testimony.
episode_summary.summary: Harry Renkmeyer is found dead, initially appearing to be pushed into traffic by a homeless man named Rudy. Investigation reveals Renkmeyer was shot before being run over. Ann Madsen, CEO of Nature's Way baby food company, is coerced into testifying against her business partner Steven Green after initially lying under oath out of fear of the Russian mob (Gruskov family). Rudy is arrested but later cleared when autopsy shows shooting, not traffic accident. Ann Madsen commits perjury under threat, then is arrested for it to force her cooperation.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney: 'The fabric under the victim's nails doesn't match the suspect's coat.' Rodgers: 'The truck didn't kill him. He was already dead. Heart attack... He was shot.' Logan: 'I'll call CSU, tell them to start looking for a shell.'
person_rows.notes: Arrested as initial suspect for pushing Renkmeyer into traffic. Cleared when autopsy revealed victim was shot before being hit by truck. Defense attorney got him released quickly when physical evidence (fabric) didn't match. No indication of public exposure beyond arrest, but transcript doesn't show what happened after release.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Stone: 'Officer, the charge is felony perjury. Take her to the 5th Precinct.' Schiff: 'And we do this to hardened criminals, not innocent bystanders.' Stone: 'If she lies, she's no longer innocent... But maybe a few nights in Rikers will change her mind.'
person_rows.notes: Ann Madsen DID commit perjury (lied under oath about seeing Rostov), but her initial lie was driven by genuine fear of mob retaliation. Stone arrested her not because she was the primary subject of investigation, but as leverage to force her to testify truthfully against Steven Green. The harm (arrest, jail time in Rikers, public exposure in court) was a prosecutorial tactic rather than a legitimate criminal case against her as a primary offender. Stone himself acknowledges this is unusual ('we do this to hardened criminals, not innocent bystanders'). She is 'partially_involved' because she did lie under oath, but the accusation's severity (felony perjury arrest) was disproportionate and instrumentalized. Transcript is truncated during her testimony, so final resolution unknown.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony. Episode centers on Dr. Nancy Haas, a non-MD scientist offering alternative cancer treatment with apricot seeds, accused of murdering Ann Bennett by convincing her to forgo surgery.
episode_summary.summary: Ann Bennett dies of liver cancer after following Dr. Nancy Haas's metabolic therapy instead of having surgery. Investigation reveals Haas charged patients $75,000 for treatments using apricot seeds (containing trace cyanide/amygdaline). No innocent suspects are publicly accused; investigation focuses on Haas from early on. Nicholas Bennett is briefly considered but never publicly accused or confronted as a suspect. The case proceeds as a murder prosecution against Haas for fraudulently convincing patients to avoid life-saving surgery.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end during Detective Logan's dialogue
episode_summary.summary: Sandra Dobson is shot and left in a coma. Police initially suspect a car radio thief named Jerome who was in the area. Jerome is arrested for radio thefts but denies shooting anyone. The investigation shifts to Michael Dobson, Sandra's husband, who is eventually arrested and tried for attempted murder/murder. The transcript appears to cut off during trial proceedings, so the final resolution is unclear.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: MONTERO: 'Spotted him running across Broadway. Three Blaupunkts and a Delco in his bag.' JEROME: 'Yo, I didn't shoot nobody. There was no shot.' JEROME: 'Man, the lady was bagged when I got here.' Detective: 'Take him in.' JEROME: 'What's up?' Detective: 'For the radios.'
person_rows.notes: Jerome was spotted running from the crime scene with stolen car radios. He was arrested but clearly states he didn't shoot anyone and says the woman was already shot when he arrived. Detectives acknowledge 'First the shooter, then this guy' indicating they know Jerome is a separate case. He was arrested only for the radio thefts, not the shooting. No indication of public exposure beyond the arrest itself - appears to be a straightforward theft arrest that happened to occur at a shooting scene.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-sentence during final discussion.
episode_summary.summary: Japanese businessman Shiunro Hayashi is murdered in his hotel room. Investigation initially focuses on hotel employee Danny Zabel and his brother-in-law Charles Kett (who robbed another hotel guest previously). Zabel is arrested as accomplice but later cleared when Kett has alibi. Singer Suzy is questioned after being with Hayashi but cleared when forensics show different woman's hair. Investigation ultimately leads to Martha Bowen, who killed Hayashi after he forced her into prostitution in Japan a year earlier. Two innocent people publicly accused: Danny Zabel and Suzy.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LOGAN: Robberies happen all the time. Now, maybe the detectives down at the Barrington let things slide, but we don't. We want the shooter, big guy. BRISCOE: I don't know, I... Good, we'll take you. You're just as good for it.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives threaten to arrest Zabel: 'Good, we'll take you. You're just as good for it.' Zabel then admits his brother-in-law Charlie Kett was the actual robber. Later cleared when Kett is found to have been in Rikers during Hayashi murder.
person_rows.notes: Zabel worked at front desk where Hayashi cashed $10k in traveler's checks. Previously worked at Barrington hotel where similar robbery occurred. Admitted under pressure that brother-in-law Charlie Kett committed Barrington robbery and threatened him to help. Cleared when Kett found to be in Rikers on DWI at time of Hayashi murder.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LOGAN: And meanwhile, the better part of 10 grand is falling out of his wallet. BRISCOE: I'm calling my lawyer. LOGAN: Good. Tell him to meet you at the station house.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Suzy about her criminal record: 'I just called up her yellows. Felony larceny, solicitation, and a little incident of you discharging a weapon. .22, surprise, surprise.' They arrest her and take her to station. Cleared when forensic evidence shows blonde pubic hair on victim's watch, but Suzy is brunette.
person_rows.notes: Suzy admitted being with Hayashi and going to his room but denied having sex or killing him. Has prior record including weapons charge with .22 caliber gun. Arrested based on being last known person with victim and prior weapon possession. Cleared when forensic hair evidence proved different woman (blonde, not brunette) was in Hayashi's bed.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at the end during Maggie's testimony
episode_summary.summary: Laura Madsen is found dead in the East River. Victor Conner (her ex-husband) is initially suspected due to a history of domestic violence and a recent argument over custody papers. He is publicly investigated but later cleared when the timeline and evidence point to Steve Martell (Laura's current husband) and potentially Maggie Conner (Laura's daughter). The transcript ends mid-testimony, making full analysis incomplete.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Sally (Laura's colleague): 'Is Victor Conner a suspect?' followed by detectives investigating him at Madsen Publishing where he maintains an office two floors below Laura's
person_rows.notes: Conner was investigated due to prior domestic violence (911 tape shows him breaking into bathroom where Laura and Maggie were hiding), recent argument over custody papers worth 'six zeros,' and Laura's car found coming from Queens direction. Timeline shows he had alibi (limo to airport confirmed, flight to Boston). Investigation at his workplace (Madsen Publishing) constitutes public exposure to colleagues including Sally Kessler who discusses his history openly with detectives.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-testimony. Analysis may be incomplete.
episode_summary.summary: Episode concerns a 1971 armed robbery of Nucon Technologies where Officer Vincent Perella was killed. In present day (1994), the stolen money and murder weapon are recovered from a safe deposit box. Investigation reveals Susan Forrest (living under alias Rita Levitan) was involved. However, Henry Maybrook (security guard) and his cousin Eddie were falsely accused of the vault burglary before the real perpetrators were identified. Professor William Goodwin was also accused but appears to have actually aided Susan Forrest (partial involvement). Transcript cuts off during Margaret Pauley's testimony, making complete analysis difficult.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'We tear your life apart, are we gonna find somebody in it with a hydraulic jack?' ... 'Let's go! Come on!' ... 'or you finish your recovery in an infirmary at Rikers. No private rooms there, Henry. No pretty nurses. The orderlies there'll slit your throat to steal your I.V.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Henry is recovering in hospital from alcoholic cardiomyopathy after being handcuffed in vault by real burglars. Detectives aggressively interrogate him in hospital bed, threaten him with Rikers if he doesn't give up accomplices. He finally gives up his cousin Eddie under duress. Later Eddie is arrested and charged, but cooperates and reveals the real stolen property location, proving Eddie WAS guilty of the vault burglary.
person_rows.notes: Henry was a victim of the vault burglary (jumped and handcuffed by real burglars). Detectives suspected him of being involved due to finding his whiskey bottle in basement and him lying about never going there. They threatened him in his hospital bed while recovering from serious heart condition. However, Henry then implicates his cousin Eddie, who turns out to be ACTUALLY GUILTY of the vault burglary. Henry himself appears innocent of the vault burglary but was coerced. The exposure was at his workplace (vault facility) and in hospital with manager present.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final courtroom resolution. Lieutenant Van Buren shoots James Gordon (mentally retarded, unarmed 14-year-old) during attempted ATM robbery. Zack Rowland (accomplice with gun) initially accused, later charged with felony murder.
episode_summary.summary: Lt. Van Buren shoots unarmed, mentally retarded 14-year-old James Gordon during ATM encounter, believing accomplice Zack Rowland had a gun. James dies; Van Buren faces investigation and grand jury. No-billed by grand jury prioritizing public safety over scrutiny of police conduct. Zack charged with felony murder after gun recovered. Episode explores competence to commit crime, prosecutorial withholding of evidence (bracelet motive), and racial dynamics in justice system. James Gordon qualifies as innocent person harmed by false accusation of attempted robbery—he lacked mental capacity to form criminal intent and was buying girlfriend's bracelet with legitimately earned money.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: OLIVET: 'James Gordon had a form of mental retardation known as microencephaly. The frontal lobes of his brain never developed... He lacked the ability to perform higher order activities, such as logical reasoning and planning... was he capable of forming the requisite criminal intent to perform a criminal act as defined by the penal law? No.' MARJORIE: 'I gave him a dollar a week. He spent it on baseball cards... He must've sold them to get the money.' [for the bracelet engraved 'Guinevere']
person_rows.notes: James Gordon is the clearest case of an innocent person killed due to false accusation. Expert testimony proves he lacked mental capacity to form criminal intent (IQ 40, microencephaly, kindergarten-level functioning). His presence at ATM was not criminal—he had legitimately earned money (sold baseball cards) to buy girlfriend's ID bracelet. He was unarmed. Van Buren shot him believing he was accomplice to armed robbery, but evidence shows: (1) he couldn't form intent to commit crime, (2) he had legitimate funds for the gift he wanted to buy, (3) girlfriend testified he 'wouldn't steal.' His mother lost her son; his reputation was publicly destroyed as 'robber.' Van Buren gives partial apology to mother at end, acknowledging 'I killed him... and I will never live a day without that tearing me up.' Consequence severity = 4 (death). Public exposure through investigation, media coverage of shooting, grand jury proceedings.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at the end during plea discussion. Episode centers on Eileen and Martin Willach, whose infant Emily dies. Investigation reveals two prior infant deaths (Daniel, Caroline) and an incident with foster child Gary.
episode_summary.summary: Baby Emily Willach is reported kidnapped from Central Park but is found dead in a cooler by the Hudson River. Investigation reveals Martin Willach faked the kidnapping to cover up the death. Focus shifts to mother Eileen Willach, suspected of smothering Emily and potentially two previous biological children plus harming a foster child. However, a bald man with a camera was witnessed in the park that morning and became an early suspect before being cleared. The case ends in a hung jury (9-3 for conviction) with prosecution seeking retrial.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: A witness puts you in the park this morning. [...] I know what this is about. It's that kid, the one on TV? [...] Kids are not my thing.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'A witness puts you in the park this morning.' ROBERT: 'I know what this is about. It's that kid, the one on TV?' [...] He has the girl's name, number, everything. He's in the clear as far as this case goes.
person_rows.notes: Robert was identified by a Guatemalan nanny as a 'bald man' taking pictures of kids in the playground around the time of the kidnapping. He was brought in for questioning, and detectives found a diaper in his possession, creating suspicion. However, he was quickly cleared when his alibi and his contact list of nannies proved he was a 'commercials agent' using a pickup line to meet lonely nannies. No public exposure beyond police interrogation. Logan states 'He's in the clear as far as this case goes.' The real crime (smothering) occurred at home, not in the park. Severity is 1 because questioning was private with no public exposure or formal charges.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through defense opening; episode focuses on sexual coercion/quid pro quo harassment rather than traditional rape case
episode_summary.summary: Lyssa Wesley dies in car crash after alleged rape by Councilman Spencer Talbert. Investigation reveals pattern of sexual coercion at his former law firm. Sarah Maslin initially claims rape but recants, revealing quid pro quo sexual harassment (partnership in exchange for sex). Prosecution pivots to larceny by extortion theory. Episode centers on distinguishing forcible rape from coerced consent under threat of career destruction.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LOGAN: 'Give me your cuffs, Lennie.' / BRISCOE: 'In the meantime, you're under arrest for the rape of Lyssa Wesley.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Locke arrested for rape but cooperates and reveals Talbert as perpetrator. DA offers plea: 'Cooperate and the charge is leaving the scene of an accident. Six months' probation.'
person_rows.notes: Locke was driving Lyssa home after Talbert allegedly raped her. Detectives initially suspect him of rape due to his presence at scene. He's arrested and threatened with multiple felonies before cooperating. Ultimately charged only with leaving scene of accident.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: SARAH: 'Now the press is all over my office. They're in the lobby of my apartment. I can't live like this, Miss Kincaid.' / McCoy threatens her: 'The case against Talbert is dropped, Miss Maslin. The case against you for conspiracy is just beginning.'
person_rows.notes: Sarah initially claims Talbert raped her, then recants when facing public exposure. Prosecution accuses her of lying/political motivation ('conspiracy'). Ultimately proven she told truth about coerced sex (quid pro quo harassment, not forcible rape). Press exposes her, career threatened by having to testify about 'sleeping her way to partnership.' McCoy's threat of conspiracy charges was baseless intimidation—she was actually a victim of different crime (extortion/coercion).

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony
episode_summary.summary: Lawyer Arthur Kopinsky is murdered. Initial investigation focuses on Carl Piselli, who was publicly accused of the murder and cleared via alibi. John Curren, a Tappan victim, is then arrested and tried. The case reveals Kopinsky was running a scam on Tappan victims while also blackmailing Tappan over hidden money. Tappan manipulated Curren into killing Kopinsky. Both Piselli and initially John Curren qualify as falsely accused persons who suffered public exposure and consequences.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'Yeah, well, Piselli fits the bill. He wears glasses and he's mad.' Later: 'Check his alibi first.' Detectives visit him at his business (Cleans and Dirties product), question him publicly. Alice Huntley testifies: 'Did the police indicate to you they had a suspect in the murder? Yes. Carl Piselli. Not John Curren? No. They didn't mention him.'
person_rows.notes: Piselli was the primary suspect during initial investigation. Police questioned him at his business about inventing dishwasher magnets. He had motive (angry at Kopinsky), threatened Kopinsky and Alice Huntley, wore glasses matching witness description. Cleared by solid alibi (Hudson Jazz Orchestra concert with witnesses). Alice Huntley's trial testimony confirms police identified him as their suspect to her, not Curren. Public questioning at his business constitutes exposure even though he was ultimately cleared quickly via alibi.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'Come on, Johnny. You found out Kopinsky was fleecing your mother after he had already fleeced you, and you got mad! Hell, I would've gotten mad.' LOGAN: 'So you went downtown to get your mother's money back. What happened? Did Kopinsky stall you? No. Did he laugh at you? No.' BRISCOE: 'I give up. What happened?' When Curren asks for lawyer: 'VAN BUREN: John Curren, you're under arrest for the murder of Arthur Kopinsky.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Lineup identification by Tanaka ('I'd think number four'). Arrested in front of wife and children at home. Trial testimony reveals Tappan manipulated him: 'He said he'd pay us back, my mother and me. Every cent, if I killed Kopinsky.' Prosecution offers manslaughter deal (8.33 years) in exchange for testimony against Tappan, the true architect of the murder.
person_rows.notes: PARTIALLY_INVOLVED: Curren actually committed the murder (pulled trigger), BUT was manipulated into doing so by Tappan, who orchestrated the killing to end Kopinsky's blackmail. Curren is a victim of Tappan's fraud (lost $830K lifesavings) who was weaponized by Tappan. The episode reveals the true architect was Tappan. Curren suffered: arrest in front of family, $100K bail he couldn't afford, public trial, coercive police interrogation. Eventually accepts manslaughter plea (8.33 years) to testify against Tappan. His harm stems from being falsely positioned as the sole responsible party when Tappan bears primary moral/legal responsibility. Including because the show's resolution treats Tappan as the real murderer ('evincing depraved indifference') and Curren as a manipulated tool.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom hearing scene
episode_summary.summary: David Lempert, a city Parks Department accountant, is murdered after serving as a juror in mob boss Vincent Dosso's trial. Investigation reveals Lempert was bribed to hang the jury. Defense attorney Paul Kopell helped facilitate jury tampering by bribing a court clerk to identify vulnerable jurors. Lempert was later killed, likely because he became a liability. The episode focuses on prosecuting Dosso and Kopell for conspiracy to murder. No falsely accused innocent persons are identified in the transcript - Furini and Dosso appear to be the actual perpetrators, and Kopell facilitated the crime. Lempert was a victim who accepted a bribe but was not accused of a crime he didn't commit.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated during Matthew Blanchard's cross-examination testimony.
episode_summary.summary: Katie Blanchard, a wealthy heroin addict, is found dead behind a dumpster. Her brother Matthew and family adviser Jerome Kamen are accused at different points. Matthew injected Katie with heroin and called Kamen when she started convulsing. Kamen took her from the apartment but left her to die instead of seeking medical help. Matthew was initially suspected and later testified against Kamen. The transcript cuts off during cross-examination, making final disposition unclear.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Enrico Sariego. Says he was partying here last night. 'So, is this one of your party pals?' 'No. I never seen her before.'
person_rows.notes: Party attendee near crime scene, briefly questioned and cleared. No public exposure beyond police interaction. Does not meet severity threshold for inclusion but documented for completeness.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: 'We're gonna check her ID. If she is one day shy of 17, we toss this whole place, then maybe we find a controlled substance.' / 'Get him some breakfast. Stick around, all right?'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Erik at his workplace (Club Beecees where he works the door) about Katie's death. They threaten to search the club and check IDs of underage girls. Erik admits he brought her heroin a week ago but denies involvement in her death. 'She called about a week ago. She was hurting real bad. She needed a score... I brought it to her... that's the last time I saw her.'
person_rows.notes: Katie's ex-boyfriend, initially suspected as 'preferred customer' drug connection. Detectives use coercive tactics (threats to search club, check underage girls' IDs) to extract information at his workplace. Admitted supplying drugs but cleared of involvement in her death. Public exposure at workplace in front of presumably other employees/patrons qualifies as severity 2.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: 'I'm sure the Feds can help me track it down.' / 'We don't really have to get them involved, do we?'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Fowler about cashing Katie's Social Security checks after she left rehab. 'According to Uncle Sam, you were still cashing her checks.' Fowler admits: 'I thought she'd come back sooner or later. I was holding her money... It's in the general operating fund.'
person_rows.notes: EXCLUDE FROM ANALYSIS - Actually guilty of fraud (embezzling disability checks from multiple addicts). Included only for context as he was suspected of involvement in Katie's death but cleared of that. His fraud was a separate crime, not the murder accusation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: 'We can call the dean and check your attendance records... We match your prints on this cup to any prints at Katie's apartment, and you're cooked.'
