person_rows.quote_or_scene: Max brought gun to party. His prints on weapon. Detectives interrogate him. CURTIS: 'We found this gun there, Max. It's got your prints on it.' Max explains he brought the gun, someone took it from his pocket. Alibi witness Gloria confirms: 'I came up in the elevator with him... he pretty much kept an eye on me the whole night.' Max charged with criminal possession of weapon but cleared of involvement in May's death.
person_rows.notes: Max Paris arrested as suspect because his fingerprints were on the murder weapon (database match). He had prior conviction for dealing drugs (6 months at Rikers). Alibi witness Gloria confirms he was with her entire time at party. Transcript shows he was 'booked for criminal possession of the weapon' but cleared of murder. Exposure appears limited to police interrogation. Excluded from count: while arrested (severity 3), exposure channel was police_only with no evidence of public/workplace/family exposure. The gun possession charge is separate from the false murder accusation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-cross-examination
episode_summary.summary: Andrea Blake is stalked via e-mail by 'Giovanni' and eventually murdered. Police initially suspect Louis Dutton (voyeur photographer) and Russell Lowery (convicted hacker). Blake fabricated first attack (threw herself down stairs) after police wouldn't help, creating credibility issues. Lowery ultimately charged with murder and attempted murder. Transcript ends mid-trial, unclear if conviction obtained. Key issue: 911 tape excluded because Blake lied about first attack, undermining her credibility when she identified killer as 'Giovanni.'

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Bouncing breasts are art? Troglodyte.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: CURTIS: We're seizing your computer, your camera, and 16 rolls of exposed, but unprocessed film... And we're arresting you... violation of penal law 235.21, and 240.20 sub 7... ROSS: They dropped the ball, somebody got killed. They're sure, and they want him [Lowery]... BRISCOE: They deduced he's an opera fan... ROSS: Lowery was in prison in Pennsylvania when the e-mail started.
person_rows.notes: Dutton was a voyeur who photographed women without consent and ran explicit website. He lived across airshaft from Blake's first apartment when e-mail started. Police arrested him for disseminating indecent material and harassment while investigating Blake's murder. He was in Rikers at time of murder, proving innocence. Charges related to voyeurism may have stood, but murder accusation was dropped when Lowery identified as real suspect. Arrested and publicly questioned/insulted by detectives.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end during Ben Odell's statement
episode_summary.summary: Paul and Donna Ericson and developer Faber are kidnapped and later found murdered. Building manager Faber is initially the prime suspect due to legal disputes with the Ericsons over his coffee bar, but he himself is kidnapped and killed. The actual killer is Matthew Odell, a mentally ill political extremist who targeted gentrifiers. Faber was wrongly suspected and then became a victim.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'Bring him in for a cup of our coffee.' / Employee: 'Faber's been gone awhile. He usually likes to keep an eye on the register.' / Later found murdered in warehouse.
person_rows.notes: Faber was the initial prime suspect due to financial motive (Ericsons' complaint could close his coffee bar and cause loan default). Detectives brought him in for questioning at his business. He was later kidnapped from Queens wholesaler and murdered by the actual killer Matthew Odell. Severity 4 due to being murdered as consequence of investigation making him visible/vulnerable to the actual perpetrator.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-sentence during Dr. Semenko's testimony.
episode_summary.summary: 12-year-old quadriplegic Michael Sutter dies from poison hemlock. Initially his mother Lois is suspected (she had an alibi with a lover), then his father Joe Sutter is arrested and charged. Eventually Dr. Lyle Semenko, the boy's physician, is revealed as the actual killer—a serial mercy killer with 17+ deaths across multiple hospitals. Both parents endured public accusation, arrest (father), and trial testimony despite being innocent.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: CURTIS: 'You couldn't stand sleeping next to a guy who'd turned your son into a potted plant. As long as Michael was still alive, you didn't have the guts to walk out.' BRISCOE: 'How'd you do it? With a pillow? A blanket?' Shows M.E. report: 'You know what that means?' LOIS: 'That can't be Michael.' 'Michael just died.' CURTIS: 'It's right there in black and white.' LOIS: 'No, it can't be. That's impossible.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Lois in interrogation, showing her the M.E. report and accusing her directly: 'You wanted out of your marriage... You couldn't stand sleeping next to a guy who'd turned your son into a potted plant.' She breaks down and reveals she left Michael with daughter Stephanie, shifting suspicion.
person_rows.notes: Lois was having an affair with dance instructor Gilbert during time of murder (3:30-4:40pm), giving her an alibi. Detectives used aggressive tactics including showing M.E. report and accusing her of murder. She broke under pressure and blamed her daughter. Exposure was to family (daughter and husband learned of affair and accusation). No formal charges filed once alibi confirmed, but suffered family rupture and public confrontation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'Were you jealous of all the attention he got? All the trouble he caused?' CURTIS: 'The whole family was falling apart because of him.' Then: 'You just have to tell us what happened. I mean, your brother was barely living anyway, right?' BRISCOE: 'Your mom's a better liar than you are, Stephanie.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Mother tells detectives 'I left him with Stephanie' after being accused herself. Detectives interrogate Stephanie: 'You killed your brother... Were you jealous of all the attention he got?' Stephanie denies, provides alibi with friend Kara at Asteroid Palace arcade from 3:15-4:15, confirmed by witnesses.
person_rows.notes: Accused by her own mother under police pressure. Detectives used coercive tactics suggesting brother 'was barely living anyway.' Alibi confirmed by friend and arcade workers. Exposure limited to interrogation room with detectives—no indication it spread to school, media, or broader community beyond family knowing mother blamed her.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: CURTIS to Joe: 'You see your boy, he used to be strong. Played soccer, yeah? ... Did you imagine Michael at 20 years old? Forty years old? Still in that same room, same bed, same tortured pain? ... Someone you love is suffering. They have no hope. What can you do to help this person? ... You had to do it. You had to do it because you loved him. Let it go. It was an act of love, Joe.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detective Curtis uses empathy-based coercion comparing Joe's situation to his own wife's MS, saying 'It was an act of love, Joe. I loved Michael.' Joe responds 'I didn't do this. I didn't kill him.' Arrested anyway. Case goes to trial with bail set at $100,000 (posted by employer Cashman). Trial testimony shows Semenko was the killer. Charges eventually dropped when Semenko arrested.
person_rows.notes: Strongest case: arrested, $100K bail, went to public trial as defendant. Evidence: worked on Southern State Parkway where hemlock grows wild, witness (Mrs. Bing) saw him spike food with 'cinnamon,' employer (Cashman) initially told police Joe wished he'd never saved Michael (later recanted). Endured coercive interrogation, public arrest, trial with cross-examination revealing affair and family dysfunction. Wife's affair exposed in trial. Employer posted bail showing support. Prosecution dropped case when Dr. Semenko arrested with hemlock in his car. Suffered massive financial (legal fees, lost work time), reputational (publicly accused of murdering disabled son), and family (wife's affair exposed, marriage strained) harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom finale and interview scene.
episode_summary.summary: Officer Dana Flynn is murdered in Riverside Park. Jesse Rosado is found shot at the scene, initially suspected of the murder. Detectives discover Monica Johnson (using alias Monica Summers) was on the same bus and followed Flynn into the park. Investigation reveals Rosado was an innocent bystander who tried to stop the attack and was shot by Flynn in her final moments. Monica is convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to death. She later claims religious conversion and waives appeals, creating legal and moral controversy.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: CURTIS: 'The second victim isn't dead. He's male Hispanic, early 20s. One shot to the head.' BRISCOE: 'Kid jumps Flynn, she badges him, he starts to stab her, she shoots him. Maybe he'll kick, and save everybody the trouble.' Later proven: 'I tried to stop her. I tried to pull her off from the woman.'
person_rows.notes: Rosado was shot in the head by Officer Flynn while trying to rescue her from Monica Johnson. Detectives initially theorized he was the attacker. He suffered life-threatening gunshot wound requiring hospitalization, critical condition, and post-traumatic amnesia. Mayor awards him medal after truth emerges. Physical harm (shot in head, nearly died) directly resulted from being misidentified as the attacker in the moment of the crime.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end during Valerie's testimony.
episode_summary.summary: A school shooting involves Sally Maxwell (14) shooting at three boys who raped her mentally disabled sister Valerie. Sally is accused of attempted murder of teacher Amanda Novak (innocent bystander hit by stray bullet). The three boys (Hayden, Randy, Roscoe) are initially accused/convicted of rape but Judge Wright sets aside the jury verdict, declaring them innocent despite evidence they knew Valerie was mentally disabled. The episode explores false accusations in multiple directions: Sally accused of intentionally shooting the teacher (she was shooting at the boys who shot first), and the rape case where the judge controversially rules the sex was consensual despite Valerie's mental disability.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Sally: 'They shot at me first! I didn't mean to hurt Mrs. Novak.' Later, prosecution offers 1-year juvenile detention acknowledging she was shooting at the boys in self-defense, not targeting the teacher.
person_rows.notes: Sally was accused of attempted murder of teacher Amanda Novak. The evidence shows Sally was shooting at three boys who had raped her sister and who shot at her first. She accidentally hit the teacher. She's innocent of attempted murder of the teacher specifically - this was collateral damage while defending herself. DA eventually offers minimal sentence (1 year juvenile detention) acknowledging the mitigating circumstances. The teacher herself 'asked us not to land on the girl with both feet.'

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Judge Wright: 'Have we gone so far down the road of political correctness that sex between willing partners is now called rape? The girl said yes. Case dismissed.' Jury had found guilty but judge sets aside verdict.
person_rows.notes: EXCLUDED FROM HARM COUNT - Actually guilty of raping mentally disabled girl. Jury convicted but judge wrongly set aside verdict. This represents a failure of justice but defendants are NOT innocent.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Judge Wright sets aside jury guilty verdict. Randy also charged with attempted murder as leverage (responding to Sally's gunfire).
person_rows.notes: EXCLUDED FROM HARM COUNT - Actually guilty of rape. The attempted murder charge was prosecutorial leverage and was accurate (he created zone of danger by telling Gina he wouldn't back down from Sally).

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Roscoe takes plea deal: 'one year for sexual abuse in the second degree' in exchange for testifying against co-defendants. Judge still sets aside verdicts.
person_rows.notes: EXCLUDED FROM HARM COUNT - Actually guilty of rape. Took plea deal and testified truthfully against co-defendants.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-cross-examination
episode_summary.summary: Maggie Abbott, appellate court nominee, dies after running into traffic while being pursued by tabloid columnist Phil Marco. Marco is initially suspected of causing her death through harassment. He pleads guilty to minor charges but is later murdered. Warren Abbott confesses to killing Marco to protect his daughter Laura from a story about her birth origins, but prosecutor suggests Warren manipulated media for business purposes.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: CURTIS: 'Are the People satisfied?' 'Yes, Your Honor.' Sentenced to 30 days suspended, community service, fine, probation for harassment/assault after Maggie Abbott's death.
person_rows.notes: Marco harassed Maggie Abbott and grabbed her sweater before she ran into traffic and died. He was accused of causing her death but only convicted of harassment/assault—lesser charges than the implicit manslaughter investigation. He did commit harassment but is 'innocent' of manslaughter/murder. However, he was later murdered, making his exposure/consequences from the Abbott case investigation significant. The courtroom exposure is public record.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'So where were you last night?' ABBOTT: 'Well, I wasn't outside Morean's shooting Phil Marco.' Initial questioning about wife's death, then Marco's murder.
person_rows.notes: EXCLUDED from person_rows per rules—Warren Abbott confessed to killing Phil Marco and the show treats him as guilty. He was initially questioned about both his wife's death and Marco's murder but confessed and is being prosecuted. Not innocent.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ROSS: 'Isn't that why you went home, to get the gun?' McCoy threatens arrest: 'If you leave now, Mr. Abbott, I will have no choice but to arrest your daughter.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: JACK: 'If you leave now, Mr. Abbott, I will have no choice but to arrest your daughter.' ROSS: 'Her whereabouts are unaccounted for and she had access to the same caliber weapon that killed Marco. And she had a motive.' Father present during interrogation; threat of arrest used as leverage.
person_rows.notes: Laura was interrogated with her father present about Marco's murder. Prosecutors threatened to arrest her if Warren left the room, using her as leverage. Warren then confessed to protect her. While contained to the interrogation room (not public media exposure), the threat of arrest in front of her father and the coercive tactics constitute harm. She had prior suicide attempt history mentioned by her aunt, making the interrogation particularly concerning. Severity 2: public confrontation with authority figure threatening arrest.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through plea agreement scene
episode_summary.summary: 10-year-old Malika Richardson is brutally raped and left in a coma. Police initially arrest Owen Stokes based on witness IDs and his confession (obtained through coercive interrogation). Later evidence reveals the actual perpetrator was Jesse Castillo, a security guard with access to the crime scene. Stokes was publicly accused, arrested, indicted, and faced mob violence before being exonerated.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: CURTIS: 'If you did something that warrants us putting you in protective custody, now's the time to tell us.' BRISCOE: 'If you don't want to put your cards on the table, that's fine, but then we don't put our cards down, either.' BRISCOE: 'We're gonna put you in the Tombs tonight. In the general population. They watch the news. They know your face. They know what you're being charged with... Those cons in the Tombs, they're not gonna be so merciful. They're gonna take their time with a short-eyes like you.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: FELDMAN: 'Mr. Stokes, my apologies to you. I recommend at this point you speak to a civil attorney. The indictment is dismissed. You're free to leave.' Earlier: 'A crowd of good Samaritans found our perp. Patrol cars got here just in time to save his ass.' STOKES: 'What's up with these people? They was gonna kill me.'
person_rows.notes: Stokes was beaten by a mob before police arrival, then arrested based on two witness IDs (drug dealer Calvin Berry and security guard who sketched him). Police used extreme coercion—threatening him with gen pop in Tombs where he'd be tortured by inmates—to extract a false confession. He was publicly arrested, indicted, and case was advancing to trial before DA discovered evidence pointing to Castillo. Judge Feldman dismissed indictment and apologized. Stokes has prior conviction for loitering near schoolyard and was taking Depo Provera (sex offender treatment drug), making him vulnerable target. Violence from mob + coerced confession + public indictment = severity 4.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-sentence during Megan Connery's testimony.
episode_summary.summary: A baby dies from medical neglect. Initially, 7-year-old Nicholas Waring is suspected and arrested for murder after confessing to attacking the baby. The M.E. later determines the baby died of heart failure before Nicholas touched her, exonerating him. The real culprits are the adoptive parents (the Connerys) who abandoned a sick baby they got from Russia, and Dr. Kostov who facilitated adoption fraud. Nicholas suffered public arrest, removal from home, and psychiatric lockup based on false murder accusation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Officers treat Nicholas gently during arrest despite his distress.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: CURTIS: 'We have to arrest him.' [Later] SKODA describes Nicholas confessing to hitting the baby. M.E. later reveals: 'that little girl was dead by the time her brother laid a hand on her.' CARMICHAEL to parents: 'It's a relief to know Nicholas didn't kill her.'
person_rows.notes: Nicholas is a 7-year-old with severe attachment disorder and history of abuse. He confessed to attacking the baby postmortem (not knowing she was already dead). ADA Carmichael had him arrested and removed from parents' custody, placing him in Bellevue psychiatric lockup. Parents witnessed arrest and begged for him to come home. M.E.'s autopsy proved baby died of heart failure before Nicholas touched her. While Nicholas has severe behavioral issues, the murder accusation was false. Parents were removed as guardians through legal maneuvering before the arrest. The arrest scene shows Nicholas being taken away while parents plead. Carmichael acknowledges relief he didn't kill the baby but no formal apology shown in transcript. Severity 4 due to arrest of a 7-year-old, institutionalization, and family separation—all life-altering for a child.

person_rows.person_label: Elizabeth and Phillip Waring (Nicholas's adoptive parents)
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'Are we under arrest?' CURTIS: 'Not at the moment.' [Implying potential arrest] Later: 'Then you can come to the station with us.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Elizabeth Waring: 'I can't believe you think we would hurt her. You think we spent five years on a waiting list just so we could beat a baby to death three days after we brought her home?' Detectives demand entry to apartment. Later, ADA Carmichael has court remove them as Nicholas's guardians so psychiatrist can interview him. Mrs. Waring: 'You people look at him and you see a little monster. My husband and I see a wonderful boy who has suffered horrors.'
person_rows.notes: The Warings were initially suspected of killing the baby they had just adopted. Detectives publicly confronted them, demanded apartment access, threatened station house questioning. When they refused entry, detectives obtained warrant. ADA Carmichael later used family court to remove them as Nicholas's guardians, arguing they were abusive/neglectful based on Nicholas's locked room—this was actually recommended therapy for his attachment disorder. The guardianship removal allowed psychiatrist access to Nicholas, leading to his arrest. Parents witnessed their son being arrested and taken away. Baby's death was later proven to be from pre-existing medical conditions, not abuse by the Warings. Carmichael acknowledges Nicholas didn't kill the baby but no apology to parents shown. The transcript shows sustained suspicion and legal actions against loving parents trying to care for extremely difficult children. Severity 3: formal legal action (guardianship removal), child removal, public accusation, but not imprisonment.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-testimony. Final outcome unclear.
episode_summary.summary: Floyd Michaels, a black auxiliary police officer, is profiled, beaten, and killed by NYPD officers Carlson, Fratelli, and Dietrich after a traffic stop. The episode focuses on prosecuting the officers, with witness cooperation issues and immunity debates. No innocent persons are falsely accused; the victim Michaels is proven innocent of any wrongdoing (suspected robbery surveillance target). Transcript ends mid-trial testimony from Fratelli, who pleaded guilty and is testifying against his co-defendants.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through arraignment/grand jury deliberations but cuts off mid-sentence at end.
episode_summary.summary: Kevin Stanton (17) is shot and lies about a carjacking by 'two black guys.' Investigation reveals he was working as an underage informant for Detective Rivera. Drug dealers Mathis and Benvenidez are arrested for shooting Kevin and killing his girlfriend Sarah. Kevin's lies stem from Rivera threatening him to stay silent. Two other juveniles (Eddie and cousin Ricky) were arrested by Rivera in May; Eddie claims Rivera framed them for refusing to inform. The episode explores Rivera's obstruction and possible manslaughter charges for letting kids walk into danger.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: EDDIE: 'Rivera singled me out because I wouldn't work for him... Informing, right? I told him he could stick it and then he passed me off to his partner and now here I sit.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: EDDIE: 'Whole thing was a hook, man. I wasn't doing nothing but holding and they get me for intent to sell? It's a total hook... Detective Rivera framed you? Yeah, he singled me out because I wouldn't work for him.'
person_rows.notes: Eddie claims Rivera framed him with intent-to-sell charges for refusing to inform. He's doing time at Crossroads juvenile facility. Tagged as partially_involved because he admits to 'holding' (possession) but claims the elevated charge (intent to sell) was retaliation for refusing to become an informant. The harm (incarceration at Crossroads) appears disproportionate if his account is accurate. His cousin Ricky had similar experience per Eddie's testimony.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: EDDIE: 'Rivera runs the same game on my cuz Ricky. And here he sits, Eddie. Framed just like you.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: EDDIE: 'And Rivera runs the same game on my cuz Ricky.' CURTIS: 'Framed just like you.' EDDIE: 'That's right. It's an injustice.'
person_rows.notes: Known only through Eddie's testimony. No direct confirmation of Ricky's status, charges, or whether he's actually innocent of elevated charges. Tagged low confidence due to lack of corroboration. Like Eddie, appears to be at Crossroads for drug charges that may have been inflated for refusing to inform for Rivera. Included because pattern matches Eddie's account and both arrested same day (May 12th incident).

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final scene discussing Allgen Corporation's sales practices.
episode_summary.summary: A 5-year-old boy dies from V.R.S.A. bacterial infection. Investigation reveals he was deliberately injected. Theresa Copeland, the father's mistress, is initially suspected and publicly accused. She is later cleared when evidence shifts to Aaron Downing, the father, who embezzled money and planned to flee. Theresa faced workplace exposure, arrest threat, and interrogation based on her affair and proximity to the murder weapon, but was innocent of the murder.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'This is bull, Lennie. Let's book her for murder.' CURTIS: 'You gave notice on your apartment three different times. You got tired of all his promises, and you figured, hey, maybe without the kid around...' BRISCOE: 'He thinks you did. [...] That's 25 years minimum, Theresa. Your boyfriend is selling you out.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'We got a witness puts you in the park the day Ryan was injected.' THERESA: 'What? The closest I ever got to Ryan was when Aaron showed me his picture.' CURTIS: 'The needle you used on the kid was found in your building's trash.' THERESA: 'I didn't put it there. You have to believe me.' [...] BRISCOE: 'Let's book her for murder.' CURTIS: 'Okay. Okay. We stole the money. Me and Aaron. From Foxbury.'
person_rows.notes: Theresa was interrogated at her workplace (Foxbury Insurance) by detectives who accused her of murdering Ryan Downing. She was threatened with arrest and 25 years in prison. The detectives used coercive tactics including claiming her boyfriend was 'selling her out' and that they would 'book her for murder.' Under this pressure, she confessed to embezzlement to prove she had no motive to kill Ryan. She is innocent of murder but partially involved in the embezzlement scheme. The murder accusation exposure occurred at her workplace, affecting her professional standing. She was cleared when evidence shifted to Aaron Downing as the actual murderer.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through deliberation scene
episode_summary.summary: Matt Bergstrom is arrested and pleads guilty to torturing Katharine Lansing and killing a mailman. Later evidence reveals Michelle Ashford's brother committed the crimes at her instigation to eliminate romantic competition. Bergstrom (a serial killer) is proven innocent of these specific crimes but guilty of six other murders he led police to. Creates prosecutorial dilemma: can't let Bergstrom go free, can't convict wrong person.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'Thirty-six hours. You didn't see anybody you knew, you didn't talk to anybody you knew, you didn't call anybody, nobody called you?' // 'I'm not even from here.' // 'We've asked the Seattle police to check you out. What are they gonna tell us?' // 'Look, you have kept me here for five hours. You searched my hotel room, you took my picture without telling me why. You accused me of something I didn't do.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: CARMICHAEL: 'Mr. Ashford has told you he's not your man.' JACK: 'Fine. I'll get an indictment. Murder One. Attempted Murder Two.' Later: 'So my sister hooks up with this Lansing guy... She tells me to get rid of the competition and make it look like some psycho did it.' Michelle's brother (Ashford) confesses he committed the crimes, not Bergstrom.
person_rows.notes: Bergstrom IS a serial killer (6 other murders) but is innocent of the Lansing torture and mailman murder. He accepted a plea deal (Murder Two, 25-to-life) for crimes actually committed by Michelle Ashford's brother. The deal included no extradition to death penalty states for his actual crimes. Exposure includes arrest, interrogation, impending allocution. Though guilty of other crimes, he is innocent of what he's being convicted for—creating the episode's central moral dilemma. Partially_involved status considered but he's genuinely innocent of THESE specific charges.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: CURTIS: 'Well, we hear you're one of those guys who get all bent out of shape when their woman leaves 'em after years of abuse.' Detectives come to his workplace and confront him publicly in front of colleague who asks 'Do you want me to call anybody, Matt?' when they take him away—wrong scene, but shows public nature. For Lansing: 'Roger Lansing?' 'Yes.' 'Can we talk to you?' 'What about?' 'Your wife.' in front of coworker who says 'I'll take this back to the art department.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Scene at workplace: detectives arrive, ask about wife's attack in front of colleague. Mother-in-law Anne says: 'Roger's a bully, he's not a monster.' vs. her insistence 'Don't waste your time on Roger. Where should we look, then?' Cleared when alibis checked: 'Roger Lansing was in Chicago, meeting with a client when the note was left.'
person_rows.notes: Initially prime suspect due to history of domestic violence and restraining order. Questioned publicly at work by detectives. Mother-in-law Anne repeatedly insists he's guilty. First alibi (McKinney) proves false, forcing him to admit he was with prostitutes. Second investigation when threatening note appears also clears him (was in Chicago). Public accusation at workplace qualifies as severity 2 exposure even without arrest.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom arguments; may be truncated at very end during judge's statement.
episode_summary.summary: An embryologist is murdered during a break-in at a fertility clinic. The perpetrator, Francis Curran (a former police detective working as a chauffeur), was hired by Arlene Galvin to destroy frozen embryos from her ex-husband's first marriage. Curran is arrested and confesses, implicating Arlene Galvin. No individuals are wrongfully accused then proven innocent. The legal conflict centers on whether embryos constitute property (affecting felony murder charges) and custody disputes between two ex-wives over frozen embryos.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-testimony
episode_summary.summary: A fire kills a severely disabled boy (Ian Parnell). Initially detectives suspect the store owner (Richie Zweck) for insurance fraud, then the ex-husband (Colin Parnell). Both are cleared. The mother, Megan Parnell, is ultimately charged with murder two for setting the fire. Transcript ends mid-trial during her testimony, so final verdict unknown. Two innocent people (Zweck and Colin) were publicly accused/investigated.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: This woman just lost her son. Shut your mouth and move your car! | ED: Better stick close to Mr. Zweck, there. He's gonna be really upset when he finds out there are people in this world who'd deliberately cost him his lithographs.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Zweck at fire scene in front of neighbors/onlookers, accuse him of insurance fraud arson pattern. ED: 'You had an electric fire a year ago that you claimed a $275,000 loss from. You had a flood the year before to the tune of four hundred grand.' After questioning employee and investigation, detectives pivot to ex-husband.
person_rows.notes: Zweck accused publicly at crime scene based on prior insurance claims. Detectives use intimidation/insults. No formal charges shown, but public exposure at workplace (his store) qualifies as severity 2. Investigation shifts away from him with no apology.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: So what'd you two used to fight about? | BRISCOE: Hey, you didn't have to be thinking about the consequences. You were walking by the building where you used to live, the family that you walked out on, you had the paint thinner to take to your girlfriend's apartment and that Irish guilt kicked in, huh? | ED: Got too much [guilt] to take?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives bring Colin in for interrogation based on neighbor witness saying she saw him at building day before fire. Coercive interrogation about paint thinner, Irish guilt, relationship to son. BRISCOE: 'Somebody set the fire that killed your son. That makes it a homicide.' Colin arrested: 'So wipe your hands. You're going for a ride.' Alibi with girlfriend Lori checks out, confirmed by dinner receipt and witnesses. Investigation shifts to mother.
person_rows.notes: Colin publicly arrested at girlfriend's apartment in front of her. Interrogation uses coercive tactics exploiting guilt about leaving family. Proven innocent via alibi (receipts, witnesses confirm he was at dinner/girlfriend's apartment during fire). Detectives never apologize. Severity 3: arrested, publicly accused of murdering his son, reputation harmed in front of new partner.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated during closing arguments
episode_summary.summary: Stephanie Donnatelli is murdered on a subway platform by Brian Gallant, a mentally ill man released from Riker's without medication or evaluation. The episode focuses on prosecuting Phillip Andrews, CEO of SMJ Medical Services (the HMO managing Riker's medical care), for manslaughter. Dr. Sanders initially emerges as a suspect/responsible party but becomes a witness against Andrews after being threatened with charges. Raffi Lorraine, a homeless man, is initially suspected but proves to be a witness. The transcript cuts off during closing arguments.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'We got you on the subway platform and we got you with the victim's possessions. Now you have somethin' to say? Because the train's about to leave the station.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: ED: 'This yours?' [showing coat] RAFFI: 'Yes, sir.' BRISCOE: 'Buttons from the wrong side, chief.' / Later cleared when he provides eyewitness account: 'This gal comes through the turnstile... and all of a sudden he hits her with a rock!'
person_rows.notes: Homeless man arrested for murder after found with victim's belongings (coat, cross). Detectives threaten him with prosecution. He explains he witnessed the actual murder by Brian Gallant and robbed the body afterward. No public exposure beyond police interrogation, but arrest and coercive interrogation qualify as harm.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: JACK: 'Manslaughter. If he was aware of the danger Gallant posed and recklessly ignored it.' / CARMICHAEL: 'I will ask him to surrender his medical license for a year.' Doctor protests: 'That's not right... He took an oath.' Later testifies against Andrews and reveals: 'I wasn't allowed to have him evaluated! Andrews fired a doctor for making too many referrals.'
person_rows.notes: Doctor was threatened with manslaughter prosecution and forced to surrender medical license for one year. While he did fail in his duties (not referring Gallant for psychiatric evaluation), he was working under threat of termination from Andrews and SMJ's cost-cutting policies. He claims 'I did the best I could' under impossible circumstances. The harm (license revocation, public exposure in court) arguably exceeds his actual culpability given the coercive employment situation. His wrongdoing (not standing up to Andrews) is distinct from the accusation (manslaughter), making this a complex case of partial involvement with disproportionate consequences.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during final courtroom scene.
episode_summary.summary: Natalie Dreisner, a 24-year-old violinist with the Manhattan Symphony Orchestra, is found murdered in her dressing room. Initially, detectives suspect stage hand Jay Brannigan, who stole her $1 million del Gesu violin. However, Brannigan has a solid alibi for the time of murder. The investigation then focuses on conductor Carl Reger, who was having an affair with Dreisner. Reger is arrested and tried for murder based on motive (Dreisner was leaving him professionally and personally), means (his wife's registered gun), and opportunity (alibi issues with E-Z Pass records). Jay Brannigan, though guilty of theft, is cleared of murder and suffered public exposure and arrest for a crime he did not commit.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Okay, man, if you're gonna lie, be creative otherwise we get bored.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Jay Brannigan? Yeah. Who are you? Police. We have a search warrant. Mind if we come in? You going somewhere? I thought I'd go up to Maine for a few days. Do some fishing. I thought you were sick. Well, I'm feeling better. Hey what the hell are you doing? Whoa! Price of worms must've gone through the roof. You're under arrest for the murder of Natalie Dreisner.
person_rows.notes: Jay Brannigan stole the violin but did not commit the murder. He was arrested in his home and charged with murder. His alibi (bartender and friends confirm he was at the Red Parrot from 11 p.m. to closing) clears him of the murder. Though guilty of theft (partially_involved), he was publicly accused and arrested for murder, which he did not commit. Detectives used coercive interrogation tactics suggesting he was lying. The arrest occurred at his home with a search warrant, implying workplace/social exposure as detectives questioned his boss and coworkers earlier.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during Bennett's testimony.
episode_summary.summary: Rafael Alejo, a gang leader, is stabbed to death at Rikers. Detectives initially suspect Hector Diaz, who was also stabbed in the incident. However, Diaz is revealed to be an undercover officer. Investigation shifts to Judd Larson (a lifer who actually committed the murder) and Corrections Officer Peter Bennett, who allegedly arranged the killing to avenge his girlfriend Angela Donahue's rape by Alejo. Diaz was wrongly suspected and publicly questioned/accused before his undercover status was revealed. Transcript cuts off during Bennett's trial testimony.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: 'Okay, Diaz, somebody stabs you and you don't see anything?' ... 'We've got a shank drippin' with blood, we gonna turn up your prints on it?' DIAZ: 'You do and you let me know. Until then, get your black nose out of my ass.' ED: 'Am I gonna have to conduct a race-relations workshop with you? Oh, you just got yourself written up.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: QUINN: 'These idiots came close to blowing two years worth of undercover work.' BRISCOE: 'These idiots? What about you not giving us a heads-up when you knew we were investigating one of your people?' QUINN: 'It was too big a risk and you know it.' BRISCOE: 'Oh, right. It's not like we're cops or anything.'
person_rows.notes: Diaz was working undercover in the Latin Lobos gang for two years, trying to infiltrate a major drug deal. Briscoe and Green's investigation (questioning him aggressively, threatening to write him up, treating him as a murder suspect) nearly exposed his cover. The Gang Unit supervisor Quinn confirms they 'came close to blowing two years worth of undercover work' and were one day away from shutting down the drug delivery operation. Diaz was publicly confronted and threatened while maintaining his cover, creating significant professional harm and operational risk.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-cross-examination of Eli Becker
episode_summary.summary: Murder-for-hire case where Saul Caplan is killed during a staged robbery. Multiple employees investigated before evidence points to Eli Becker, the victim's partner's son, who hired Sal Bonafiglio to arrange the murder. Early suspects include stockroom workers questioned about theft. Transcript ends mid-trial cross-examination.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: When did you take the coat you gave her? ... RONNIE: Last week. ED: You're sure it wasn't last night? RONNIE: Saul was like a second father to me, all right? I mean, I took a coat, yeah. I took a coat. But I don't kill this man. I love this man, okay?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: ED: Where were you last night, Ronnie? RONNIE: Home. ED: Do you have anybody that can confirm that? RONNIE: My wife. My kids. My neighbor. ED: Your wife know about your friend, Jeffrey?
person_rows.notes: Stockroom worker accused because he gave stolen coat to transgender friend Toreador. Detectives confronted him at work about murder and his secret relationship. His alibi checked out. Admitted to theft but cleared of murder. Exposure at workplace in front of family (wife mentioned as confirming alibi).

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: How much did you pay for it? TOREADOR: It was on sale. Drastically reduced, don't you know. ED: Is that what you call murdering the storekeeper? ... ED: You wanna have this conversation here or you wanna have it down at the station?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: What kind of jacket is that, Dolce & Gabbana? TOREADOR: Oh, my. Aren't we something of a fashionista. ED: How much did you pay for it? TOREADOR: It was on sale. Drastically reduced, don't you know. ED: Is that what you call murdering the storekeeper?
person_rows.notes: Transgender sex worker accused of murder based on possessing new leather jacket from Becker & Caplan. Detectives confronted in public outdoor location (by dumpster where relieving self). Threatened with station interrogation for murder. Cleared when coat traced to Ronnie who gave it as gift.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: See, Sal, whoever broke into the store worked in the store. Now, you work there in the stock room, and you have felonies on your record. ... BRISCOE: Hey, maybe you went to rob the place, and you didn't know Saul was gonna be there.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: ED: Where were you Thursday night between 10:00 and 1:00? SAL: I was with my nephew in Uniondale. BRISCOE: Whether this guy's alibi checks out or not, he's involved.
person_rows.notes: EXCLUDED from harm count per rubric: Sal was guilty of arranging the murder (middleman between Eli and Eddie). Though initially accused of committing murder himself (which he didn't), his actual crime (conspiracy/murder-for-hire) makes him 'partially_involved' to the point of excluding from victim count. He cut a deal (Man 1, 15-25 years) to testify against Eli.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Detective Jake Kearsey is shot and killed outside an apartment at 35 St. Nicholas Avenue while attempting to locate a witness (Albert Rosa) for an unrelated robbery case. The shooter is Lateef Miller, a former Black Panther and current community activist, who was inside his girlfriend Selina Watts' apartment. Miller claims self-defense, stating Kearsey pointed a gun at him first after he opened the door. The episode explores police-community tensions and racial issues, with Miller's defense arguing Kearsey was a racist cop who acted aggressively. Miller is tried for Murder in the First Degree. No false suspects are identified - Miller is the only suspect throughout, and the case centers on whether his self-defense claim is valid. While there are discussions of police conduct and racial profiling, no innocent person is wrongly accused of the murder itself.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final courtroom confession scene.
episode_summary.summary: Detectives reopen a 1981 cold case murder of 16-year-old Marybeth Mosley. Initial suspect Fred Jenks (plumber) was cleared by alibi but remained under suspicion for years. Michael Sarno, wealthy ambassador's son with rape history, is eventually arrested. Detective Tommy Brannigan confesses in court that Inspector Holsick pressured him to abandon Sarno investigation in exchange for promotion to First Grade, and arranged for murder weapon to disappear from Property Room. Jenks suffered work/reputation harm from being primary suspect despite solid alibi.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: We sweated him as much as we could. / Frigging cops practically rented a space in my keister.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: JENKS: 'Frigging cops practically rented a space in my keister.' BRANNIGAN: 'We sweated him as much as we could, but we didn't get anywhere.' Jenks was working in the Mosley house at time of murder; had criminal record; wife and Johnny Estrada both provided alibi for time of murder (buying recliner).
person_rows.notes: Despite solid alibi from both wife (ex-wife by time of reopened investigation) and Johnny Estrada, Jenks remained primary suspect. Police conducted intensive, prolonged investigation including interrogations. He explicitly states police 'practically rented a space in my keister' indicating sustained harassment. As plumber working in victim's home, this would have been known to employers/clients, causing work-related reputation harm. Severity 3: sustained public exposure as murder suspect, intensive police pressure, long-term reputation damage in professional context.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony
episode_summary.summary: Russell Crider, a youth hockey coach, is beaten to death in a parking garage. Initially, detectives focus on his teenage player Keith Taylor after finding Keith's hockey stick with the victim's blood. Keith is interrogated, his home searched, and he is ordered by a judge to provide voice samples for a 911 call match. Keith lies to protect his father Ray Taylor, who actually committed the murder in a rage after Keith was suspended from a game. Keith is publicly accused, arrested as an adult for murder, and forced to testify against his father only after a plea deal.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: There's only one question here, Keith. Who's going down for this? Hey, if you made the 911 call, you're the good guy here. You were trying to save your coach's life. Yeah, help yourself out. BRISCOE: He's not saying another word. Your mom's not gonna do the time kid, you are. Unless you get smart, real quick.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Judge: 'Your client is ordered to provide a sample of his voice, under such circumstances as required.' JACK: 'We've decided to charge Keith in the death of Russell Crider as an adult. If he's convicted, he'll do hard time.' Keith's father ultimately confesses: 'He had nothing to do with it. Any of it.'
person_rows.notes: Keith was 16 years old. His DNA/blood evidence on hockey stick was from being at scene as witness, not perpetrator. He attempted to stop his father and made the 911 call to save the victim. Was subjected to police interrogation pressure, court-ordered voice matching, threatened with adult murder charges and hard time. Only cleared when father confessed during plea negotiation to save his son. The prosecution used threat of adult murder conviction to pressure the father into confession. Keith was forced to testify against his father as part of immunity deal.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: You use this on her, too? BOBBY: I didn't use that on nobody. ED: Oh. So, you won't mind if we check it out with Forensics, huh? ED: Maybe you didn't mean to kill him. Maybe it was just an unlucky thing. Is that what it was? BRISCOE: You could lose a lot more than your J-O-B, Bobby. ED: Come on, it's now or never.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: ED: Look, you told us you were here all morning. BOBBY: I hit the john. ED: You were gone over 20 minutes. BRISCOE: Do you remember an assault conviction, Bobby? BOBBY: Oh, come on, man, that was five years ago. Husband-wife thing. ED: You use this on her, too? [Shows nightstick] Bobby's alibi: purchased lottery ticket at 8:29 a.m., verified by receipt.
person_rows.notes: Garage attendant with prior domestic assault conviction. Detectives confiscated his nightstick and subjected him to aggressive interrogation suggesting he killed Crider, using his criminal history and absence from post as evidence. Was accused of leaving post to commit murder/robbery. Cleared by lottery ticket receipt showing he was at deli during murder. Nightstick came back clean from forensics. Fear of job loss mentioned multiple times during interrogation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through mistrial declaration and final scene.
episode_summary.summary: Richard Morriston/Mark Landry murders four people including his girlfriend Gail Churchill after she discovers he's a con man who previously killed another girlfriend in Utah. No innocent persons are publicly accused or harmed by false accusations in this episode. The case focuses on prosecuting the actual perpetrator, who represents himself at trial and manipulates a juror, resulting in a mistrial.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence at end. Mrs. Peavey's conversation with prosecutors is incomplete.
episode_summary.summary: George Peavey, a security guard, breaks into a research lab to free monkeys infected with AIDS virus. One monkey escapes and bites researcher Ronald Lee, who dies from anaphylactic shock. Peavey confesses to the break-in and is charged with manslaughter. His defense argues justification based on animal welfare. His girlfriend Maxine Walden is questioned but Peavey explicitly states she was not involved. No one is falsely accused - Peavey is the actual perpetrator and confesses. The case focuses on whether his actions were justified, not on wrongful accusation.

episode_summary.episode_title: Sunday in the Park with Jorge
episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated during defendant's testimony
episode_summary.summary: Susan Capp is murdered in Central Park during Puerto Rican Day Parade chaos. Police initially pursue suspects from parade wilding incidents, then focus on Seth Teitel (victim's husband's business partner) who had financial motive. Seth is arrested and tried, but new witness testimony leads to charges against Nestor Salazar, who actually committed the murder. Seth is exonerated after public trial.

person_rows.episode_title: Sunday in the Park with Jorge
person_rows.quote_or_scene: JACK: 'I did apologize. And it was sincere.' [after Seth is exonerated when real killer identified]
person_rows.notes: Seth Teitel was vice president of victim's husband's company. Had clear financial motive (company dissolution in divorce would destroy his stock options). Was at crime scene and initially lied about it. Was arrested, indicted, and tried for murder. Girlfriend testified against him in public trial. Political pressure from City Hall to convict someone from the wilding incidents complicated case. Prosecution later discovered new witness (Kiki Morales, then David Stenopolis) who identified actual killer Nestor Salazar. McCoy formally apologized. Seth endured full public trial before exoneration.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during McCoy's closing argument
episode_summary.summary: Chinese restaurant owner Thomas Ngai is murdered during a food delivery to a vacant apartment. Homeless veteran Marvin Warner is initially suspected based on his blanket at the scene and history of confrontations at that location. Warner is cleared when he explains teenagers forced him out and took his blanket. Investigation reveals five teenagers (Mitch Regan, Peter Franco, Chris Donnels, Nick Simms, Heather Russo) lured Ngai to rob him of food, then murdered him when he saw their faces. Warner was publicly accused and arrested before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Briscoe: 'Here comes Sergeant Slaughter.' Ed: 'Tell him what he's won. You've just won a fabulous time-share upstate.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'Marvin Warner?' [arrest scene] WARNER: 'I didn't rob anyone and I didn't kill anyone.' BRISCOE: 'Well, we got a witness who says you're a pretty belligerent guy, Marvin.' ... WARNER: 'You want to know about my blanket? Well, how about you just ask those kids? The kids that kicked me off of the landing.'
person_rows.notes: Warner was arrested based on his blanket being found at scene and prior conflicts at location. Cleared when he explained teenagers took his blanket and witnesses confirmed seeing teenagers. While arrested publicly (sarcastic 'prize patrol' comments), there's no evidence of broader public exposure beyond police interaction. Borderline inclusion - arrested but appears contained to police custody without work/family/media exposure. However, the public arrest with mocking treatment qualifies as severity 2.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony but may be truncated before final verdict
episode_summary.summary: A gay man (Bradford Mandel) is beaten to death and his adopted son kidnapped by the biological father (Robert Kelley) and mother (Celia Goddard). The biological father is prosecuted for murder; the biological mother cooperates and testifies against him. No false suspects are identified or accused - police quickly identify the correct perpetrators through evidence at the scene and ransom call tracking. Gene Burdick opportunistically made a fake ransom call but was not wrongly accused of the murder/kidnapping itself - he admitted to the ransom scam. The case centers on the murder prosecution of the actual guilty party.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony
episode_summary.summary: Dena Meredith, a pregnant bank loan officer, is found shot in the trunk of her stolen car. Two carjackers (Gabor Hartunian and Mark) are initially suspected but are later proven innocent—they stole the car after she was already shot and in the trunk. The real perpetrators are Marcus Cole (hired killer), Henry Williams (coordinator), and Chris Cody (NBA player who fathered Dena's baby and ordered the hit to avoid child support). The carjackers faced public accusation and arrest before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You should've made sure she was dead. Now she's gonna be able to identify you. Yeah, not to mention the couple you rear-ended. You're gonna be as old as they are by the time you get out.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: Where were you Saturday night? GABOR: Home. ED: This is before or after you took the Camry? GABOR: I don't know about Camry. [...] ED: Did you know that woman was pregnant? GABOR: What woman? [...] No, no woman. Just car. [...] No shoot no woman. [...] I swear on my father's grave. [...] The car was in a lot on the west side. [...] Twelfth Avenue and 66th Street. [...] Around 10:30. We broke into the car and drive it off the lot. That's it.
person_rows.notes: Gabor and his partner Mark stole the car around 10:30 PM from a parking lot, unaware Dena was already shot and in the trunk (shooting occurred around 8 PM). They were arrested and interrogated with threats about the murder and the pregnant victim. Search warrants executed on their homes. Forensics later proved their prints were only inside the car, not near the trunk, clearing them. Exposure occurred during arrest and interrogation in what appears to be a workplace setting (auto parts operation).

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You should've made sure she was dead. Now she's gonna be able to identify you.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: MARK: Whoa, whoa. Nobody shoot no one. [...] The car was in a lot on the west side. [...] We broke into the car and drive it off the lot. That's it. And then he hit that other car. We never even looked in the trunk.
person_rows.notes: Mark was arrested alongside Gabor. Both were subjected to the same threatening interrogation tactics and search warrants. Forensics confirmed they had no involvement with the trunk or the shooting. They were car thieves who happened to steal a car that already contained a dying woman, making them initial suspects in a murder they didn't commit.

episode_summary.episode_title: Swept Away - A Very Special Episode
episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony. Reality TV show 'Deal With It' cast member Wes Tatum dies after rooftop fight with Paul Wyler. Network VP Byron Stark charged with manslaughter for inciting the confrontation.
episode_summary.summary: Wesley Tatum dies after being pushed off a roof during a fight with castmate Paul Wyler. Wyler is initially arrested for murder, but dying declaration reveals Wes was told to provoke the fight by network executive Byron Stark for ratings. Wyler becomes prosecution witness against Stark. The show's executive producers (Kaufmans) were also arrested but appear to have cooperated, implicating Stark as the one who orchestrated the confrontation.

person_rows.episode_title: Swept Away - A Very Special Episode
person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, indicted, tried as murder defendant, later becomes prosecution witness after dying declaration reveals setup
person_rows.quote_or_scene: ED: 'Paul Wyler, you're under arrest for the murder of Wesley Tatum.' Later, dying declaration testimony: 'He said, "What'd you do that for, man? Didn't you talk to 'em?" ...Then he said, "It was an act."'
person_rows.notes: Paul was manipulated into a situation where he reacted violently to staged provocation. Wes's dying declaration ('It was an act') proves Paul was an unwitting participant in a setup orchestrated by network executive Byron Stark for ratings. Paul was arrested, charged with murder, and put on trial before the truth emerged. He ultimately testified against Stark but suffered massive legal/reputational harm first.

person_rows.episode_title: Swept Away - A Very Special Episode
person_rows.consequence_detail: identified by castmates as having 'problem with Wes,' questioned as suspect, had alibi (poetry reading on tape)
person_rows.quote_or_scene: TRENT: 'Jeremy might have had a problem with Wes.' ED: 'So, uh, where does Jeremy go when he wants to defy authority?' Later Jeremy states: 'I was at a poetry reading, Saint Mark's Church... it's on tape, fellows.'
person_rows.notes: Jeremy was pointed out by castmates as having conflicts with Wes and was questioned as a suspect. However, he had a solid alibi (poetry reading captured on tape by show's camera crew). No evidence of public exposure beyond police questioning. Severity = 1 because questioning remained private with no material consequences shown.

person_rows.episode_title: Swept Away - A Very Special Episode
person_rows.consequence_detail: publicly confronted by police in front of friends, threatened with murder charge, coerced into confession about camera theft and drug dealing
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: 'That's cool, bro, you just better hope that a jury agrees with you.' ED: 'Look, man, I wasn't at the loft on Monday.' BRISCOE: 'Where were you?' Response: 'Selling pot, man.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: ED: 'Come on, step away from your friend a minute.' Friend: 'I'm watching for police brutality, you put a hand on him.' Later: 'Look, man, I wasn't at the loft on Monday... Selling pot, man.'
person_rows.notes: Aaron was publicly confronted in front of friends, accused of murder based on prior theft of camera and connection to Wes. Police used verbal threats about life imprisonment to coerce admissions about drug dealing. Had alibi (witnesses placed him in Tompkins Square selling drugs). Severity = 2 because confrontation was public (friends present) but he was not arrested/charged for murder.

person_rows.episode_title: Swept Away - A Very Special Episode
person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested publicly at work during taping, charges later appear to have been dropped when they cooperated against Stark
person_rows.quote_or_scene: COREY: 'I don't know, why don't we just trim the headshot and go directly to the kitchen?' CARMICHAEL: 'You're both under arrest for the murder of Wesley Tatum.' COREY: 'You can't arrest us. We have a show to finish.' CARMICHAEL: 'Consider yourself canceled.'
person_rows.notes: The Kaufmans were arrested publicly during a taping session in front of their crew. They were initially charged with murder but later cooperated with prosecution, testifying that Byron Stark (not them) orchestrated the rooftop confrontation. Melanie's testimony against Stark suggests charges were dropped. Severity = 3 for arrest and public work exposure, though ultimate outcome appears to have been cooperation deal rather than conviction.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence at end
episode_summary.summary: Angela Jarrell is murdered; investigation leads to drug dealer Francis Partell (aka 'Taz'). Detectives discover Partell may have committed a 1998 Bronx murder for which Tony Shaeffer is serving 25-to-life. Three witnesses (Verona, Pistone, Quintana) now identify Partell as the real shooter. Shaeffer was convicted based on two eyewitnesses, his 'confession' to girlfriend Valerie Mankowitz (bragging to impress her), and his mother's alibi being dismissed. Manhattan DA prosecutes Partell for the Bronx murder using jurisdictional exception. Transcript ends mid-negotiation; Tony Shaeffer remains wrongfully imprisoned throughout visible transcript.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney: 'From the day I was assigned to the case' [believed Tony was innocent]. Shaeffer: 'I did not kill that man, Mr. McCoy.' Three new witnesses identify Partell as the actual shooter; Shaeffer passed polygraph; made false 'confession' to girlfriend to impress her.
person_rows.notes: Shaeffer is serving 25-to-life for a murder committed by Partell. Convicted on: (1) two eyewitness IDs later contradicted by three witnesses; (2) 'confession' to girlfriend Valerie that was bragging to impress her ('Tony was a wimp'); (3) mother's alibi dismissed as biased. Third witness Peter Verona told police at time that Shaeffer WASN'T the shooter but was ignored. Anonymous Crime Stoppers tip naming 'Taz' was never followed up by Detective Boyle. Bronx DA Robertson refuses to reopen case. By end of transcript, Shaeffer remains imprisoned; Manhattan is prosecuting Partell but transcript cuts off before resolution.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Ed: 'Maybe you'd be more comfortable talking down at the precinct.' Briscoe asks if he bought ecstasy: 'I get tested here, all right.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives question Gibson at his workplace about buying drinks for Angela Jarrell and possibly ecstasy. Gibson protests: 'I get tested here' (drug testing at work). Alibi confirmed with doorman; cleared immediately.
person_rows.notes: Gibson was last known person with Angela at the Zircon lounge. Detectives show up at his workplace, question him about drug purchase in front of colleagues (he mentions workplace drug testing). Alibi checks out (doorman confirms 1:00-1:15 AM arrival). Exposure was workplace confrontation but quickly cleared. Included because public accusation at work constitutes severity 2 harm even though resolution was swift.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during cross-examination of Alice Marner
episode_summary.summary: Karen Hall, an investigator for the NY Attorney General's office, is murdered. Initial investigation by state troopers focuses on abduction from Albany train station based on weak eyewitness. NYPD discovers she was having affair with her supervisor Alec Conroy. Multiple people publicly accused/investigated before Conroy arrested. Alice Marner (Conroy's other mistress) provides false alibi, later testifies against him. Defense attorney suggests Marner herself committed the murder. Transcript cuts off during trial.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Women. Can't live with 'em, can't kill 'em, huh, Nate?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: RICHARDS: Like I told the guy from the A.G.'s office. I wouldn't know Karen Hall if I fell over the broad... BRISCOE: Well, can you account for your whereabouts on the night of the February 19th? RICHARDS: Yeah. Where every good defendant is supposed to be. In my lawyer's office.
person_rows.notes: Richards was already serving time on fraud charges. Detectives questioned him at prison facility about Hall murder based on his relationship with witness Angela Cofresta and domestic violence history. Has alibi (with lawyer). Publicly accused during prison interview but harm limited as already incarcerated.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: STAN: You met him at the apartment that he had rented for the two of you. Only you weren't invited. And when you showed up to see your lover of 12 years in the arms of this young girl... STAN: Your late husband owned a .22 caliber handgun, did he not, Mrs. Marner?... STAN: You're the one who killed that woman that night, aren't you? ALICE: No!
person_rows.notes: Marner initially provided false alibi for Conroy, then became prosecution witness. Defense attorney publicly accused her of the murder during cross-examination in open court, suggesting jealousy motive. She denies it. Transcript cuts off so ultimate resolution unclear, but prosecution clearly proceeded against Conroy (not Marner), strongly implying defense theory rejected. Public courtroom accusation constitutes exposure and harm even without arrest.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony but may be cut off at the end during redirect examination.
episode_summary.summary: Daniel and Rosa Alvarez are murdered during a cocaine deal. Investigation reveals Caryn Wyman, an Army major's wife, smuggled cocaine from Colombia using military flights. She testifies against Joe Pena, who threatened her and killed the Alvarezes. Two building witnesses (doorman Conners and resident Brady) initially identify Pena but recant at trial, likely due to intimidation. However, neither witness was falsely accused themselves—they were intimidated out of testifying truthfully. Caryn Wyman was prosecuted but received probation in exchange for testimony. No person in this episode qualifies as being falsely accused and then proven/strongly implied innocent with public exposure or material harm as a consequence of that accusation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony
episode_summary.summary: Episode centers on a staged car accident insurance fraud ring. Hector Santiago dies from injuries sustained in a staged accident orchestrated by attorneys Petrie and Sanders, chiropractor Dr. Raleigh, and moving company owner Bill Reed. The investigation reveals a conspiracy where illegal immigrants were recruited as crash victims to generate fraudulent insurance claims. All defendants are prosecuted; no one falsely accused is later cleared. Mrs. Ndabe testifies about her husband's death in a similar staged accident.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated at end during cross-examination of Jessie Lucas
episode_summary.summary: Scott Wilder is found shot dead at Wilshire School. Investigation initially focuses on homeless shelter residents, then drug connections. Detectives discover Scott was involved in extortion scheme with Jessie Lucas, who believed Peter Wilder was her father. Kinship analysis proves Peter wasn't her father. Jessie testifies that she backed out of meeting but Scott went ahead alone. Evidence (missing gun, red dust on shoes, $250K withdrawal) points to Peter Wilder killing his own son. Transcript ends mid-testimony at preliminary hearing.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: Tell us what you did with the gun. / GREG: I told you, kid gave me a cigarette. I didn't have any gun. / BRISCOE: What time was that? / GREG: Look, I'm out after curfew, I could lose my room. / ED: You could lose a whole lot more than that if you don't tell us the truth.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: His fingerprints were found on cigarette pack in victim's pocket. Detectives threaten him with losing his housing and 'a whole lot more' to pressure confession. He explains he only bummed a cigarette and witnessed argument between Scott and a girl.
person_rows.notes: Greg is threatened with loss of housing and worse consequences during interrogation despite being innocent. His fingerprints on cigarettes were from bumming a smoke, not murder. He becomes a witness, not perpetrator. Exposure remained with police only, no public outing, but coercive tactics qualify as harm.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: What happened, he rip you off? Is that it? / BRISCOE: You know what I think? I think he got you pregnant, and that he didn't want the baby. / ED: Or maybe he didn't want you. I mean, he's sleeping with you. He's living with another woman. That couldn't make you feel good. / ED: Then you find out that he won't pay, only you know about his family. You know that he comes from money. / ED: What was that, a shakedown?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Witness places her arguing with Scott at crime scene. Detectives interrogate her in front of her mother with aggressive theories about pregnancy, jealousy, shakedown. She's arrested at home for extortion (not murder initially). Later testifies at preliminary hearing that she backed out and Scott went alone to meet Peter Wilder.
person_rows.notes: Jessie is accused of murder based on witness placing her at scene and drug buy involvement. Subjected to aggressive interrogation in front of mother with false theories about romantic relationship with Scott (actually her half-brother). Arrested at home for extortion, creating family exposure. Murder charge appears to shift away from her toward Peter Wilder as evidence develops. Her testimony at preliminary hearing suggests she's now prosecution witness against Wilder rather than defendant.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through investigation revealing Donald Lonegan killed Malloy and Cally killed witness Waziri
episode_summary.summary: James Malloy is murdered at a driving range. Investigation reveals he was business partners with gangster Cally Lonegan and was dating Cally's niece Margaret, who died of a drug overdose from drugs Malloy provided. Cab driver Sebastian Waziri witnesses someone leaving Malloy's murder scene and is later killed. The case reveals Cally is an FBI informant protected by agents Innes and Washington. Ultimately, evidence shows Donald Lonegan (Cally's brother, Margaret's father) killed Malloy to avenge his daughter's death, while Cally killed witness Waziri to protect his brother. No one proven/strongly implied innocent is publicly exposed or harmed by false accusation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through arraignment and pretrial phases
episode_summary.summary: School shooting at George Mason High kills 4, wounds 11. Police pursue multiple false leads before finding actual shooter Henry Semple. Howard Earl, a Black student, is publicly arrested and perp-walked based on witness tip, then cleared via alibi (was visiting mother in prison). Case against Semple later dismissed on Fourth Amendment grounds when gun evidence suppressed.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'Watch your head.' [Putting Howard in car] ... 'We got company.' ED: 'I see 'em.' BRISCOE: 'Come on, move 'em back, Officer. Come on, out of the way.' ... One of my ex-wives called this morning to bust my chops.' ED: 'Which one?' BRISCOE: 'I forget. God, he really does look like he's eight years old.' ... SHAMBALA ADAMS: 'Should've checked his alibi, officers. He was with his grandmother. They were visiting Howard's mother up at Bedford Hills at the time of the shootings. He was literally under lock and key. I'll have the prison fax you the sign-in sheet.'
person_rows.notes: Howard Earl, described as a 16-year-old Black student with prior weapons violation (box cutter), was publicly arrested in his neighborhood with media present after classmate tip. Detectives acknowledge 'he really does look like he's eight years old.' Attorney Shambala Adams produces ironclad alibi (prison sign-in sheet). Despite being released, no apology shown. Public exposure in his own community with photographers present clearly caused reputational harm. Severity 3: arrest + perp walk + media coverage in his neighborhood.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony but cuts off mid-sentence during final discussion
episode_summary.summary: Judge Linda Karlin survives assassination attempt by Frank Morris (killed by detective). Investigation reveals Morris visited Rikers inmate Randall Wylie before attack. Wylie charged with attempted murder based on jailhouse informant Jimmy McGowan's testimony that Wylie solicited the hit. Multiple innocent suspects publicly investigated before focusing on Wylie: Evan Bruce (threatening note writer), Vince Ellis (gun owner). Wylie claims entrapment by McGowan. Transcript ends mid-trial, ultimate verdict unknown.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: 'Man, we don't know who you keep company with.' / BRISCOE: 'Well, maybe you better hang on to that lawyer cause we're gonna send this to the D.A. and see if they want to prosecute.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: 'Somebody sent Judge Karlin this little love letter. Whoever wrote it called her a stank ass bitch.' / ED: 'We want you to write it for us.' / EVAN: 'Look, I was just letting go of my anger from being in there so long.' / ED: 'Is this guy a friend of yours?' [showing Morris photo] / EVAN: 'Does he look like a friend of mine?' / EVAN: 'But why I'm gonna kill her? My lawyer beat the case on a speedy trial motion. Ain't nothing she can do to me now.'
person_rows.notes: Bruce wrote threatening note to Judge Karlin from jail but his case was already dismissed on speedy trial motion. Detectives threaten prosecution for the threatening note itself. Cleared when they find actual shooter Morris. No public exposure - interrogation stayed with police.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: 'They found your gun in the shooter's hand.' / BRISCOE: 'You heard about this judge who got shot at in the city? One where the shooter got killed? What about it? They found your gun in the shooter's hand.' / ED: 'Who is it? You know, we could charge you right now as an accomplice, Mr. Ellis.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Gun registry traces to Ellis. Detectives confront him at work. BRISCOE: 'You Vince Ellis?' ELLIS: 'Far as I know.' ED: 'We got your name off the state handgun registry. You bought a .38 about a year ago?' ELLIS: 'Yeah, but I don't got it no more. It was stolen.' / Later identifies nephew Frank Morris as thief: 'It's my nephew, Frankie. Frank Morris. Asked if he could use my .38. Said he'd give me 200 just to borrow it. I told him no way. Next night I come in and it's gone.'
person_rows.notes: Gun traced to Ellis through state registry. Detectives confront him at his workplace, threaten accomplice charges. Cleared when he identifies nephew Morris as the one who stole gun. Public exposure at work when detectives questioned him there about murder weapon.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom proceedings about ballot counting motion.
episode_summary.summary: Martha Krasner is shot and killed in what initially appears to be road rage. Investigation reveals she was killed by mistake - the intended target was investigative journalist Kate Pierce, who was working on a story about election fraud in a state senate race. The killer, Martin Blatt, was hired by Albert Bonatto on behalf of State Senator Anne Benton to prevent Pierce from exposing a scheme to rig voting machines and steal ballots. Multiple innocent people are investigated before the true conspiracy is uncovered.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BRISCOE: How'd you find out we were looking for you? / Uh, on TV. That bag lady. / I was parked on the corner, like she said. / And you got into the car and... / It was a tight spot, you know, and I just got the car. So I'm trying to pull out, and I guess I was going kind of slow or something.
person_rows.notes: Simon came in voluntarily after seeing himself described on TV. Detectives quickly cleared him when they realized the shooter was on the victim's left side, not right where Simon was parked. No public exposure beyond the police interview.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BRISCOE: Where were you Monday morning, Mr. Krasner? / Teaching at PS 124, and I have 30 ten-year-olds who can vouch for me. / Where was your car? / On the street. / Mr. Krasner, is it possible that your wife was seeing someone else?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: The husband drives a green mid-size sedan. / Yeah, and the shooter really wanted her dead. He used a dum-dum bullet. / Maybe we should round those bases again.
person_rows.notes: Detectives pursued the husband as a suspect based on: (1) his green car matching witness description, (2) the professional nature of the hit (Black Talon bullets suggesting premeditation), and (3) marital disagreements mentioned. He was questioned about his whereabouts and possible marital infidelity. Though confined to police interaction, the questions about affairs and the implicit accusation constitute exposure with severity 2.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: Then how come you hit her up for seven grand? / It was business. / Did you, uh, ever throw Martha any connections downtown in Tribeca?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: JEFF: It was a dynamite business plan. / What was your role in this dot-com? / I gave her an office, got her some meetings, helped with the first round of financing... / And, uh, who gets the rights to this dynamite business plan now that Martha's dead? / Guys, I don't need to shoot people to get rich. I'm already rich.
person_rows.notes: Waites was investigated as a suspect due to: (1) business partnership that went sour, (2) $7,000 payment from victim shortly before death, and (3) potential inheritance of business rights. Detectives employed aggressive questioning implying he might have killed her for the business. The direct accusatory questioning ('who gets the rights now that Martha's dead?') constitutes severity 2 exposure even though confined to police.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: We think your car may have been involved in a homicide. Now, if you have any idea who might have stolen it, please tell us.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: MENDEL: Nice they send you all the way from Manhattan. That where they found it? / Yeah, it was seen there two days ago. / What do you mean, seen? Seen by who? / Why don't you just start by telling us where it was stolen from?
person_rows.notes: Rabbi Mendel owned the green Buick Skylark used in the murder. He reported it stolen and was questioned when it was connected to the homicide. While technically not accused of the murder itself, the detectives' visit and questions about the car being involved in a homicide put him in an uncomfortable position. Severity 1 as it remained private with no formal accusation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial portion; includes defense objection to Roger Ellery testimony
episode_summary.summary: Kevin Seleeby, a professional baseball player, is arrested and tried for murdering his limo driver Norman Pratt. Two accomplices (agent Martin Stanley and cousin David Arkuss) testify against Seleeby in exchange for deals. Defense argues steroid-induced psychosis. Prosecution's motive theory (blackmail over steroid use) is blocked; actual motive (blackmail over Seleeby being gay) is also blocked due to attorney work-product privilege. No innocent persons are falsely accused then later cleared in this episode. The investigation quickly identifies Seleeby as the perpetrator through witness testimony and evidence, and he remains the defendant throughout.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final courtroom scene and partial final dialogue.
