episode_summary.summary: Five-year-old Jennifer Clark is kidnapped from her mother's car. Detectives initially suspect the father Jimmy Clark due to custody/child support disputes. He is publicly confronted at his workplace and interrogated but proves innocent. The actual kidnapper, Dwight Jacobs, is arrested and uses the child's life to extort his freedom. Jennifer is ultimately recovered alive.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detective to Jimmy Clark: 'Somebody snatched your kid. Now, you make us believe you had nothing to do with it.' Context: Detectives chased him down at work, tackled him publicly ('Get up, get up, get up'), then interrogated him aggressively about his daughter's kidnapping.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: GREEN: 'Two o'clock today, where were you?' JIMMY: 'Right here. Been working since noon.' GREEN: 'Your boss will vouch for that?' JIMMY: 'Check my timecard.' GREEN: 'Do you know why we're here?' JIMMY: 'The drunk driving warrant. I skipped my court date, right?' GREEN: 'Oh, man, you're not even close. We're here about your daughter.' JIMMY: 'Oh, come on, I'm gonna pay her the money. Denise is pissed she missed a sale at Filene's.' GREEN shows photo. FONTANA: 'She was abducted four hours ago. Somebody snatched your kid. Now, you make us believe you had nothing to do with it.' JIMMY: 'What? No way. Denise says you two have been having issues.' JIMMY: 'I would never do something like this.' GREEN: 'She's been hassling you for money!' JIMMY: 'Look man, I'm just short on cash. Where's my kid? What happened?'
person_rows.notes: Jimmy Clark was behind on child support and had been denied visitation by his ex-wife Denise. Detectives suspected him based on custody dispute and financial motive. He was chased down and tackled at his construction site workplace in front of colleagues, then aggressively interrogated. His timecard and boss confirmed his alibi. The detectives' assessment afterward: 'A deadbeat, but we don't think he snatched the kid.' No apology shown. Exposure was public (coworkers witnessed the takedown) and the interrogation was coercive, qualifying as severity 2.

episode_summary.source_notes: Complex con scheme involving fake shooting, followed by real murder; mother-daughter team of con artists
episode_summary.summary: Patrick Sullivan is found murdered after a staged con. Initially investigators pursue David Glass as a witness to the con, but Glass is later found dead (suicide). Lorraine Dillon confesses to killing Sullivan in self-defense, claiming he molested her daughter April. April later testifies against Lorraine, claiming Lorraine killed Sullivan out of jealousy. Both women appear to have manipulated the system, with April ultimately revealed as having killed her own infant years earlier. The episode centers on who really killed Sullivan and reveals layers of deception between mother and daughter.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: April testifies in court: 'Patrick Sullivan and I were lovers... It was consensual. It was always consensual... She killed Patrick out of jealousy... She's my mother. What was I supposed to do?' Then later: 'I told them you confessed to me about murdering the baby... When I testify, the jury's going to love it.'
person_rows.notes: Lorraine is convicted of murdering Patrick Sullivan. However, the episode strongly suggests April actually killed Sullivan or was the primary actor. April manipulates the trial, first supporting her mother's self-defense claim (molestation story), then flipping to accuse Lorraine of jealousy killing. In the final scene, April's willingness to testify against Lorraine regarding the baby murder (which April actually committed) reveals her pattern of manipulation. Lorraine's statement 'You've got the wrong one' and April's demonstration of her capacity for murderous deception both point to Lorraine being wrongly convicted. The episode ends with April getting a deal by falsely implicating Lorraine in the baby's death, showing she'll sacrifice her mother repeatedly.

episode_summary.source_notes: Original investigation 10 years prior to current episode; Rob Dolan wrongfully suspected of killing daughter Sara; Johnny Zona eventually convicted.
episode_summary.summary: Rob Dolan was the prime suspect in his 12-year-old daughter Sara's rape and murder 10 years ago. Despite no arrest, he faced intensive police investigation, public suspicion, hired a lawyer immediately, and endured a decade of being viewed as having killed his own child. His wife Amy eventually died (implied stress-related). The real killer, Johnny Zona, is eventually identified via deathbed confession of accomplice Dan Flood. Dolan testifies at trial, finally vindicating himself.

person_rows.person_label: Rob Dolan (father of victim Sara Dolan)
person_rows.quote_or_scene: DOLAN: 'Are you sure? After more than 10 years? You were trying to impede the investigation. Since you were the prime suspect.' ... 'I was. But that's not my fault.' HELLER: 'Really? Whose is it?' DOLAN: 'The lying, press hungry detectives in charge of the investigation.' ... DOLAN to McCoy: 'I come all the way down here, pick out the son of a bitch who killed Sara, and now you don't want me to testify?' ... 'my so-called friends continue to assume I raped and murdered my daughter.' ... DOLAN on stand: 'When that man strangled Sara, he murdered my daughter, my wife and me. I may still be breathing, but I've been dead for 10 years, three weeks and four days.'
person_rows.notes: Dolan was never formally arrested or indicted, but was the 'prime suspect' for 10 years in his daughter's rape-murder. The case received massive media attention ('couldn't go five minutes without reading about her'). He hired a lawyer immediately, which detectives interpreted as guilt. He was publicly suspected by police, press, and community. His wife Amy died the day after Zona's indictment (heavily implied to be stress/grief-related from the decade of suspicion). Dolan describes himself as 'dead for 10 years' due to the accusation. McCoy offers partial apology by saying 'I'd like to say that I'm really sorry' at end. Fontana's final scene shows regret/discomfort, suggesting partial acknowledgment. Severity 4 due to wife's death, decade-long destroyed reputation, and life-altering consequences of being publicly suspected of murdering and raping his own child.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through the verdict and final scene.
episode_summary.summary: Robert Barrows is killed by a car bomb while trying to remove his comatose wife Karen's feeding tube. The investigation leads to Walter Byrd (bomb maker), Reverend Harlan Dwyre (who orchestrated the murder), and the Lamar family (Karen's parents and brother Steven). Steven Lamar planted the bomb. All suspects are actually guilty of involvement in the murder conspiracy. No innocent people were falsely accused with public exposure or material harm. The jury convicts Steven Lamar but deadlocks on Reverend Dwyre (mistrial).

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Teresa Richter, an aspiring journalist, is murdered while undercover investigating L-12 gang activities. Initially, gang members Beto Torres and Dolores (Lola) are investigated. Kevin Drucker, a businessman, is arrested and convicted for the murder. His son Patterson was incarcerated with L-12 gang members who coerced Kevin into killing Teresa by threatening Patterson's life. Diablo Morales, L-12 lieutenant, is later prosecuted as the one who ordered the hit through Kevin. Lola is investigated but never charged. Patterson Drucker, while involved in contraband smuggling, was a victim of coercion and ultimately released.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Girl, you racked up quite a record before you were 18. Maybe now you're trying to turn things around. But I'm going to tell you, you go down for a conspiracy murder charge, you won't hold your son again until he's 28.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives threaten her with conspiracy murder charges during interrogation. Later dialogue reveals she panicked and called Diablo (not Beto) when she learned Teresa was a journalist, but no charges are filed. DA later says 'We don't even have enough to prosecute her.'
person_rows.notes: Lola was interrogated under threat of losing her son to Child Services and facing conspiracy murder charges. She called Diablo about Teresa being a journalist, which led to the murder order, but she did not order or participate in the killing. The show pivots away from her as a suspect; DA explicitly states insufficient evidence to prosecute. No public exposure occurred; questioning was contained to police interrogation room.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Here's how it works, Alberto. You give us whoever shot this girl in the back three times, we take him in and that's that. You play games, we run Lola through the system and good luck getting your kid back from Child Services.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives visit Beto in prison, accuse him of ordering the hit. He denies: 'No way I green-lit this girl. You think I need this kind of heat when I'm about to walk out of here?' Later revealed Diablo ordered the hit without Beto's knowledge because the child Beto thought was his was actually Diablo's.
person_rows.notes: Beto was the initial theory as gang leader whose girlfriend (Lola) vouched for Teresa. Detectives assumed he ordered the hit. However, it's later revealed Diablo acted independently because Lola's son was actually Diablo's child, not Beto's. Beto explicitly denies involvement and notes he's up for parole, wouldn't want this heat. Interrogation contained to prison; no public exposure beyond that setting. The 'wrongful conviction' tag applies because the false accusation potentially impacts his parole eligibility.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Your father's smuggling them in to you. Contraband for L-12. In exchange for protection? Doesn't seem to be working. You've been in the infirmary three times in the last six months.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Patterson in prison, suggest he's complicit in the murder conspiracy. Patterson responds: 'My dad's got his problems, I got mine.' Later, DA McCoy states: 'We won't prosecute your son for conspiracy. He'll be eligible for parole in two years.' Patterson is ultimately released after his father testifies.
person_rows.notes: Patterson was accused of conspiracy to murder based on his involvement in smuggling contraband cell phones that facilitated the murder plot. However, he was a victim of gang coercion (assaulted multiple times) and his father was acting under duress to protect him. DA explicitly chose not to prosecute him for conspiracy and arranged his release. While the accusation occurred in prison (not fully public), the threat of conspiracy murder charges and interrogation constitutes exposure severity 2. He was already incarcerated for an unrelated drug offense.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode involves a nurse performing unauthorized sterilizations on young women, one of whom dies. Primary focus is on the nurse's culpability, but episode explores multiple victims of false/coerced medical procedures.
episode_summary.summary: A street vendor, Sam Register, is murdered. Detectives initially pursue Traci Sands, a 16-year-old with a criminal record, as the primary suspect based on witness testimony and physical evidence. Traci dies in custody from complications of an unauthorized sterilization procedure. The case pivots to prosecuting Nurse Gloria Rhodes for Traci's death. Multiple other young women are revealed as victims of Rhodes' unauthorized sterilizations, performed without their knowledge or consent.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney Robinette cross-examines: 'Convicted of solicitation. How many times?' 'Four.' 'Have you ever had gonorrhea?' Isabella is forced to publicly admit her criminal and medical history in open court.
person_rows.notes: Isabella Perez was a victim of unauthorized sterilization by Nurse Rhodes, not accused of the murder. However, she was publicly humiliated in court when her criminal history (solicitation convictions, STD history) was exposed during cross-examination as part of the defense strategy. This represents harm from public exposure of past accusations/convictions in service of discrediting her as a witness. While not a 'false accusation' in the traditional sense, her past arrests are used to publicly shame her. Including for completeness as she suffered reputational harm from exposure of old accusations.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-testimony during cross-examination of Arlene Tarrington
episode_summary.summary: Chelsea Hagerty is murdered and her newborn stolen. Initially, her boyfriend Steve Monroe and ex Frank Stoll are investigated. Arlene Tarrington, who kidnapped the baby, is arrested for murder. She later claims Frank Stoll committed the murder as part of a baby-selling scheme. DNA reveals Stoll is the biological father, devastating Monroe who believed the child was his. Arlene testifies against Stoll. The transcript cuts off during cross-examination, making final resolution unclear.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: FONTANA: 'Mr. Monroe, where were you between noon and 2:00 this afternoon?' MONROE: 'What? Wait, wait, wait, listen.' FONTANA: 'The sooner we rule you out, the sooner we can focus on other suspects.' MONROE: 'Rule me out? I didn't kill Chelsea.' GREEN: 'Look, nobody's saying you did. But right now, everybody's a suspect.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives question Monroe about his whereabouts during the murder window, implying he's a suspect. He provides alibi of being at work filing patient intake forms.
person_rows.notes: Monroe is cleared early via alibi. No public exposure occurred - questioning was private at crime scene. He's the victim's partner and believed father of the baby, making him an obvious initial suspect. Later devastated to learn via DNA test that the baby is not his biological child, but this harm is unrelated to the murder accusation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: GREEN: 'If you were involved in this murder and this kidnapping, you're gonna wanna come off that position.' STOLL: 'Man, you're way off base.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: McCoy develops theory that Stoll conspired with Arlene to sell the baby and murdered Chelsea. Arlene testifies: 'Frank came running out of the apartment in a panic. He said something had gone wrong... Chelsea was lying on the floor, and there was blood all around her head.' Stoll was arraigned and remanded to Rikers.
person_rows.notes: Stoll admits to being at the apartment during the murder timeframe and to being the baby's biological father. He admits planning to sell the baby but denies the murder. Arlene Tarrington takes a plea deal and testifies that Stoll committed the murder, but her credibility is questionable given her own criminal involvement and insanity defense. The prosecution's case relies heavily on her testimony. Transcript cuts off during cross-examination before verdict, but the episode structure suggests Arlene may be the actual killer and is framing Stoll. However, definitive innocence cannot be confirmed from truncated transcript. Marking as 'strongly_implied_innocent' based on narrative trajectory suggesting Arlene is unreliable.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Trucking company owner Clayton Stack is murdered. Investigation reveals he was smuggling illegal immigrants and caused the deaths of 12 people when his refrigerated trailer malfunctioned. Lester Wilkes, a former employee Stack fired, is initially suspected but cleared. The case ultimately focuses on Nathaniel Prentiss, a member of the anti-immigration Countrymen Borderwatch group, who killed Stack. Terry Dorn, another Countryman, is revealed to have sabotaged Stack's truck (causing the 12 deaths) and manipulated Prentiss into the murder to cover up his own crimes.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Can you account for yourself, at about 6:00 this morning? ... You're saying that's a reason to kill him? You tell us.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: LESTER: Look, fellas, scratch an inch into Stack's life, you'll find 20 stories like mine. // GREEN: Damn, look at his lifestyle. // BRISCOE: Hey, wait a second. Are you telling us that it doesn't add up with the business?
person_rows.notes: Wilkes was a former driver Stack fired after Wilkes was arrested in Texas for carrying a gun (which Stack had told him to carry). Wilkes assaulted Stack when fired and had motive. However, detectives pivot away from him after finding Stack's smuggling operation and cars, suggesting other motives. No public exposure or material harm beyond police questioning, so severity is minimal.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: DEFENSE: Or did you just see your best shot at citizenship? // ALVAREZ: No. I saw him, that man. // DEFENSE: Ten years in our country, Ms. Alvarez, you ever pay taxes? ... Did you ever pay taxes? // ALVAREZ: No. // ... // McCOY: Immigration cases are clogging the federal courts. It's no time to be making personal appeals. And even if you could, Judge Todd would think we bought her testimony. It can't be helped.
person_rows.notes: Blanca Alvarez is a key witness who saw Prentiss hide the murder weapon. Defense attorney publicly accuses her in open court of fabricating testimony to obtain citizenship, exposing her undocumented status and tax evasion. She was also physically attacked/nearly kidnapped by Dan Moncrief (hired by Terry Dorn) to intimidate her. The courtroom exposure and attack qualify as severe harm. Ultimately faces deportation in two months, separating her from her husband, children, and mother who all live in the US. Episode ends with McCoy unable to help her avoid deportation. While she is a witness rather than accused of the primary crime (murder), she is publicly accused of perjury/fabrication in court and suffers life-altering consequences (deportation, family separation, physical attack). This is a complex case but meets the threshold: accused of lying, proven/implied truthful (her testimony holds up), publicly exposed in court, and faces deportation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Three women named Sylvia Rossi murdered by Harlan Barnes (using Troy Hendricks identity) on orders from father Leland Barnes to eliminate witnesses from 9-year-old workplace shooting case. Leon Whitaker and wife also killed. Elaine Bowman survives but recants testimony.
episode_summary.summary: Episode centers on witness elimination, not false accusations. Leland Barnes orchestrated murders of witnesses from his original trial through his son Harlan. No innocent persons were falsely accused - the perpetrators (Leland and Harlan Barnes) were correctly identified. Elaine Bowman recanted under pressure but was never accused of wrongdoing herself. The murdered Sylvia Rossis were victims, not accused parties.

episode_summary.source_notes: Emily Newton was burned with drain cleaner 6 months before her suicide. Jason Corley was accused, case dismissed due to witness recantation, then re-prosecuted after confession obtained via sting operation.
episode_summary.summary: Emily Newton dies by suicide after being facially disfigured with industrial drain cleaner 6 months prior. Detectives investigate her death and determine her ex-boyfriend Jason Corley threw the acid. Corley is arrested but the case is dismissed when witness Christine Hill recants her line-up ID due to intimidation. A sting operation using Corley's new girlfriend and victim's sister Callie leads to his confession. Corley pleads guilty to 25 years. No falsely accused persons identified in this episode - Corley was guilty throughout.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through trial testimony but cuts off mid-sentence at end
episode_summary.summary: Jeffrey Kilgore is murdered outside a synagogue while destroying a sacred Torah. Initially suspected of being an anti-Semitic hate crime perpetrator, Kilgore is later revealed to be hired by Eric Speicher (congregation president Barry Speicher's cousin) to desecrate the book as leverage to force sale of the building. Barry Speicher killed Kilgore when he caught him in the act. The Palestinian laundromat owner Fadi Abu Libdeh is also briefly suspected due to neighborhood tensions but is cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: With all due respect, Lieutenant, what does a Arab have to do with a dead white guy?... Isn't this the flag of Hamas?... So it wouldn't spike your blood pressure if I threw it in the gutter?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: LIBDEH: 'I was cleared, 100%!' Detectives confront him at his laundromat based on Councilman's claim he 'harasses the congregation' and was questioned about previous arson. GREEN: 'If you have any involvement with the dead person at the synagogue, let's get in front of it now.' LIBDEH: 'He's no one I know. I heard it was a white guy.'
person_rows.notes: Accused based on neighborhood tensions and prior arson investigation. Detectives brought him in for questioning with antagonistic tactics (threatened to throw Hamas calendar in gutter). Cleared when victim identified as Jeffrey Kilgore. Exposure appears limited to police interrogation; no evidence of broader public exposure or material consequences beyond the hostile questioning itself. Does not meet threshold for inclusion as consequence_severity 1 with police_only exposure.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: KAYLA WATSON (girlfriend): 'They said on TV that Jeff tore up a Jewish bible.' She is confused because 'Jeff didn't have anything against them.' Eric Speicher later admits: 'I paid Kilgore to go to the synagogue and mess up the book.' Kilgore was a hired contractor, not a hate crime perpetrator.
person_rows.notes: Though Kilgore is the murder victim, he was publicly accused (via media coverage) of committing an anti-Semitic hate crime/desecration. He was actually a paid contractor hired by Eric Speicher. His girlfriend indicates TV coverage portrayed him as having torn up a Jewish bible out of hatred. He suffered the ultimate harm (murder) but also reputational harm as a hate criminal when he was innocent of that motive. The actual desecration was commercial, not ideological. He is innocent of hate crime accusations, though he did commit burglary/destruction of property as hired.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Philip Messick is murdered and his wife Valerie severely wounded in what appears to be a robbery. The detectives arrest Darrell Dennehy and Jay Fleckner. Valerie identifies Fleckner, but her testimony falters under cross-examination about her emotional state and certainty. Fleckner is acquitted. Retired detective Bob Cerullo (friend of the Messicks) later shoots Fleckner, claiming self-defense. Investigation reveals Cerullo and the deceased detective Dumar were selling stolen property through Messick to the mob. The gun on Fleckner was planted by Cerullo. At trial, Valerie Messick shockingly testifies she asked Cerullo to kill Fleckner and deliberately sabotaged her own testimony to ensure Fleckner's acquittal so Cerullo could kill him. Cerullo is convicted of murder. Multiple defendants from Cerullo's past cases file for new trials based on his admitted evidence planting.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: I framed one of Fleckner's buddies on a drug murder. A lowlife named Anton Brady.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: CERULLO (on stand): 'I framed one of Fleckner's buddies on a drug murder. A lowlife named Anton Brady.'
person_rows.notes: Cerullo admits on the stand to framing Brady on a drug murder. Brady is described as one of 'Fleckner's buddies' who 'ran with' Fleckner. The transcript indicates motions for new trials were filed after Cerullo's confession. This is a wrongful conviction case where the framing cop later admits to fabricating evidence.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Another guy he ran with, Jimmy Mills. I sent him up on a rape beef.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: CERULLO (on stand): 'Another guy he ran with, Jimmy Mills. I sent him up on a rape beef.' Context: Cerullo is admitting to framing people in his career.
person_rows.notes: Cerullo admits on the stand to framing Mills on rape charges. Mills is described as another person who 'ran with' Fleckner. The context of Cerullo's testimony is that he's admitting to framing 'a lot of bad guys in my day' to establish why Fleckner would have reason to kill him. The episode ends noting that motions for new trials have been filed based on Cerullo's admissions.

episode_summary.source_notes: Tadhg Ruane was mentally ill (dysphoric mania) and suicidal. He was not accused of a crime; rather, he was the victim of a suspected murder staged as suicide. Multiple people were investigated as suspects but later cleared.
episode_summary.summary: Journalist Tadhg Ruane's death is initially staged as suicide but investigated as murder. Multiple suspects are investigated and publicly questioned/arrested before the real circumstances emerge: assisted suicide involving his girlfriend Angela Burkett and brother Gerald Ruane. Alfred Biso (gun dealer) and Angela Burkett were both initially suspected of murder before being cleared or having charges reduced. Biso was publicly arrested in front of customers; Angela was publicly accused in court before the assisted suicide defense emerged.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Look, bro, you sell guns to kids. That's serious enough. You add murder to that...
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Police raid Biso's gun shop, arrest him in front of young customers. Detectives: 'Police! Get down! Get down!' Then: 'Unless there's some other Biso. Dude was a journalist? That nutcase?' Biso provides alibi confirmed by neighbors; detectives move on.
person_rows.notes: Biso was investigated because victim Tadhg had scheduled a meeting with him and Tadhg's notebook indicated concern about Biso's illegal gun sales. Detectives arrested Biso at his shop in front of customers, accused him of murder, and threatened him with serious charges. Biso's alibi (confirmed by neighbors) cleared him. Though he was guilty of illegal gun sales (arrested for that), he was innocent of the murder accusation that brought the public raid and interrogation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Oh, come on! He had a kid with another woman. You're the scorned woman. You got revenge. Admit it!
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives: 'Oh, come on! He had a kid with another woman. You're the scorned woman. You got revenge. Admit it!' Angela: 'No, Tadhg needed my help. He wanted to die. All I did was help him!' Later indicted for murder two, publicly tried, eventually plea to assisted suicide (man two, 3 years) after evidence showed it was assisted suicide, not premeditated murder.
person_rows.notes: Angela was indicted for murder two based on prosecution theory she murdered Tadhg out of jealous revenge. Evidence later showed she assisted his suicide (still a crime, but not murder). She was publicly tried, cross-examined, and exposed in court. The murder accusation was excessive given the assisted suicide reality; she eventually pled to man two (assisted suicide) with 3-year sentence. Marked 'partially_involved' because she did assist a suicide (illegal) but was innocent of the MURDER charge that brought the public trial and greater exposure/harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript appears complete through verdict.
episode_summary.summary: High school student Alex Garcia is murdered in a music room. Investigation reveals he was selling test answers to other students. Greg Loomis, a wealthy student whose grades depended on Alex's cheating scheme, killed Alex after he refused to provide more answers. Defense claimed Centinall (ADHD medication) caused involuntary intoxication, but prosecution proved Greg was selling his pills rather than taking them. Freddie Colon committed perjury by falsely claiming he bought pills from Greg (at victim's father's urging), but Greg was ultimately convicted of Murder in the Second Degree. No innocent parties were publicly accused or harmed by false accusations in this episode.

episode_summary.source_notes: Complex case involving mother killing son; includes wrongful ATM murder accusation subplot
episode_summary.summary: Danny Ashburn, a troubled teen living on the streets, is murdered by his mother Allison. Investigation reveals Danny had Type 2 Conduct Disorder and his biological father was serial killer Walter Flint. During trial, defense uses justification argument that Danny posed imminent threat. A surveillance video emerges showing Danny committed an ATM murder of Philip Brown - an innocent victim wrongly counted in initial suspect Shawn's profile, and Philip Brown himself as murder victim. Tina Keith (Danny's girlfriend) knew details but didn't report. Mother convicted of Murder 2.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Attempted murder of a police officer is what you're looking at. Ballistics is checking into that gun of yours. My guess is it's gonna tie you to Danny's murder.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Ballistics on the boy's .38. It's not the murder weapon. You're kidding me.
person_rows.notes: Shawn was arrested after shooting at police during tunnel raid. Detectives believed his .38 would match Danny's murder weapon based on motive (Danny stole his girlfriend Tina). Ballistics cleared him. He had active warrants and escaped from Bellevue, so exposure was contained to police interaction, but he was arrested and faced attempted murder charges for shooting at detective.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: John Ashburn, you're under arrest for the murder of Danny Ashburn. ... Allison, what the hell is going on here? ... Where's my wife? She's being interviewed right now.
person_rows.notes: John was arrested at his home in front of his wife after police found the murder weapon. He had motive (fed up with stepson), opportunity (E-ZPass records showed GW Bridge trip), and the gun was found in his house. His wife Allison later confessed to the actual murder. He was publicly arrested and his two other children were present. The arrest and accusation occurred at his home with family present, constituting public exposure in the family domain.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final scene where defense accepts plea deal.
episode_summary.summary: Derek Miller, an investment banker, is murdered after threatening to expose his affair with his boss Sophia Keener. Detectives initially suspect Lucas Brody (jealous colleague) and Robert White (estranged husband). Both are publicly questioned but later cleared. Robert White is arrested based on forensic evidence but ultimately cooperates against Sophia. The case reveals decades of sexual abuse by Andrew Keener (Sophia's father) of both Sophia and her daughter Katie, used as leverage for alibis and corporate control.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You were mad at Derek because of the money. You got in a fight and things got out of hand.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Mark Rhodes tells detectives: 'Lucas Brody. Him and Derek had words at The Baby Doll.' Detectives later find Brody passed out drunk in his office. Brody: 'Derek's dead? How?' Detective: 'You don't know nothing about it?' Brody: 'No... I didn't kill him.'
person_rows.notes: Brody was publicly questioned at his workplace (Morgan Keener office) by detectives who found him drunk and unconscious. Detectives accused him based on reported argument with victim over bonus disparity. Cell phone records and bartender confirmations cleared him. He quit his job, likely due to bonus disappointment and possibly the public accusation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Your white SUV, it's under a microscope in our lab. We found Derek's blood and his hair. Explain that... He was in back of the SUV. And he was either dead or dying when you hauled him to the dumpsite.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detective: 'Your white SUV, it's under a microscope in our lab. We found Derek's blood and his hair.' Robert was arrested and brought to precinct. Later cooperated and received deal after explaining he helped dispose of body after the murder but wasn't present when Derek died. E-Z Pass records showed he entered Manhattan at 3:20am, after Derek's time of death (midnight-2am).
person_rows.notes: Robert White was Sophia Keener's estranged husband in bitter custody dispute. Forensic evidence (Derek's blood/hair in his white SUV) led to his arrest for murder. He was publicly arrested and brought in for questioning. He was innocent of the murder itself but guilty of helping dispose of the body (accessory after the fact). Time-stamped E-Z Pass records proved he couldn't have been present during the murder (midnight-2am) as he didn't enter Manhattan until 3:20am. He ultimately cooperated against Sophia in exchange for misdemeanor plea on body disposal.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through most of trial but cuts off during final dialogue.
episode_summary.summary: Jeffrey Pope, a supervisor for private military contractor Diamond River Group, is murdered in a NYC hotel. Investigation leads to Kevin Boatman (ex-colleague who blamed Pope for deaths in Iraq roadside bomb) and Robbie Howell (brother of Nick Howell, killed in that ambush). Both are charged with murder. Defense introduces video of Nick Howell's ambush/beheading to show Pope's incompetence created enemies. Boatman reveals Pope was in NYC to ransom an Iraqi terrorist (Abu Zayeed) to Israelis for $15M - DRG team captured him and held him in secret facility. Pentagon needs Boatman's cooperation to locate Zayeed and potentially save three captured Marines. DA dismisses charges against Boatman in exchange for cooperation; proceeds against Robbie Howell alone. No falsely accused innocent persons are identified in this episode - the defendants appear to be the actual perpetrators, though defense argues justification/context.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows bank robbery case where manager Howard Grant is initially suspected of complicity but was forced to participate under threat to his daughter Julie.
episode_summary.summary: Bank manager Howard Grant is arrested after robbing his own bank and killing security guard. Detectives discover Grant's 6-year-old daughter Julie was kidnapped by accomplices to force his cooperation. Grant was publicly arrested at work and initially treated as criminal before truth emerged. Mitchell Lowell is eventually identified as mastermind.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Security guard: 'Better back off, Mr. Grant. I'm going to have to detain you, sir.' ... Ex-wife Cheryl: 'Imagine my surprise when Mrs. Fogarty knocks on my door and tells me that the man I married, father of my only child, is being lead away from his place of employment in handcuffs.'
person_rows.notes: Grant robbed his own bank only because kidnappers held his daughter hostage and threatened to kill her. He was publicly detained/arrested at his workplace (the bank) in front of colleagues and customers. His ex-wife mentions he was 'lead away from his place of employment in handcuffs.' Though he fired a gun killing accomplice Cyrus Lowell, this was defensive/under duress. Detectives quickly establish he was coerced once they learn about daughter Julie's kidnapping.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-testimony. Dr. Copelan's final answer cut off.
episode_summary.summary: A diner shooting initially targets the wrong victim (Oscar Morales killed instead of hospital administrator Elaine Clemens). The shooter, 14-year-old Jeremy Miller, believed Clemens and Children's Healing House killed his sister Emily through medical mistreatment. Jeremy dies by suicide during arrest. Investigation reveals Dr. Andrew Copelan was conducting unauthorized drug experiments on foster children with AIDS, including Emily Miller, who died from over-medication. Foster mother Pamela Henson was wrongly charged with abuse for taking Emily off medications. Drug dealer Prim is briefly suspected but cleared. The trial focuses on whether Dr. Copelan's experimental treatment constituted depraved indifference murder.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Ten decks of heroin within 1,000 feet of a school? That's 'B' felony territory. 'B' felony, that's nine years, bro.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: DETECTIVE: 'Fifteen of your boys and their hootchie-mamas isn't gonna make an alibi for you!' PRIM: 'I swear on my grandmother I ain't do this!'
person_rows.notes: Carlos (victim's nephew) told police Prim had motive after being pepper-sprayed. Prim was arrested with 10 decks of heroin, interrogated aggressively about murder, threatened with 9-year sentence. His alibi (15 witnesses at bodega across street) was verified by bodega owner. Gave police Jeremy Miller's name. Exposure appears limited to police interrogation, but arrest itself is public harm (severity 2).

person_rows.quote_or_scene: PAMELA HENSON: 'Three months ago, Child Services took her to court and had her charged with abuse.' ORDERLY: 'That abuse charge was ludicrous! And it wasn't for physical abuse, it was because I stopped giving Emily her AIDS medication.' HENSON (on stand): 'That's when he called in Child Services to take Emily away.'
person_rows.notes: Foster mother charged with child abuse by Child Services at Dr. Copelan's instigation. The 'abuse' was taking Emily off medication that was making her sicker. Emily improved off meds (gained weight, could walk, smiled). Henson tried alternative treatment (mumbala khan root). Lost custody of Emily. Defense attorney implied at trial that Henson's actions killed Emily, but context shows Dr. Copelan's over-medication was the actual cause. No indication charges were formally dropped, but she's treated as cleared. Public court exposure plus loss of child = severity 3.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final scene with Lund plea negotiation.
episode_summary.summary: Undercover detective Dana Baker is murdered after her cover is blown by a newspaper column written by Veronica Drake. Investigation reveals political operative Eric Lund leaked her identity to discredit her father Tom Baker's book criticizing Congressman Prescott. Lund obtained Baker's info by impersonating Homeland Security to NYPD Payroll, then fed it to reporters. No innocent persons were falsely accused in this episode - the investigation correctly identified Lund as responsible, though he attempted to fabricate exculpatory evidence during trial. The victim Dana Baker was undercover but never accused of crimes; her father Tom Baker inadvertently mentioned she was NYPD at a private gathering but was not formally accused of wrongdoing.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through investigation and first trial
episode_summary.summary: Detective Nick Falco wakes up to find Sonia Vasquez murdered in his bathroom. He becomes a suspect but insists he was drugged. Investigation reveals Sonia was part of a robbery crew drugging wealthy men. Keisha Sipes is charged with Sonia's murder (motivated by Sonia's pregnancy with Keisha's boyfriend Henry's baby) but is acquitted. Evidence emerges suggesting Angela Young (Henry's mother) orchestrated both murders as payback against Falco for imprisoning her ex-husband Tyrell Young years earlier. Multiple innocent people are publicly accused and harmed.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Lieutenant: 'Well, as of this morning, what you are is a suspect, on modified assignment, with no gun or shield. And the sooner you accept that, the better.'
person_rows.notes: Falco is suspended and stripped of gun/shield in front of colleagues. He is the primary suspect throughout most of the investigation. His apartment is the crime scene. The show strongly implies he is innocent - drugged by Sonia, then framed by Angela Young as revenge. Defense attorney publicly accuses him of planting evidence and murder in court testimony.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detective to Keisha during arrest: 'Hey, I've had enough of you, shut it.' And physical handling implied during arrest.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Rowen: 'We've come across information that leads us to believe Angela Young set up your client to kill Sonia Vasquez.' Final verdict: 'We find the defendant, Keisha Sipes, not guilty.'
person_rows.notes: Keisha is arrested, indicted, and tried for Sonia's murder. The prosecution believes she had motive (Sonia pregnant with her boyfriend's baby) and evidence (bloody shoes in her dumpster). At trial's end, evidence emerges that Angela Young orchestrated the murder and framed Keisha. She is acquitted. The show strongly implies Angela manipulated Keisha into committing the murder or framed her entirely. Either way, the formal legal process (arrest, trial, public accusation) caused significant harm.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Falco to Tyrell: 'You're a criminal. You're dirt!'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Fontana: 'Both Sim Var and Sonia were reading books just like that one.' Green: 'That would explain the mixed crew.' Fontana: 'Looks like Tyrell found himself a new way to recruit.' Later, detectives bring Tyrell in for questioning about the robbery crew and murder conspiracy.
person_rows.notes: Tyrell is brought in for questioning on suspicion of coaching/running the robbery crew and orchestrating Sonia's murder as revenge against Falco. Detectives theorize he's behind it all. Falco publicly degrades him. Later evidence shows Angela (his ex-wife) was actually running the crew and orchestrated the murders. Tyrell is exonerated by the narrative pivot. However, exposure appears limited to the interrogation room - no indication it spread beyond police. Borderline inclusion due to limited public exposure, but the public confrontation and insults from Falco in front of other detectives qualifies as severity 2.

episode_summary.source_notes: Complex case involving home invasions, corrupt DEA agent, and controversial prosecutorial tactics following ADA Borgia's murder
episode_summary.summary: Home invaders kill the Andreas family while trying to lure Frank Andreas home. Andreas had been cooperating with ADA Borgia about selling fake DEA badges to the killers. The case reveals a corrupt DEA agent (Kevin Almonte) was tipping the home invaders to drug houses. Multiple innocent people are publicly accused before the real perpetrators (Ricky Robinson and Vincent Mulkowsky) are caught. ADA Borgia is kidnapped and murdered. McCoy stages a controversial fake prosecution to pressure Almonte into revealing the killers' location.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You're our witness. We own your ass... Help us apprehend these men or all deals are off... You want to withhold information, you can do it from a cell in Rikers... And you better believe they'll get to you in there.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Andreas initially arrested in fraud raid for selling pirated DVDs and stolen NYPD equipment. Released on ROR to cooperate. His wife Danielle, mother-in-law, and two children are murdered by the home invaders while trying to lure him back. Andreas: 'I used to come here every Sunday with my two kids. No wife, no work. Two hours a week where everything seemed okay. And now I got them all killed, didn't I?'
person_rows.notes: Andreas was selling stolen police equipment and made fake DEA badges, but is innocent of the home invasion murders. His cooperation with prosecution led to his family being targeted and killed. The public raid on his store and his arrest exposed him, making him a target for the killers who feared he would identify them.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Andreas reveals: 'Dave Morley. He tends bar at Alberta's on Avenue A. He's the guy I asked. He might know where they are.' Van Buren reports: 'The bartender Andreas gave up, this guy Morley, he took his back pay two days ago and left town.'
person_rows.notes: Morley was the person Andreas contacted to get information about Ricky and Vincent. When police came looking for him, he had already taken his back pay and fled town, suggesting he feared retaliation or police involvement. No evidence he was involved in the crimes.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Police! Get up! Get up. Get out of that bed. Get on your knees. Get down!... You pawned an engagement ring he stole from a quadruple homicide. You want to be a part of that, huh, do you?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: During violent raid: Officer: 'What's your name?' 'Alphonse.' 'You got ID?' 'In my pants. In my wallet.' Officer: 'Where's Ricky?' 'Who the hell's Ricky?' Officer: 'You pawned an engagement ring he stole from a quadruple homicide. You want to be a part of that?' Alphonse: 'Look, Officer, I didn't want them here. But they didn't give me much of a choice, okay?'
person_rows.notes: Alphonse was an unwilling host to Ricky and Vincent who apparently forced him to let them stay. Police raided his apartment violently, accused him of being part of the murders due to pawning a stolen ring, but he was clearly a victim of the killers' coercion. He cooperated and gave information about Ricky and Vincent.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through most of trial scenes but cuts off mid-sentence at end during plea negotiation.
episode_summary.summary: A police officer (Det. Carvahal) is killed interrupting a burglary. Gary Blanchard is initially questioned as the former tenant whose apartment was burglarized. Celebrity photographer Blanchard is accused/suspected of involvement but is later revealed to be the victim of the burglary and a co-conspirator/blackmail target of the actual killer Justin Smolka. Blanchard cooperates and testifies against Smolka. No evidence he was ever seriously accused of the murder itself, but he faced public questioning and potential charges for obstruction/blackmail before cooperating.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: ED: Stop being a smart ass. / BLANCHARD: I thought I was the victim here. / ED: We did mention that a police officer was killed, right?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Blanchard at his workplace/studio where he's shooting photos. He's treated as a potential suspect initially. ED: 'Yeah, I take shots at dance clubs and I post them on my site.' Later treated dismissively: 'He was a cocky little snot.' Blanchard emails the squad to complain about 'disrespectful' treatment.
person_rows.notes: Blanchard was the burglary victim and is never formally accused of murder, but detectives initially treat him with suspicion and hostility as if he might be involved. He's questioned publicly at his work location in front of clients/subjects. Later revealed to be involved in blackmail scheme but cooperates. Severity = 2 because questioning was public (at workplace) but no arrest/formal charges for the murder.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode involves organized street fighting leading to death of Todd Hauser, then a retaliatory brawl. Multiple suspects cleared or charges reduced.
episode_summary.summary: Todd Hauser dies from injuries sustained in a Union Square street fight. Vince Fonsella is arrested for murder but pleads to negligent homicide and avoids jail time. Hauser's friends and Fonsella's crew engage in a retaliatory brawl that kills three people including Fonsella. Key defendants are charged with terrorism but ultimately plead to lesser charges. Donna (the girl who introduced Todd to street fighting) is initially accused of conspiracy but cleared. The prosecution's terrorism theory is controversial but used as leverage for plea deals.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BERNARD: 'Yeah. Well, you are in trouble.' LUPO: 'You know, I think she liked watching Todd get the life beat out of him.' BERNARD: 'No way, I didn't...' LUPO: 'And the guy who killed him is her boyfriend.' BERNARD: 'That's insane. No, no, I like the sound of this. You liked watching your man kill someone, didn't you?' LUPO: 'In fact, you probably set the whole thing up. Yeah, she probably played Todd. Made him fight.' BERNARD: 'Unless you want a charge of conspiracy to commit assault, you better help us find this person.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: LUPO: 'Unless you want a charge of conspiracy to commit assault, you better help us find this person.' DONNA: 'Okay. I think I saw him in one of the videos. On the Internet.'
person_rows.notes: Donna was threatened with conspiracy charges during interrogation to get her to identify Vince Fonsella. Detectives falsely claimed she set up the fight and liked watching Todd get beaten. She was never formally charged and the threat was purely coercive. Exposure remained within police interrogation room with no public consequences, so severity is low despite the threatening tactics.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Rick Devon is murdered in a park. Police initially suspect drug dealers Big Chuck and Momo, who are arrested and charged. Later, Rick's brother Ted Devon is revealed as the actual killer. Pete Harris (Rick's mentally disabled brother) is coerced by family to falsely confess to protect Ted, leading the ADA to threaten charging Pete with murder to force the truth out.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: We're going to lean on your crew until somebody gives somebody up, anybody. We don't care, just as long as we've got a body to stand before the judge. You dig?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Docket number 85632. People v. Charles Thomas and Daryl Main, one count of murder in the second degree... People request remand.
person_rows.notes: Big Chuck and Momo were arrested and charged with Rick Devon's murder based on circumstantial evidence (being near body, having victim's money). First-degree murder charge added for killing Diego Cardenas. Later proven innocent of Rick Devon's murder when Ted Devon confessed. Pled guilty only to Cardenas killing.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: First squeal gets the deal... We have the witness you thought you were killing, the one who saw you in the park.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Docket number 85632. People v. Charles Thomas and Daryl Main... People request remand. Both defendants have long criminal records with histories of violence.
person_rows.notes: Momo was arrested and charged alongside Big Chuck for Rick Devon's murder. Charges for Devon's murder were dropped when Ted Devon confessed. Pled guilty only to Cardenas killing. Detectives used 'bake-off' interrogation tactic trying to get one to turn on the other.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Seems to me, you going into this bathroom is something they would've noticed... You can't rely on their memory.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Shot in the mouth. Subtle... This is the witness who saw you with the body in the park Tuesday night.
person_rows.notes: Diego was suspected of being involved in Rick Devon's murder because his key was found at the crime scene bathroom. He lied about being in the park to protect Pete Harris (the actual witness) and because he was cruising for gay hookups. Big Chuck and Momo murdered him thinking he was the witness who could identify them for Devon's murder. His death was a direct consequence of being suspected, even though police exposure was limited.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: It wasn't true, 'cause I was lying. Teddy didn't hurt Ricky. I did. Yeah, Ricky wanted me to live with him, and I didn't even know him... We argumented and I pushed him and he fell and he hit his head.
person_rows.notes: Pete, a mentally disabled man, was coerced by his family to falsely confess in court to killing his brother Rick in order to exonerate their brother Ted (the actual killer). The ADA threatened to charge Pete with murder to force the family to stop manipulating him. Pete was the actual witness to Ted's crime but was pressured to recant and self-incriminate. The confession was given in open court, making it public exposure.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Sounds like you had the same motives to fight with Rick that Ted did. Maybe you killed him.
person_rows.notes: During cross-examination, Connie Rubirosa suggested Matt Devon could have killed Rick, pointing out he had the same financial motives as Ted and had loaned Ted money. This was a tactical accusation during trial but remained at the interrogation/theory level with no public exposure or arrest. Minimal harm - included primarily for completeness as the suggestion was made in open court but immediately withdrawn.

episode_summary.source_notes: Polygamist fundamentalist Mormon sect (Church of the True Path) from Boyd Canyon, Arizona. Episode involves murder, attempted kidnapping conspiracy, and systemic abuse within religious compound.
episode_summary.summary: Caleb Friendly is murdered in Central Park. Patrick Friendly is arrested and charged with murder after confessing to stabbing Caleb with a chisel to protect Michelle Landon (a runaway wife of the sect's Prophet Wyatt Landon) from kidnapping. Evidence later reveals Wyatt Landon orchestrated a kidnapping conspiracy involving Caleb, making Patrick's actions potentially justified. Wyatt Landon and co-conspirators are charged with attempted kidnapping and felony murder but charges are ultimately dismissed when Michelle, now pregnant with Wyatt's child, refuses to testify and returns to the compound.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, indicted, tried, charges pending dismissal after conspiracy revealed
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LUPO: 'Now you're starting to make sense. You probably brought a weapon with you, right?' ... 'Patrick, whatever you did, was in defense of Michelle, and a righteous thing.' ... Patrick: 'All right. Yes, I did have something. A wood chisel from the super's toolbox.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: MS. ZIFF: 'Even the police told my client that his actions were righteous.' CONNIE: 'They were working a suspect, Ms. Ziff, you can't take them at their word.' ... Later evidence shows Caleb was conspiring with Wyatt Landon to kidnap Michelle, making Patrick's actions potentially justified defense of another.
person_rows.notes: Patrick confessed to stabbing Caleb with a chisel after detectives told him his actions were 'righteous.' Later investigation revealed Wyatt Landon orchestrated a kidnapping conspiracy involving Caleb to abduct Michelle, strongly suggesting Patrick's actions were justified defense of another. He was publicly tried and held on $1M bail. The episode ends with charges dismissed against Landon's co-conspirators, strongly implying Patrick's case would also be dismissed or result in acquittal given the justification defense and prosecutorial focus shifting to Landon.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence during final plea negotiation
episode_summary.summary: A crane collapse kills worker Carlos Lazaro, initially blamed on bribery/overloading. Investigation reveals Carlos's wife Amelia was pushed into a pool by employer Sandra Talbot (not over suspected affair with Gary Talbot as first believed, but over Amelia's religious opposition to controversial medical procedure planned for the Talbots' disabled daughter). Gary Talbot falsely accused of affair; city inspector Eddie O'Connor falsely suspected of being sole bribe recipient. Transcript cuts off during final plea negotiation.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: GARY: 'You can't receipt every out-of-pocket expense on a $200 million building. And anyway, I'm not the only one with access to the petty cash.' Detectives question him about missing petty cash. Later proven the missing money went to pay for Amelia Lazaro's hospital bills (Gary was helping Carlos), not bribes.
person_rows.notes: Initially suspected of embezzlement/bribery scheme when petty cash went missing. Questioned at work by detectives. Later cleared when forensic accounting showed missing amounts matched his payments for Amelia's hospital care (helping Carlos whose wife was in coma). No affair occurred—Gary and Amelia were not romantically involved despite Sandra's beliefs.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: SANDRA (in allocution): 'Amelia admitted that she and Gary had been together.' CUTTER later proves: 'There was no call from Gary Talbot that day, or any other day.' And to Gary: 'There was no affair.' Phone records prove no romantic contact between Gary and Amelia.
person_rows.notes: Sandra Talbot accused Gary of having an affair with Amelia Lazaro (basis for her pushing Amelia into pool). Sandra claimed she found voicemail from Gary on Amelia's phone arranging meeting. Phone records proved no such call existed. The real motive was Amelia's religious opposition to medical procedure for Lacy. Gary's marriage was damaged by false accusation. Cutter acknowledged 'There was no affair' in court.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Dr. Ziering: 'Anonymous threats left on his office voicemail.' AMELIA OVER PHONE: 'This is for Dr. Ziering. What you are doing to Lacy Talbot is against God's plan. You cannot change her nature. It's evil and you will burn in hell if you do this.' Sandra pushed Amelia into pool after Dr. Ziering refused further treatment.
person_rows.notes: Amelia worked as caretaker for Talbots' disabled daughter Lacy. Left religious-based voicemail messages to Dr. Ziering opposing estrogen therapy/surgery planned for Lacy ('against God's plan'). Dr. Ziering stopped treating Lacy due to these calls. Sandra Talbot, enraged that Amelia cost them their doctor, pushed Amelia into pool knowing she couldn't swim, resulting in dry drowning/coma then death. While technically guilty of making threatening calls, the consequence (being murdered) vastly outweighs the offense, and her opposition was religiously motivated concern for child's welfare.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through investigation and arrest but may be cut before final resolution.
episode_summary.summary: William Timmons, a reformed drug addict and confidential informant for Sheriff Burkhart, is murdered in NYC. Dianne Cary, whom Timmons raped 3 years prior (charges dropped due to Burkhart's protection of his CI), is initially suspected and arrested. Investigation reveals Sheriff Burkhart murdered Timmons to prevent him from exposing Burkhart's cover-up of the rape and other crimes. Deputy Linz is framed/pressured into suicide with the murder weapon. Dianne Cary is publicly arrested, jailed, and exposed despite being innocent of murder.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, publicly confronted at workplace, jailed_pretrial, reputation_damage, multiple_interrogations
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You followed him back to his bus, you hunted him down... / Lupo deliberately touches her to provoke arrest after she says 'I don't like people touching me'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: VAN BUREN: 'Miss Cary, you're under arrest for murder.' LUPO arrests her at flower shop after she assaults Timmons there. Later: CUTTER: 'We matched the slugs in his body to the slugs you fired during target practice.' DIANNE: 'I didn't kill that bastard. I should've, but I didn't.'
person_rows.notes: Dianne is a rape victim whose rapist (Timmons) was protected by Sheriff Burkhart because Timmons was Burkhart's confidential informant. When Timmons visited her in NYC (apparently to make amends per AA), she assaulted him at her workplace but did not kill him. Police arrested her publicly at the flower shop in front of coworkers, interrogated her aggressively, obtained bail of unknown amount. She is eventually cleared when evidence shows Sheriff Burkhart murdered Timmons and framed Deputy Linz. Severity 3: arrested, jailed pretrial, public workplace arrest, aggressive interrogation, but not life-destroying (no trial, charges appear to be dropped when true killer identified).

person_rows.quote_or_scene: LINZ'S WIFE: 'Sheriff Burkhart came by. Him and Robbie went out to the garage to talk. Robbie looked upset after the Sheriff left. He didn't say why. He just told me not to worry. And then he went into the woods.' BERNARD: 'Ballistics said the .38 that Linz shot himself with was the same gun that was used to kill Timmons.' LUPO: 'We're supposed to think he murdered Timmons. Very neat and tidy.' CUTTER: 'That must be what Burkhart told him, that he'd have to take the fall for Timmons' murder, like a good soldier.'
person_rows.notes: Deputy Linz was pressured/manipulated by Sheriff Burkhart into committing suicide with the murder weapon (the .38 used to kill Timmons), making it appear Linz was the murderer. Burkhart visited Linz, spoke privately, Linz appeared upset, then went into woods and shot himself. This was Burkhart's plan to frame Linz and protect himself. Linz's wife and family were exposed to the implication he was a murderer. Severity 4: death (suicide) as direct consequence of being positioned to take the fall for murder he didn't commit.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript involves fake memoir scandal, multiple false identities claiming to be 'Sweetie Ness', and wrongful conviction.
episode_summary.summary: Dale Marks, an actor hired to impersonate the author 'Sweetie Ness,' is murdered. Cody Larson is accused and convicted of the murder, but evidence later suggests the agent Kate Tenny (who may be the real 'Sweetie Ness' and wrote the memoir herself) committed the crime. The prosecution moves to vacate Larson's conviction at episode's end.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: CUTTER: I'm filing to vacate the conviction of Cody Larson. / JACK: You can't prove she did it. / CUTTER: Something may turn up. And if it doesn't? She wins. We lose.
person_rows.notes: Larson is convicted of murdering Dale Marks but evidence emerges implicating Kate Tenny instead. ADA moves to vacate at episode end, acknowledging wrongful conviction. Larson suffered full public trial and conviction despite being innocent.

episode_summary.source_notes: Judge Reynolds' mental decline becomes central to trial proceedings; case involves Nancy Hartwig's murder and Chris Mason's involvement.
episode_summary.summary: Nancy Hartwig is murdered with a shovel at a community garden. Chris Mason, who borrowed her car to visit his children, becomes the prime suspect after the car is found at a New Jersey cop-killing scene. Joe Hartwig, Nancy's husband, is also investigated as a potential jealous spouse. Mason is eventually established as the true killer, but Joe faces public accusations of being involved due to the unusual zero-carbon lifestyle and Nancy's secret rule-breaking (meeting Mason at bars, driving their car). The case is complicated by Judge Reynolds' cognitive decline and his law clerk manipulating court proceedings.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: ESTELLE ADAMS (cross-examining Joe): 'Isn't it possible that your wife didn't tell you about meeting Mr. Mason because she was breaking another kind of rule?' JOE: 'I don't believe that.' ESTELLE: 'Well, let me ask you a hypothetical. If you knew your wife was seeing another man, how jealous would you have been?' JOE: 'I would've been jealous, but I didn't know.' ESTELLE: 'But you didn't have a hint, when she was sneaking out at odd hours?' ... ESTELLE: 'Do you honestly expect us to believe...'
person_rows.notes: Joe Hartwig is publicly questioned in court about whether he killed his wife out of jealousy over her secret meetings with Chris Mason. Defense attorney Estelle Adams aggressively cross-examines him about his potential jealousy and whether he would have been angry enough to kill Nancy if he discovered her breaking their rules and potentially having an affair. While the prosecution ultimately establishes Mason as the killer, Joe endures public humiliation and accusation in open court. He is never formally charged but faces the severe implication that he murdered his wife. The transcript shows the defense using him as an alternative theory, and he must publicly testify about intimate marital matters while being portrayed as a controlling, potentially violent husband.

episode_summary.source_notes: Episode involves adoption fraud/slavery ring; double murder of lawyers investigating child welfare.
episode_summary.summary: Frederick and Elizabeth Bellamy, family law attorneys, are murdered. Investigation reveals they were looking into the welfare of Isabelle Carter, an adopted Haitian girl returned to Haiti after her adoptive parents' divorce. The case uncovers a slavery ring operated by Eric and Miriam Johnson, who fraudulently adopted Haitian children (including Patrick Johnson) to use as servants. The Johnsons' daughter Karen coerced Patrick into shooting the Bellamys to protect her parents. No innocent persons were falsely accused with public exposure or material harm in this episode. Bill Carter was initially investigated but never publicly accused or arrested. Patrick Johnson, while technically innocent (coerced into the murders by Karen), is charged as a defendant rather than being falsely accused, and his harm stems from his enslaved status rather than a false accusation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom scenes. Episode centers on attorney Martin Winston who allegedly committed perjury that led to Victor Cruz's wrongful execution.
episode_summary.summary: Contractor Charles Sawyer is murdered after discovering potential exculpatory evidence for PanWorld Airlines in a class action lawsuit. Investigation reveals his attorney Martin Winston likely killed him to protect a potential $400M settlement. Winston had previously testified falsely against his own client Victor Cruz in a judge's murder case, leading to Cruz's wrongful conviction and execution. Winston is arrested for Sawyer's murder but evidence is suppressed. He is later charged with Cruz's murder via depraved indifference/perjury theory.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: MARIA: Victor didn't kill that judge. He never hurt anybody in his whole life... Mr. Winston treat us like family. / CUTTER: Cruz testified that he sometimes smoked at Winston's office... Winston categorically denied that he ever let anyone smoke in his office. / MS. PALINA: If Mr. Winston had said that, I would have had a reasonable doubt about Mr. Cruz's guilt. I wish he had said it.
person_rows.notes: Victor Cruz was convicted of murdering Judge Davis in the PanWorld case based significantly on Winston's perjured testimony that Cruz was never allowed to smoke in his office. Cruz claimed he was framed - that someone took his cigarette butts from Winston's office and planted one at the crime scene. A juror testified that if Winston had corroborated Cruz's testimony about smoking in the office, she would have had reasonable doubt. Cruz was executed by the federal government. His widow Maria maintains his innocence. The episode strongly implies Cruz was framed by Winston, who had the most to gain from Judge Davis's death (protecting his potential $400M legal fee from the airline lawsuit).

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears truncated mid-sentence at trial. Susan Walden appears to be about to testify but transcript ends.
episode_summary.summary: A science writer Ned Lasky is charged with murdering 12-year-old Eric Foley and housekeeper Grazinya to revenge a 30-year-old slight at a Dartmouth sorority mixer where Joyce Foley (Eric's mother) asked him to leave. Lasky's daughter Molly testifies against him regarding evidence (briefcase, gas receipt), motivated by a $100,000 reward. Joyce Foley identifies Lasky from a college photo as the boy she 'squashed' at the mixer. The defense attacks both witnesses' credibility. No falsely accused persons are identified in the transcript - Lasky is the primary suspect throughout and no one else is seriously pursued or publicly exposed as a suspect. The transcript ends mid-trial with Susan Walden about to testify.

episode_summary.summary: Vladimir Rezanov (Vic Russell) is murdered; Stanley Klein is accused, convicted of enterprise corruption for fraud/murder, but is himself later murdered. The investigation reveals Klein's son Chad may have killed both Klein and Klein's wife for inheritance purposes. Klein was initially the prime suspect for Rezanov's murder and was publicly tried and convicted before being killed.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Klein is publicly tried and convicted of murdering Rezanov via enterprise corruption charge. Later murdered himself; his dying words 'The Russians. It was the Russians' suggest someone else killed Rezanov. The transcript reveals Chad (Klein's son) likely killed both Rezanov AND Klein/Klein's wife for inheritance, meaning Klein was wrongly convicted.
person_rows.notes: Klein was convicted of murdering Rezanov in a public trial with enterprise corruption charges. He was separated from his wife, had bail/home confinement, and lost his business assets to forfeiture. Later evidence (his murder and Chad's possession of Rezanov's watch, knowledge of abatement law, motive for inheritance) strongly implies Chad killed Rezanov with Klein's gun, then killed Klein and his wife. Klein's dying accusation of 'the Russians' further suggests he didn't kill Rezanov. Klein suffered public trial, conviction, asset seizure, and ultimately murder - all consequences of a false murder accusation.

episode_summary.summary: Sergeant Scott Waylon is killed after taking hostages; investigation reveals he robbed collector Norman Lukovitch (who died in struggle) for John McIntyre, who wanted JFK's hair to prove paternity. Multiple innocent people publicly accused before real connections emerge.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: TRISHA: It's all my fault. I was so stupid. [...] I got an email from some official person in Nigeria. [...] How much did they take you for? $62,000. All of our savings.
person_rows.notes: Detectives initially explore whether Trisha was involved in husband's crimes due to financial motive. She reveals she lost their savings to Nigerian scam. No public exposure, consequence limited to interview stress. Does not meet threshold for inclusion as harm was minimal and private.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BERNARD: Another one. I'm starting to think it's us. [...] GUARD: His new bodyguard went aboard. Left a couple of minutes later. [...] Just before 8:00. LUPO: Waylon was here Monday night around 8:00, two hours before he took hostages at the bar. Dryden might have been dead by the time Waylon got here.
person_rows.notes: Dryden found dead on yacht. Initially suspected of being killed by Waylon (who visited boat). Later proven Dryden was killed Monday morning (bagel in gullet), while Waylon was on duty in Bronx. Real killer was McIntyre, who Dryden was working for. Dryden was murdered to cover up the Lukovitch robbery conspiracy.

episode_summary.summary: A firebomb at a military recruitment center kills a baby. Initially Derek Sherman is arrested after vigilante group Operation Molly identifies him and finds evidence (bricks, butane in backpack). Later the widow Carly Di Gravia is arrested after drunk statements and revelations about her husband's bomb-making history. Operation Molly members plant evidence (butane in backpack), and testimony reveals Derek dealt marijuana (non-violent profile). The transcript ends before verdict, but both Derek and Carly appear to be wrongly accused at different points, with Derek's arrest dropped when focus shifts to Carly.

person_rows.consequence_detail: perp_walk, public_trial, arrested, remanded_to_jail, assaulted_by_vigilantes, media_outing
person_rows.quote_or_scene: CLERK: 'Docket Number 93250, People v. Derek Sherman, Arson and First Degree Murder.' [...] JUDGE: 'Your client's remanded.' (CHEERING) [...] SHERMAN: 'That's right. I got drunk and I got angry about my sister. I'm not pleading to anything but that. [...] All I did [was throw] a brick through a window.' [...] CUTTER: 'Dismiss against Derek Sherman and make a deal with Carly. Quietly.' [...] WELLER: 'The fact here is that you had a perfectly good suspect in Derek Sherman. But because Carly didn't grieve in a manner that suited you and your mob of vigilantes, you decided to punish her.' [...] Evidence revealed as planted by Operation Molly member who sprayed butane in his backpack.
person_rows.notes: Derek admitted only to throwing a brick through window while drunk, angry about his sister's death in Iraq. Vigilante group Operation Molly planted butane evidence in his backpack after assaulting him and stealing it. He was arrested, remanded to jail, publicly tried, and called 'baby killer' by crowds at arraignment. Charges eventually dropped when prosecution pivots to Carly. The planted evidence makes his innocence clear, though transcript cuts off before formal exoneration shown.

[...] WELLER: 'But because Carly didn't grieve in a manner that suited you and your mob of vigilantes, you decided to punish her.' [...] VICKI testifies Carly was drunk (8-9 drinks) when making recorded statements. WELLER cross: 'You got her drunk, didn't you? You toyed with her like a marionette. You planted ideas in her head.' [...] WELLER: 'A shock like almost being burned to death, and losing your child and husband. Did that figure in your profile?'
person_rows.notes: Transcript ends before resolution, flagged needs_deep_review. Carly was the victim/witness (nearly burned to death, lost baby) who became accused based on: (1) drunk statements to vigilante hairdresser who got her drunk and 'toyed with her like a marionette,' (2) father-in-law's testimony about husband's childhood bomb (disputed as revenge for blame), (3) access to blasting caps through father. Defense argues PTSD explains behavior, prosecution had 'perfectly good suspect' in Derek first. Evidence against Derek was planted by vigilantes. Prosecution appears to be pursuing her for political reasons (election concerns mentioned repeatedly). Strong implication she's innocent but being scapegoated. She's publicly called 'baby killer' by crowds, arrested with perp walk, tried publicly.

episode_summary.summary: Larry Novak confesses to murdering his wife years ago after mistakenly believing the rapture occurred. Sam Burwell is murdered after discovering George Darvey embezzled from Sacred Exodus charity. Evan Corliss is initially suspected after confronting Burwell about stolen gold coins. Reverend Reeves initially provides false testimony to protect the charity's work before ultimately revealing the truth about Darvey.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: EVAN: 'I didn't kill him.' ... LUPO: 'He was motivated because you threatened to kill him, isn't that right?' EVAN: 'It was a bluff. I wasn't going to kill him.'
person_rows.notes: Evan confronted and assaulted Burwell about stolen gold coins and threatened him, creating reasonable suspicion. However, detectives quickly cleared him after establishing timeline and motive pointed to Darvey. No public exposure beyond police interrogation, so excluded from harm count despite being questioned.
