episode_summary.summary: Blair Carlson is kidnapped and held in a Harlem building, then dies fleeing into traffic. Ronny Aldridge, who runs Fresh Horizons youth program, is charged with kidnap-murder after failing to get $200k from Pete Gardner (Carlson's boyfriend, a disgraced Wall Street CEO) to buy a farm for his boys. Carlos, a youth in the program, is initially suspected but cleared. Aldridge claims Carlson suggested faking her own kidnapping. Despite strong evidence, jury acquits Aldridge (jury nullification influenced by Gardner's Wall Street greed). No persons shown to be falsely accused meet the innocence + public harm criteria.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Because you think the police are stupid. / If he chased our Jane Doe into traffic, we'll be adding murder to his resume. / You sound like a little boy. It's time to man up and tell the truth.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: LUPO: 'We found your prints on a bottle of water in the room where she was held.' Carlos: 'I seen this on TV, you lie about having some BS evidence or whatever.' Later proven his prints were there because Aldridge told him to put water in the van. Aldridge: 'I told Carlos to put water in the van. I didn't tell him why. That's why his prints are on those bottles.'
person_rows.notes: Carlos is interrogated under pressure with false evidence claims, threatened with murder charges, but interrogation remains police-only with no public exposure. He's pressured to testify against Aldridge in exchange for dropped charges. No indication his accusation became public to family/school/community. Severity 1 because harm was limited to intimidating interrogation without public exposure or formal charges.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through final scene where Martin Garvik reveals the truth about his wife being the actual spy.
episode_summary.summary: Alex Boone, a journalist who spent 6 years in Chinese prison for espionage, is murdered while writing a book about a 1998 espionage case. Police initially suspect David Sutton, son of convicted spy Martin Garvik, of hiring hitman Mike Adams to silence Boone. The case reveals Martin Garvik took the fall for his wife Elyse, who actually passed defense secrets to China before committing suicide. David believed he was protecting his father by having Boone killed, not knowing his mother was the real spy.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: MARTIN: 'Your mother. Elyse's parents were missionaries in China... she took files from my computer and passed them to the Chinese. I didn't know. After I was arrested, she wanted to confess, but we decided that it would be better, better for you children, if I was the one who went to jail.'
person_rows.notes: Martin Garvik was wrongfully convicted of espionage in 1998 and sentenced to life without parole. He is now dying of pancreatic cancer in federal prison. His wife Elyse was the actual spy who passed defense software to China, but Martin took the fall to protect his children from knowing their mother was a traitor. Elyse's guilt and Martin's life sentence drove her to suicide. Martin has served approximately 10+ years for a crime he didn't commit. The wrongful conviction destroyed his family, caused his wife's death, and orphaned his children. Federal prosecution proceeded despite the case being built on false premises.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: DAVID: 'Me? I thought it was you.' MARTIN: 'Well, after Mike called me out of the blue and the cops told me what happened, I thought you'd put Mike up to it.' DAVID: 'No.' MARTIN: 'David, you know me better than that. I would never trade somebody else's life for mine.'
person_rows.notes: David was arrested and prosecuted for hiring Mike Adams to murder Alex Boone. The final scene reveals David did NOT hire Adams - his father Martin thought David had done it, and David thought Martin had done it, but neither actually hired Adams. The transcript ends mid-sentence ('Not to worry, at this point w') suggesting Adams may have acted independently or there's another explanation. David is technically innocent of HIRING the murder, though he made threatening statements about Boone and clearly wanted him dead. His arrest and prosecution caused public exposure and legal jeopardy for a murder-for-hire he didn't actually orchestrate. However, the transcript is incomplete so final disposition is unclear.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided; episode centers on murder of TV reporter Dawn Prescott investigating fraud; fraud mastermind's wife Irene Matson is the killer.
episode_summary.summary: TV reporter Dawn Prescott is murdered while investigating hedge fund fraud by Frederic Matson. Initially, Matson himself is suspected, then Colombian drug lord Livan Santana (who had motive to silence Prescott). Evidence eventually points to Matson's wife Irene, who confesses. Frederic Matson nearly takes the fall by falsely claiming Santana did it to protect his wife. Joe Delaney, a news anchor, is briefly threatened with murder charges for endangering Prescott by tipping off Matson, but is never formally charged—he loses his job instead. Livan Santana is accused of murdering whistleblower Vince Decker but is not prosecuted (fled to Colombia). The episode shows multiple false paths before reaching the true killer.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives arrest Matson: 'I confess... Oh, no, no. To fraud. To very big fraud... I never met Dawn Prescott in my life.' Later, wife Irene confesses: 'I went to see that girl... She wanted to destroy us, Freddie... I couldn't let that happen.'
person_rows.notes: Matson confessed to fraud (which was true) but was arrested as murder suspect. He was publicly arrested and booked. Later tried to falsely confess to murder (claiming Santana did it) to save his wife, but prosecutors discovered his wife was the actual killer. He is innocent of murder, guilty of fraud.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cutter to Delaney: 'We're considering prosecuting him for murder... If he knew that Dawn's source had been killed for blowing the whistle on Matson, then by telling Matson what Dawn was up to, he put her life in danger. That's reckless endangerment. That's homicide.' Sue Martin runs to news director: 'She comes running in to the news director with a big scoop. I am being arrested for murder. She wants to lead the broadcast off with it without me!' Final scene shows Delaney's farewell broadcast, indicating he lost his job.
person_rows.notes: Prosecutors threatened Delaney with murder/reckless endangerment charges to pressure him into testifying. The threat was conveyed in his workplace (Sue Martin learned and spread it). He was never formally charged, but the threat and exposure forced him out of his 35-year career. His final broadcast shows him retiring/forced out. The murder theory was used as leverage, not a genuine prosecution.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Matson testifies: 'Santana killed Decker... He killed Vince Decker, and he killed Dawn Prescott... He asked me if there were any loose ends... I told him about Dawn Prescott... And he told me, she'd be dealt with.' Irene Matson contradicts: 'He's lying. Santana never said anything. I sat in every meeting with that man. Fred was afraid of him. He never said anything like that.' Prosecutors prepared 'an extradition request to the Colombian authorities' but abandoned it after Irene's confession.
person_rows.notes: Colombian drug lord falsely accused by Frederic Matson (who lied to save his wife). Prosecutors prepared extradition request, making the accusation part of court proceedings (public exposure). Irene Matson's confession proves Santana did not kill Dawn Prescott. He may have killed Vince Decker (different murder, different weapon, no alibi for Irene), but is strongly implied innocent of Dawn's murder. He fled to Colombia, avoiding prosecution.

episode_summary.summary: Three white teenagers (Tony Stratton, Timothy Moore, Kyle Chase) attack Hispanic day laborers in a series of hate crimes, including branding victims with a heated Mexican peso. Oswaldo Morales is thrown from their car after being assaulted and branded, left in a coma. Rey Alvarez, an unemployed auto worker from Detroit, is murdered by the same group. All three teens are arrested and prosecuted. Kyle Chase testifies against the other two in exchange for a lighter sentence. The defendants are acquitted of murder but convicted of first-degree assault against Morales. They receive 5-year sentences. After the trial, Morales dies when his mother signs a DNR order, leading to new murder charges. No person in this episode qualifies as falsely accused then proven/strongly implied innocent.

episode_summary.source_notes: Law & Order transcript: murdered firefighter couple, hidden drug money, rookie firefighter Nick Spence wrongly implicated then killed in suspicious warehouse fire
episode_summary.summary: Firefighter Lt. Thomas Cooper and wife Linda are murdered in home invasion. Investigation reveals Cooper found $400K in 40-year-old drug money hidden in walls. Rookie firefighter Nick Spence becomes suspect due to hazing motive and knowledge of money. Spence dies in suspicious warehouse fire before arrest. Later revealed: senior firefighters Brad Toshack, Walters, and McShane committed murders for money; Toshack deliberately sent Spence to death in fire to silence him.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BILL SPENCE (father): 'He was worried. He kept saying crazy stuff, like how he felt responsible for Tom Cooper getting killed... I knew my son was no killer.' CUTTER: 'Spence must have talked to somebody... What about the last two weeks? He was worried.' Later proven: 'Toshack got worried that [Spence] knew too much. So, he set a fire, and he sent Spence up the stairs.'
person_rows.notes: Spence became murder suspect because: (1) hazing victim of Lt. Cooper (forced to eat dog food causing hospitalization), (2) knew Cooper found hidden money (helped with renovation), (3) denied transfer request shortly before murders, (4) weak alibi. Detectives were 'a little late' to question him because senior firefighter Toshack deliberately sent him into warehouse firetrap with improperly stored aerosols, knowing layout from moonlighting asbestos removal job. Spence told father he felt 'responsible' for Cooper's death—because he'd told Toshack about the money, enabling the real killers. Killed to silence him before he could implicate Toshack, Walters, McShane in double murder. Exposure was workplace (firehouse) where he was known suspect. Harm consequence of false accusation: murdered to prevent testimony.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows two British exchange students murdered; initial suspect Charlie Headlind (bipolar neighbor) died trying to save them; actual killer was Wendy Teal (bipolar woman whose brother withheld her meds); brother Dennis convicted via conspiracy theory after initial depraved indifference case collapsed.
episode_summary.summary: Two British grad students (Colin Tilbrook and Geraldine Sarfield) were murdered and set on fire. Police initially suspected Charlie Headlind, a bipolar neighbor, but he died from smoke inhalation trying to save them. Investigation revealed Wendy Teal (Charlie's girlfriend, also bipolar) killed them during a psychotic episode. Her brother Dennis had withheld her medication for three months to incapacitate her while he stole/sold a family heirloom. The prosecution initially pursued Dennis for depraved indifference murder, then pivoted to conspiracy/felony murder theory when Dennis's own testimony about Wendy being 'rational' undermined the original case. Dennis took a plea for 20-to-life.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BERNARD: 'Charlie must've caught fire when he lit them up. I'd like to ask him what he thinks of the irony of that.' // Later: MEDICAL EXAMINER: 'Charlie died trying to save two people he didn't know were already dead.'
person_rows.notes: Charlie was the initial suspect based on proximity and mental health history (bipolar, paranoid tendencies, lived in halfway house). Detectives theorized he committed the murders. Medical examiner proved he died trying to save the victims, not kill them. No public exposure occurred before his death; investigation was contained to police. He is a tragic hero who died rescuing strangers, wrongly suspected due to mental illness and proximity.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: STUDENT REPORTER: 'Last summer, Geri was hired to do an energy audit of public schools in the Bronx. One of her assistants filed a sexual harassment complaint against her.' // LUPO: 'We got through to Geraldine's former assistant up in the Arctic Ocean. He said he never filed a complaint, because there was no harassment.' // TECH REPORT: 'Geraldine never accessed the page from her home computer, or the one at her office. Not only that, the bikini shot... Photoshopped. Then all her affairs? Probably made up.'
person_rows.notes: Geraldine was the victim of an elaborate smear campaign orchestrated by Dennis Teal to manipulate his sister Wendy. Fake sexual harassment complaint, fake promiscuous blog ('Geri Knickers'), photoshopped images—all fabricated and published in student newspaper. The assistant confirmed he never filed harassment complaint. Campaign destroyed her reputation publicly before she was murdered. This fabricated accusation was instrumental in motivating Wendy's psychotic rage that led to Geraldine's murder. Severity 4 because the false accusation contributed to her death.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BERNARD: 'So, Sturge, you ever visit with Geri when she was home alone?' STURGIS: 'Visit?' BERNARD: 'Yeah.' STURGIS: 'You mean, uh... You know...' LUPO: 'Yeah. That's exactly what we mean.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: STURGIS: 'I heard a rumor, stupid rumor about me and her and the paint job, but...' // Investigation reveals the blog entry about affair with 'Sturge' was fabricated as part of smear campaign.
person_rows.notes: Landlord Sturgis was falsely accused (via fabricated blog) of having an affair with Geraldine in exchange for paint work. Detectives confronted him based on fake blog. The entire affair was fabricated as part of Dennis Teal's smear campaign. Exposure limited to police questioning; no public consequences documented. Included because he was directly confronted by police about fabricated sexual misconduct, causing embarrassment and potential marital issues (he emphasized being 'happily married').

episode_summary.summary: Three homeless men are murdered in a copycat of the 'Boxcar Basher' serial killer. Police initially suspect Jonah Applebaum (the 'Bipolar Roller'), a mentally ill street performer, believing victim Paco was killed because he wore Jonah's skates. Jonah is arrested, publicly exposed, and jailed. Later evidence reveals Jonah's business partners Grant and Bob Carpenter committed the murders to frame Jonah or eliminate him from their failing T-shirt business. Jonah is cleared of these three murders but is subsequently revealed to be the actual Boxcar Basher serial killer (9 prior murders). The partners are prosecuted for the three copycat murders.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, perp_walk, indicted, jailed_pretrial, charges_dropped, public_trial_preparation
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LUPO: He lied about the stolen skates. He's got motive. / Detectives pursue arrest warrant and physically chase/tackle Jonah on roller skates in public street.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: JUDGE: 'Case number 16352, People v. Jonah Applebaum, three counts of Murder in the Second Degree.' / Public arrest chase: 'Jonah! You're under arrest! On the ground! Now!' / Later: 'CUTTER: You want to dismiss the charges? Yes, for the murders here... Mr. Applebaum is also the suspect in eight other murders. But not the three he was charged with the other day? No, not those.'
person_rows.notes: Jonah was publicly arrested, booked, indicted, and jailed for three murders he did not commit. The arrest involved a dramatic public chase on roller skates through city streets. He was later cleared when evidence showed his business partners (Grant and Bob Carpenter) committed these specific murders as a copycat. HOWEVER: Jonah was subsequently re-arrested as the actual 'Boxcar Basher' serial killer for 9 OTHER murders. His innocence applies ONLY to the three murders at Randall's Island. The public exposure and legal process (arrest, indictment, jail) qualify as material harm (severity 3). The irony is that while falsely accused of these three, he was guilty of nine others—but per the rubric, we count harm from the FALSE accusation of the Randall's Island murders.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows complex political corruption case involving Governor Shalvoy's wife Rita arranging murder of Charles Whitley to obtain his job at Boland Initiative
episode_summary.summary: Charles Whitley, executive director of Boland Initiative, is murdered. Investigation reveals Rita Shalvoy (Governor's wife) hired Bobby Amato to force Whitley out of his job so she could take it as part of a political deal involving a U.S. Senate seat appointment. Amato made compromising S&M videos of Whitley, then killed him when he wouldn't resign. No falsely accused innocent persons with public exposure - Amato confessed and was actually guilty; investigation targeted the correct perpetrators throughout.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom proceedings but cuts off before verdict is read.
episode_summary.summary: Greg Tanner, a war veteran dealing drugs on campus and suffering PTSD from Abu Ghraib, is shot and killed by Professor Kevin Franklin. Franklin claims self-defense, saying Tanner threatened him. Investigation reveals Tanner witnessed torture sanctioned by Franklin's legal memos and may have been trying to implicate Franklin or seek compensation. Case pivots from self-defense murder trial to prosecution of Franklin and Bush administration officials for conspiracy to commit torture. No falsely accused innocent parties are identified—Franklin is the defendant throughout and claims self-defense rather than innocence of the shooting itself.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript analyzed for false accusation patterns
episode_summary.summary: Emma Kim falsely accuses livery driver Nestor Maldonado of attempted rape to deflect from her involvement in murdering Daisy Chao. Maldonado is arrested, publicly identified in lineup, and held in custody before charges are dropped when Emma's lies unravel. Jim Anderson (Daisy's fiancé) is also falsely suspected of murder and arrested for obstruction before Emma is revealed as the actual killer. Emma also falsely accuses Detective Lupo and Judge Maxen of sexual coercion during trial.

person_rows.episode_title: Just a Girl in the World
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You're trying to jack me up on something I didn't do. I want a lawyer, now!
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Emma: 'Number two. He's number two. That's the man who attacked me.' ... Later: 'Jim told me what he looked like, about the decal. So you agreed to commit a felony for him? I felt I owed him. I never imagined he killed her.'
person_rows.notes: Livery cab driver falsely accused by Emma Kim of attempted rape/kidnapping. Emma fabricated the entire incident including detailed description to frame him. He was arrested, put in lineup (with problematic witness contact), held in custody, and went through suppression hearing before Emma admitted lying. Blood evidence later proven fabricated by crime lab investigator Colin Burke. Maldonado has family in Dominican Republic (wife and son). Experienced coercive interrogation and threats from detectives.

person_rows.notes: Graphic artist and fiancé of murder victim Daisy Chao. Initially prime suspect due to shaky alibi, missing money from victim's account, evidence of affair, and inability to account for whereabouts during murder. Subjected to aggressive interrogation with threats and deception tactics. Arrested for larceny and obstruction. Work colleagues at Jam magazine questioned by police about him. Later cleared when Emma Kim proven to be actual killer. His reputation at workplace damaged by investigation.

episode_summary.summary: Episode involves two parallel bomb plots targeting synagogues. One plot (led by Charles Cole, Ali Hossam, and Joe Darwish) is entirely orchestrated by police informant Sameer Ahmed with fake explosives. A second, real plot (Arthur Jackson group) uses a police-supplied detonator that was meant for the fake plot. Don Sorenson shoots James Caruso (an innocent dumpster diver) during a virtual kidnapping scheme, believing Caruso was his daughter's kidnapper. Multiple people are falsely accused or entrapped.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: PARAMEDIC: He's got a bullet in his chest, could be bleeding out. Let's go. / WITNESS: The kid was poking through this one when the guy came up. The kid was reaching for something in the can, and the guy ran up to him and pulled a gun. / DON SORENSON: I wanted to make him tell me where my daughter is.
person_rows.notes: Caruso was an innocent 'curb shopper' (dumpster diver) shot by Don Sorenson who mistook him for his daughter's kidnapper. The kidnapping was fake (virtual kidnapping scheme). Caruso survived but suffered life-threatening gunshot wound. Exposure was limited to the street incident, but physical harm qualifies as severity 4.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BERNARD: We found some speed, methamphetamine, in the bedroom, in this envelope with a handwritten note. 'Hope this helps. Be strong. Sameer.' / The guy was missing meetings, planning sessions. He could barely drag himself out of bed. So you gave him an illegal drug to keep his energy up?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: SAMEER (on stand): What role did Mr. Darwish and Mr. Hossam play in this alleged conspiracy? Not much. Anything at all? Not really. I just kind of pulled them through it. / DEFENSE: Mr. Darwish was diagnosed with unipolar depression last year. / SAMEER: And I gave drugs to Darwish... Because they wanted me to... If I hadn't helped them, they would have found somebody else.
person_rows.notes: Darwish was a clinically depressed individual drugged with methamphetamine by police informant Sameer Ahmed to maintain his participation in a fake bomb plot orchestrated entirely by the informant and police. Despite informant's courtroom admission that Darwish played no real role and was 'pulled through it,' he was still convicted. The transcript shows entrapment of a mentally ill person who needed drugs just to leave his apartment, making his participation coerced rather than genuine. However, transcript shows guilty verdict, creating ambiguity about his ultimate legal status—but the informant's testimony strongly establishes manufactured guilt.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: DEFENSE: And who told him that that spiritual leader had been bought off by Jews? Mr. Ahmed. Your informant? Yes. Arguing Mr. Hossam into joining the plan.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: HOSSAM (on tape): That's not what the imam on Atlantic Avenue says. [His spiritual leader advised against violence] / SAMEER: That guy? He's so busy going to interfaith breakfasts with rabbis. They bought him off. / SAMEER (on stand): What role did Mr. Darwish and Mr. Hossam play in this alleged conspiracy? Not much. Anything at all? Not really. I just kind of pulled them through it. / CUTTER: Mr. Hossam is a dishwasher who was just along for the ride.
person_rows.notes: Hossam was a dishwasher who initially deferred to his imam's teaching that violence was not a religious duty. Police informant Sameer Ahmed argued him into the conspiracy by claiming the imam had been 'bought off by Jews.' The informant later testified Hossam played minimal role and was 'pulled through' the fake plot. Despite this and prosecutor's own acknowledgment he was 'just along for the ride,' he was convicted. Clear case of entrapment of a follower manipulated by government informant.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript includes complete dialogue and stage directions.
episode_summary.summary: Joy Johnson is murdered. Multiple people are accused or investigated: son Tim (autistic, initially confesses), teen D.J. Lovell (took Tim's key), reality show competitor Belinda Alvarez (visited house day of murder). All are later cleared. Larry Johnson (husband) is ultimately charged but trial ends in mistrial. Tim, D.J., and Belinda all experienced public exposure and consequences from accusations before being cleared.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LUPO: 'Look, Tim, this could've been an accident. Now, if something bad happened, this would be a good time to tell us.' ... BERNARD: 'Tim, why don't you want to tell us why you were throwing pencils?' LUPO: 'We're police detectives. We need your help. You do want to help us, don't you?' TIM: 'Yes.' LUPO: 'Then answer my question.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: TIM: 'Okay. I did it. I killed her.' ... LUPO: 'Okay, we just need you to tell us exactly what you did.' TIM: 'I told my mom what happened and she got mad.' ... [After questioning about the fanny pack] TIM: 'My key was in the left compartment. Dad said I was trustworthy. But I wasn't. I let it get taken.'
person_rows.notes: Tim confessed under police pressure but confession was false. He's autistic and wanted to 'help' the detectives. Exposure appears limited to police interrogation room with father present. No indication of public exposure or material harm beyond the coercive interrogation itself. Severity 1 because confined to police questioning with no public audience or formal sanctions.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BERNARD: 'D.J. Lovell, he's got a record. Petty theft and assault.' ... LUPO: 'So if we look around, we're not going to find it?' D.J.'s MOTHER: 'Why do you care about a fanny pack?' BERNARD: 'Joy was murdered. Tim Johnson had a key in there. Whoever killed Mrs. Johnson might have used a key to get in the house.' MOTHER: 'You think... D.J...'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: D.J.'s mother realizes detectives suspect her son of murder during questioning at their home. MOTHER: 'You think... D.J...' Detectives then search home for fanny pack and stolen money. D.J. admits taking fanny pack but denies knowing about key or murder.
person_rows.notes: D.J. accused in front of his mother at home. Detectives tell mother they suspect him of murder because he stole fanny pack with key. Home searched. He's cleared when he admits prank but denies murder knowledge. Public exposure in family context (mother present and informed) qualifies as severity 2.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: LARRY (on reality show): 'I've been drawn into this bizarre murder trial involving two of our very special families.' ... Trial scenes show Belinda testifying about wanting to be on show and her audition tape where she said 'I swear, to get on this show I would kill.' Prosecution uses this to suggest she's the real killer. Reality show later features both families competing 'to figure out which head of household murdered Joy Johnson.'
person_rows.notes: Belinda identified as 'bouncy Hispanic woman' leaving house around time of murder. Questioned by police. At trial, prosecution presents her as alternative suspect, highlighting her desperation for show and statement 'I would kill' to be on it. Trial is covered on reality TV show. Prosecution uses her as reasonable doubt for Larry Johnson. She's cleared implicitly when Larry is charged, but suffers public accusation during televised trial. Severity 3 for public trial exposure and reputation damage via media.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Dr. Walter Benning, a late-term abortion provider, is murdered during church service. Detectives investigate several suspects including Jonah Leland (boyfriend of patient), Kevin Morton (grandfather of patient's fetus), and ultimately Wayne Grogan, a pro-life protestor who confesses. During investigation, Jonah Leland and Kevin Morton are both publicly accused/questioned but proven innocent. Morton was publicly confronted at his workplace (university) and interrogated regarding the murder. Jonah was also publicly questioned at his workplace in front of his boss.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: JONAH: 'Look, I got to get back to work. My boss is looking at me funny.' Context: Detectives questioned him at his workplace about the murder, visibly in front of his employer.
person_rows.notes: Detectives questioned Jonah at his workplace about allegedly calling Dr. Benning repeatedly and being angry about girlfriend's abortion. He denies involvement. His boss observed the police interaction. Later proven innocent when Wayne Grogan confessed.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: LUPO: 'You were scared off by one of the crazies?' / BERNARD: 'You probably think the guy that you talked to saved your grandson's life. Yeah, he may have, but he's also a killer, and there's no reason to think that he won't kill again.'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Scene where detectives pull Kevin Morton aside at university ('Professor Morton? Excuse me.') and interrogate him about the murder. MORTON: 'This is ridiculous. I'm not a killer. I'm a science teacher.' Detectives confront him about lying regarding his whereabouts and his car being chalked at the clinic.
person_rows.notes: Kevin Morton was publicly questioned at his university workplace by detectives. They caught him in a lie about going to the clinic, found his car had been chalked there, and pressured him to reveal what Wayne Grogan told him. He was clearly treated as a suspect. Detectives used coercive tactics suggesting the person he spoke to was a killer. Later proven innocent when Wayne Grogan confessed to the murder.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows Sid Maxwell/Todd Bissell murdered by Kerry Sands (insane); several people from FlashPosse website prosecuted for manslaughter. Detective Bernard publicly exposed for child support issues during trial.
episode_summary.summary: Sid Maxwell is found dead in apparent autoerotic asphyxiation. Investigation reveals he was murdered by Kerry Sands, a schizophrenic woman incited by the FlashPosse website that posted his address, building codes, and threats. During prosecution of website operators, defense attorney publicly exposes Detective Bernard's past child support failures using internet-sourced information, humiliating him in court. Bernard's personal life becomes evidence to attack his credibility.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney Margolis: 'Detective Bernard, can you tell us who's in this picture?' Shows graduation photo with woman. 'Did you and she have a child together?' Bernard: 'Yes.' 'Did you sign a child support agreement... and then fail to send that money three times in the first six months?' 'There was a question about paternity.' 'If you lied to the mother of your child about supporting your son, why should we believe anything you say to us now?'
person_rows.notes: Defense attorney uses internet-sourced information about Bernard's past child support issues to attack his credibility as a witness in open court. Bernard is accused of lying/being untrustworthy, but the transcript shows he had legitimate paternity questions and paid 'every month' once blood test confirmed. The public humiliation in courtroom qualifies as exposure harm. Not accused of a specific crime formally, but his credibility/character publicly attacked as if he were dishonest. This undermines the prosecution's case and humiliates Bernard professionally.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives question Bonnie about Maxwell's car vandalism after finding threatening texts between them. She admits vandalizing the car ('Text this, scumbag!') but her prints don't match the car. Later transcript shows prints belong to Todd Bissell (Maxwell's old identity), clearing her. Investigation moves to website members.
person_rows.notes: Bonnie Allman was initially suspected of Maxwell's murder due to hostile text exchanges and car vandalism she admitted to. However, forensics proved the vandalism prints were Maxwell's own (under his old identity), clearing her. No indication questioning occurred publicly or that she suffered consequences beyond private police interview. Does not meet threshold for inclusion as exposure remained police_only with no material harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows investigation of Nina Wilshere murder by Mexican cartel-trained child assassins. Focus on Rafael Alvarez's prosecution and cooperation.
episode_summary.summary: Nina Wilshere is murdered by cartel-trained teenage assassins. Investigation initially focuses on chiropractor Lucas Evra, who owed money and had confrontation with victim. He is cleared when police discover Nina was a marijuana dealer targeted by rival cartel. Episode examines prosecution of juvenile killers and witness intimidation.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confront Evra at his chiropractic office about money dispute with victim. Building surveillance shows heated confrontation day before murder. EVRA: 'I ran into Nina in the Hamptons last summer... Next thing, I'm coughing up a retainer. Then, she blows me off for three appointments... If she didn't want the job, I wanted my money back.' DETECTIVE: 'Looked like a big deal on the video.' EVRA: 'No, guys, 5 grand wasn't going to break me... if you'll excuse me, I've got patients.'
person_rows.notes: Evra was caught on building surveillance video having heated argument with victim day before murder over $5,000 retainer. Detectives questioned him at his medical practice in front of patients. Cleared when investigation revealed victim was marijuana dealer targeted by drug cartel, not killed over business dispute. His fingerprints on door led to initial suspicion from prior DWI arrest in system.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Arraignment scene: ADA RUBIROSA: 'People v. Rafael Alvarez, Samuel Molina and Carlos Cabezas. Murder in the Second Degree.' Later, JUDGE: 'In light of the court's ruling, I move to dismiss all charges against my clients for lack of evidence.' ADA CUTTER: 'We have video placing Alvarez and Molina at the crime scene.' JUDGE: 'I'll sustain the indictments against Alvarez and Molina. But, I'm dismissing all charges against Mr. Cabezas.' CUTTER: 'Okay, we lost the driver of the getaway car, but we still have the shooters.'
person_rows.notes: 14-year-old arrested and indicted for murder as part of cartel assassination team. Identified as getaway driver. Held at Bridges juvenile facility pending trial. All charges dismissed when judge suppressed evidence from search warrant (ruled 90% of currency has cocaine residue, invalidating probable cause). Prosecution explicitly states video only places other two defendants at scene, not Cabezas. Released after public arraignment and pretrial detention.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Brenda Sawyer is accused of drunk driving after a fatal crash killing 7 people (including 3 children). Public and family believe she was intoxicated (.09 BAC, grain alcohol bottle found). Later proven she was drugged with Propofol by coworker Zach Marshall, who wanted to discredit her whistleblowing about Lextenda (a cancer drug). Marshall intended a minor accident/DWI to ruin her credibility with FDA, but it resulted in mass fatalities. Brenda suffered extreme public vilification, family accusations, media coverage, and died from injuries - all while innocent of drunk driving.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BOB RENQUIST: 'Your drunk wife killed our babies!' MATT SAWYER: 'No! She killed them. No. She wasn't drunk. Bob, I swear.' ... Later ME confirms: 'Blood alcohol was .09' and grain alcohol bottle found. Public believes she was drunk driver. Transcript shows: 'Miss Sawyer's blood alcohol has already been leaked to the media. With six fatalities, including three dead kids, people are gonna want to string her up.' ... Later proven via autopsy: 'Propofol? An anesthetic... It would cause immediate disorientation, and lack of control if she sprayed it in her nose.' Marshall confession: 'I just wanted her to get pulled over. The police would think she was drunk, she'd get a DWI, she'd get fired and lose her credibility with the FDA.'
person_rows.notes: Brenda Sawyer died from injuries but was publicly vilified as a drunk driver who killed 7 people including 3 children. The entire community, her family (brother-in-law screamed she killed their babies), media, and investigators believed she was intoxicated. Blood alcohol .09 and grain alcohol bottle appeared to confirm drunk driving. Later proven she was deliberately drugged with Propofol in her nasal spray by coworker Zach Marshall to discredit her FDA whistleblowing. Marshall also planted the alcohol bottle. She was about to expose illegal marketing of Lextenda cancer drug. Her death occurred while under this false accusation, making this the ultimate consequence of being wrongly accused. Media leak mentioned explicitly. Husband became 'pariah' due to association. Severity 4: death while publicly accused of killing children.

episode_summary.source_notes: Complex episode involving defense attorney Marcus Woll conspiring to murder witnesses across multiple cases spanning 5 years
episode_summary.summary: Defense attorney Marcus Woll (former ADA) systematically murders or arranges murders of witnesses who threaten his cases. Henry Lovett, a pharmacist who could have cleared Alvin 'Mad Dog' Jackson of murder, was killed after Woll obtained his location. Maggie Hayes was murdered before testifying against Woll's client William Avery. Jackson was wrongfully convicted and served 5+ years. The episode also features failed attempt to kill witness Paige Regan.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: JACKSON: 'Oh, he got me, too. It made a pretty nice little headline.' [...] 'Mr. Jackson, you are currently serving a prison sentence of 25-years-to-life because you were tried and convicted of murder, is that correct?' 'That's correct.'
person_rows.notes: Jackson was wrongfully convicted of murdering his own lieutenant. The actual killer was Eddie Rice, a rival drug dealer. Defense witness Henry Lovett, a pharmacist who saw the real killer, was murdered before trial after prosecutor Marcus Woll obtained his location and passed it to Eddie Rice. Jackson served 25-years-to-life sentence. This is a clear case of wrongful conviction with life-altering consequences caused by prosecutorial misconduct (witness murder conspiracy).

episode_summary.source_notes: Three men enter Stan Harkavy's tax office for what appears to be a robbery. Stan shoots all three with his shotgun, killing two. Investigation reveals the 'robbery' was actually a shakedown orchestrated by Max Purcell to intimidate Stan into selling his building. Stan recognized one of the men (Duane Jefferson) from an earlier encounter but lied about it, suggesting he knew it wasn't a real robbery when he opened fire.
episode_summary.summary: This episode contains NO qualifying innocent people who suffered harm from false accusations. While Max Purcell was accused and charged, he was actually guilty of orchestrating the shakedown. Stan Harkavy killed two men who were not actually planning to rob him (it was intimidation), but the men themselves were willing participants in the criminal scheme and were not falsely accused. The episode explores Stan's potential guilt for an unjustified shooting, but no innocent person was harmed by false accusations.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Community organizer James Landy is murdered and initially believed to be a federal employee. Investigation reveals he was secretly videotaping Rights Alliance colleagues to expose potential extortion. Multiple suspects emerge: Jerry Ganz (Patriot Ranger who threatened Landy), Marissa Rumstead and Arturo Ramirez (Rights Alliance employees who disposed of evidence), and ultimately Courtney Owens and Davis Webb (who ordered the killing to prevent exposure of their affair and embezzlement cover-up). Jerry Ganz is arrested, jailed, and publicly protested over before being cleared as the killer.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: In the hands of an anti-government activist, possession of that amount of toxin falls under the terrorism laws. Who do you think you are? Big Brother. And you're under arrest.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Free Jerry! Free Jerry! Free Jerry! [protesters with weapons confronting police during prisoner transport] ... You're letting socialist scum get away with murder while an innocent patriot rots in jail.
person_rows.notes: Ganz threatened Landy with a gun and took his camera, but did not kill him. Arrested for terrorism charges (rat poison possession), held in jail while supporters staged armed protests. Lab evidence (no blood/fluids) and timeline cleared him, but he was still prosecuted and accepted 5 years probation for robbery and obstruction. The case demonstrates severe consequences from false accusation despite eventual clearance - he faced terrorism charges, jail time, and public spectacle before being proven uninvolved in the murder.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows a blackmail scheme involving journalist Megan Kerik's research on Vanessa Carville's relationships with female employees. Multiple people accused during investigation.
episode_summary.summary: Journalist Megan Kerik is murdered while researching an exposé about TV personality Vanessa Carville's sexual relationships with female employees. Initial investigation focuses on Carville as a suspect due to motive to silence Kerik. Editor Dennis Di Palma is later revealed to have hijacked Kerik's research for blackmail, then arrested for extortion. Di Palma is initially suspected of murder before evidence points to his accomplice Derek Fanning. The case involves false accusations against Carville and temporary suspicion of Di Palma for murder before the real killer is identified.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: LUPO: 'So I'm a suspect?' VANESSA: 'I have to be.' Context: Detectives inform Carville that victim Megan Kerik was working on an exposé about her, making her a suspect. Later: 'If she killed Megan Kerik to keep her quiet, coming forward with a bogus blackmail story would be a pretty smart way to throw us off.'
person_rows.notes: Carville was suspected due to motive (silencing Kerik's exposé) but investigation remained police-only. She later became a cooperating witness/victim when blackmail scheme was revealed. No public exposure of murder suspicion occurred, and investigation shifted quickly to Di Palma and Fanning. Severity 1 because suspicion was private and brief.

episode_summary.source_notes: Triple homicide case involving Justin Sachs and Amanda Evans (aka Bonnie). Amanda claims amnesia and is found legally insane, then testifies against Justin.
episode_summary.summary: Justin Sachs and Amanda Evans (going by 'Bonnie') murder the Morgan family (5 victims). Amanda is found legally insane and pleads NGRI. She testifies against Justin, who is convicted. No innocent persons are wrongfully accused in this episode - the investigation correctly identifies the perpetrators from the start. Detective Lupes' past PTSD issues are revealed during cross-examination but are not related to a false accusation in this case.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Cesar Ramirez, a 16-year-old charter school student, is murdered by being set on fire. Investigation initially focuses on jealousy and school rivalries. Abel Dolan and three friends from Aaron Burr High School are eventually revealed as the killers. The case involves false alibis provided by Principal Woodside, a gang-rape of witness Jolie Henderson to silence her, and Moses Dolan (Abel's brother) initially providing then retracting a tip about evidence. Jolie Henderson emerges as a key false suspect who suffered severe consequences including gang-rape as retaliation for potential testimony.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives discover burn marks on sweatshirt in Jolie's apartment matching video evidence, making her a suspect. Later: 'Jolie... Did it happen because of Cesar? Because they don't want you to talk about that?' Principal Woodside accidentally tipped off the real murderers that police identified Jolie as witness, leading to gang-rape: 'if she believed those boys were innocent and she told them, you know, Good news. The police have found a witness who can clear you. She didn't realize what she was doing.'
person_rows.notes: Jolie was present at murder scene but not a perpetrator. She was friends with victim Cesar and dating suspect Abel Dolan. Police found burned sweatshirt in her apartment leading them to suspect her involvement. Principal Woodside inadvertently revealed Jolie as witness to the actual murderers (Abel Dolan and friends), who then gang-raped her to prevent testimony. Three boys participated in rape wearing condoms (planned). This is the most severe harm case - witness who becomes false suspect then suffers retaliatory gang-rape as consequence of police investigation revealing her as potential witness.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Detectives confront him about detailed knowledge of murder: 'How'd you know about that? Hmm? You got a magical view of 115th Street from your couch?' implying he must have been present
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Jay identified as jealous ex-boyfriend who had confronted Cesar about Jolie. When detectives visit: 'I got the swine flu... I mean, I don't go around firing people up anyway, you know? I mean, I didn't like Cesar, but, damn...' Detectives verify his alibi with doctor.
person_rows.notes: Jay Spivey was identified by Jolie as someone who had confronted Cesar about her. He had motive (jealousy) and was questioned at home while sick. Detectives used confrontational tactics suggesting he must have been present based on his knowledge of crime details. However, he had legitimate alibi (swine flu, doctor verification) and his knowledge came from seeing video circulating on cell phones. Cleared but subjected to accusatory questioning in his home with family present.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Justine Stebbins, a physical therapy student moonlighting as an escort, is murdered in a NYC hotel. Lab tech Robby Vickery, who dated her, is initially arrested. He's later cleared when evidence points to grad student Alex Conway, who was robbing escorts to fund gambling. Vickery was publicly arrested at work and interrogated before being cleared.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Scene: Detectives arrest Vickery publicly at his lab: 'How many times do I have to tell you guys, you're supposed to feed the rats on schedule or they get sick! Robby, put the rat down. Put the rat in the cage. Now! You're under arrest, Robby.' Later cleared when Conway's involvement is proven.
person_rows.notes: Vickery was arrested in front of colleagues at Chelsea University medical research lab. He bragged about dating Justine, which made him initial suspect. Rat feces evidence from his shoe placed him at crime scene. He was jailed, then released and charges dismissed after Conway was identified as real killer. Prosecutor fulfilled promise to dismiss charges after Vickery cooperated by wearing wire on Conway.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows complex case with Innocence Coalition challenge to conviction; main defendant Cedric Stuber ultimately pleads guilty to man one (25 years). Multiple layers including witness credibility issues, potential alternative suspect Jon Sorrentino, and prosecutor Mike Cutter's license controversy.
episode_summary.summary: Cedric Stuber is arrested and convicted for a gay-bashing murder based on eyewitness ID. The Innocence Coalition challenges the conviction with new evidence suggesting the victim's husband Jon Sorrentino may have arranged the killing. Investigation reveals the Coalition's witness (drug dealer Rodrigo Diaz) was likely lying in retaliation. Stuber is ultimately not innocent but Jon Sorrentino was also falsely accused of hiring a hit man during the investigation. The episode explores themes of wrongful accusation, prosecutorial conduct, and the complexity of determining innocence.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Cutter to Sorrentino: 'You want to get rid of your husband, a few weeks later he winds up dead.' and 'You can help us out with that [arresting Rodrigo].'
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cutter accuses Sorrentino: 'You want to get rid of your husband, a few weeks later he winds up dead.' Sorrentino responds: 'Alan was killed by Cedric Stuber. He was yelling gay epithets. This was a hate crime.' Later asks: 'Do I need a lawyer?' Cutter: 'If I had something to hide, I'd make a phone call.'
person_rows.notes: Jon Sorrentino was the prosecution's star witness who identified Cedric Stuber. However, when the Innocence Coalition produced witness Rodrigo Diaz claiming Jon solicited a hit man, Jon became a murder suspect himself. He was brought in for questioning where Cutter aggressively interrogated him about hiring someone to kill his husband Alan. The accusation was based on Diaz's testimony (later proven unreliable - Diaz was angry at Jon for threatening to report him to police for drug dealing, and was paid drinks/cash by law students). Jon was proven innocent when investigation revealed Diaz's motive to lie and lack of corroboration. While exposure was limited to police interrogation, the accusation of arranging his own husband's murder in a potential conspiracy qualifies as severity 2 (public accusation by authorities of a serious crime, even if not formally charged). Ultimately Stuber pleaded guilty, confirming Jon's original identification was correct.

episode_summary.source_notes: Four police officers shot in pizzeria; shooter Calvin Stokes arrested. Investigation reveals Officers Ramos and Colson were running an informant operation, pressuring Stokes.
episode_summary.summary: Calvin Stokes kills four police officers. During investigation, detectives briefly suspect and publicly question an innocent man who had stolen a BMW near the crime scene. The suspect is brought in for lineup, interrogated, tested for gunshot residue, and witnesses (including a widow of one of the victims) are brought to identify him before he is cleared. This public process occurs in front of multiple officers and the widow.

person_rows.consequence_detail: arrested, interrogated, lineup, GSR tested, publicly questioned in front of victim's widow and officers
person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You don't like cops, do you? / Where'd you ditch the weapon?
person_rows.quote_or_scene: LUPO: 'You did five years for felony gun possession and resisting arrest. You don't like cops, do you?' / SUSPECT: 'You want to talk about a stolen car, we'll talk. The rest of this is just wrong place, wrong time.' / LUPO: 'You got that right.' ... MRS. COLSON (widow): 'Is that the man? Did he kill my husband?' ... KAPLAN (witness): 'I don't think so. I don't see him.' ... Later: 'The counterman couldn't make an ID either. Plus, he was negative for gunshot residue.'
person_rows.notes: Man arrested for car theft near murder scene. Detectives strongly implied he was the cop killer. Subjected to lineup where victim's widow was brought to identify him. Interrogated with accusatory questions. Tested for gunshot residue. Cleared when witnesses could not identify him and GSR test negative. Public exposure occurred in front of widow, witnesses, and multiple police officers from the precinct.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom testimony
episode_summary.summary: Dr. Oscar Silva is poisoned at a climate symposium. Initially, Dr. Everett Brown is suspected due to stolen emails showing data manipulation and Brown's statement against cap-and-trade that benefited Silva financially. Investigation reveals Silva's ex-wife Dana and her father Nelson Lehman as the true conspirators, with Dana's current husband Phillip Shoemaker also involved. Brown was publicly accused and threatened despite being innocent.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: Did you tell that to Dr. Silva? [...] not to mention it getting out that you were monkeying with your data.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Brown: 'He said he'd ruin me if I didn't come out publicly against cap and trade. [...] He threatened to send the e-mails to the media if I went to the police. I was scared.' Detectives treating him as suspect: 'Maybe you thought the extortion had just begun.' Brown: 'I did not poison him. I swear.'
person_rows.notes: Brown was accused based on stolen emails showing data manipulation and his suspicious public statement against cap-and-trade that benefited Silva financially. Detectives confronted him at work/conference setting. He was blackmailed by Silva but proven innocent when investigation shifted to Shoemaker/Lehman. Police used coercive tactics suggesting he poisoned Silva to stop extortion. No formal charges but clear public accusation in professional context.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Brenna Lane is murdered after trying to expose her brother Dustin's inappropriate relationship with Senator Peterson's wife, Camille. Initially, police suspect Dustin of killing Brenna out of jealousy/obsession. Evidence is gathered via a terrorism-justified search warrant (later suppressed). Andrea Wheaton, the senator's chief of staff, is arrested for the murder after cat hair from the crime scene is found in a rental car she procured. Andrea falsely confesses to an affair with Dustin to protect Camille Peterson. Eventually, Camille confesses to killing Brenna to prevent her from exposing the affair and destroying her relationship with Dustin. Andrea served as an accessory after the fact, cleaning up the crime scene and planting evidence in Dustin's apartment to frame him.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: But you wanted to touch her... Maybe she was rocking that body around the house, working those modeling moves... Were you involved with her sexually? You were kids experimenting, maybe... When you saw your sister flirting with that old man, it must've done a number on you... You wanted to hook up with her that night, didn't you? / You got a dirty mouth. I'm not talking to you anymore.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Judge: 'Neither the FBI nor the Queens DA found any evidence that Mr. Henry had ties to terrorist organizations... What disturbs me more than the threat of terrorism is the misuse of that word to bypass due process. I'm granting the defendant's motion.' Defense attorney: 'Incarceration has been difficult on him, Your Honor. He's under suicide watch.' Later, Camille Peterson confesses: 'Andrea told me to go home. She said she'd come over and clean up the mess. And she was the one who planted the evidence in your apartment.'
person_rows.notes: Dustin was arrested and held in jail with suicide watch after physical evidence (blood, cat litter, lingerie receipt) was planted in his apartment by Andrea Wheaton on Camille Peterson's instructions. The search warrant was obtained under false pretenses (terrorism threat after FBI ruled it out), leading to suppression of evidence. However, he remained incarcerated and was publicly accused at his workplace. His employer expressed disbelief at the accusation. The detectives used highly coercive interrogation tactics implying incestuous relationship with his sister. Andrea Wheaton falsely confessed to protect Camille Peterson, further extending Dustin's exposure. He was eventually cleared when Camille confessed to the actual murder.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detective: 'We arrested your girlfriend, Andrea, for murder. Police found cat hairs in a car she rented that were a DNA match for the cat in Brenna's apartment.' Defense attorney: 'If he agrees to testify against Andrea, will you drop the charges?' Later, Andrea falsely confesses: 'It was me. I was having an affair with Dustin Henry... Dustin must've gone crazy. I'm calling a lawyer.' Eventually: 'Andrea took five years as an accessory' [not murder].
person_rows.notes: Andrea was arrested for Brenna's murder based on cat hair evidence in a rental car she procured. She falsely confessed to having an affair with Dustin (when it was actually Camille Peterson) to protect her boss's wife. She was preparing for trial when Camille eventually confessed to the actual murder. Andrea pled guilty only to accessory after the fact (5 years) for cleaning up the crime scene and planting evidence, not to murder. She was innocent of the murder charge itself but complicit in the cover-up. Given she actively participated in framing Dustin and made a false confession to protect Camille, her inclusion is marginal, but she was publicly arrested and charged with murder before the truth emerged.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detective to Senator's chief of staff: 'Well, you tell us. These two crashed your reception and honed in on your boss. And Brenna Lane, she called your office before and after the party... all roads lead here... the senator might want to get out in front of this if anything was going on.' Chief of staff: 'I can assure you the senator had nothing to do with her. He's been happily married for 30 years. He's been vetted by some of the toughest political operatives on K Street.' Later, to Senator directly: 'Where were you last Monday, Senator?... For a second, I thought he was gonna smack her... I give you my word as a gentleman, there was nothing going between me and that girl.'
person_rows.notes: Senator Peterson was investigated as a potential suspect when Brenna Lane repeatedly tried to contact him and crashed his Gracie Mansion reception. Police questioned him and his chief of staff about a possible affair with Brenna. His chief of staff emphasized his reputation and vetting. While questioned at his home with his wife present, this was clearly a public political exposure given his position. The investigation threatened his political career and marriage. He was ultimately cleared when it emerged the affair was between his wife Camille and Dustin Henry, not between him and Brenna. The consequence severity is rated 2 because he was publicly questioned as a U.S. Senator in a murder investigation but not formally charged or arrested.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through courtroom scenes and final dialogue.
episode_summary.summary: Randy Colwyn is initially accused of murdering his cousin Annie Douglas via heroin overdose to benefit from inheritance; he is later proven to have supplied drugs but not murdered her. Catherine Douglas, Bruce Graham, Dr. Balicheck, Roger Weil, and Louise Casey are arrested for murder conspiracy related to deaths timed for 2010 estate tax avoidance. Charges against Casey and Weil are dismissed; Dr. Balicheck is revealed as an unwitting participant and charges dropped. The episode focuses on proving Catherine Douglas orchestrated her father's death through accountant Graham and Dr. Balicheck's clinic.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: He gave her the drugs that did kill her. Two plus two, he's looking at criminally negligent homicide.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detectives confronted Randy and his lawyer: 'He scores the dope, they shoot up, his cousin is dying. And he sends her home with Jeeves.' Randy is arrested and interrogated. ADA later offers plea: 'Criminal facilitation. Probation.' Final deal: 'Six months' jail time.
person_rows.notes: Randy was accused of murder for financial gain (inheritance). He DID supply the heroin and cleaned up the scene, showing consciousness of guilt, but prosecutors acknowledged 'it was a weak case' for murder. He is partially involved in causing Annie's death through drug facilitation but innocent of intentional murder. The accusation was public (lawyer present, detectives made statements) and resulted in arrest and jail time via plea deal.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cutter to Balicheck: 'You didn't do anything wrong. You treated Henry Douglas and Roger Weil's mother to the best of your ability... And now we believe you.' Balicheck explains: 'It was Graham. He asked me to keep the man alive by any means necessary until January... If I didn't do it, he'd pull out the patients that he'd already brought me.'
person_rows.notes: Dr. Balicheck was arrested and charged with three counts of second-degree murder as part of alleged conspiracy. Prosecution theory: he was a 'hit man' killing wealthy patients on schedule for 2010 tax benefits. Reality: he was manipulated by Graham, didn't know he was part of murder scheme, and genuinely tried to treat patients. ADA Cutter explicitly cleared him: 'We've dropped the charges against you. You didn't do anything wrong.' Balicheck became prosecution witness against Graham. His arrest was public (courtroom scene with all defendants present), causing severe professional reputation damage to an already controversial doctor.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney in court: 'As to defendant Casey, the charge is that she prolonged her father's life for a mercenary reason. Now, I can't find any statute that makes it a crime to prolong a life for any reason.' Judge: 'No sale, Mr. Cutter. Charge against Ms. Casey is dismissed.'
person_rows.notes: Louise Casey was arrested and charged with one count of second-degree murder for allegedly conspiring to kill her father. The charge was legally unsupportable: her father lived LONGER than expected (into 2010), not shorter. Judge dismissed charges, noting no statute makes it a crime to prolong life for any reason. This was clear prosecutorial overreach. Casey suffered arrest, public courtroom exposure with multiple co-defendants, and the stigma of a murder charge before being cleared.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: Cutter after severance ruling: 'The charge against Casey was never going to hold. We don't have a witness against Weil.' Court scene shows Weil arrested with group: 'This is outrageous.' His lawyer: 'I'm trying to reach my lawyer. Nobody say a word.'
person_rows.notes: Roger Weil was arrested and charged with one count of second-degree murder for allegedly conspiring in his mother's death at Balicheck's clinic. Prosecution admitted they had 'no witness against Weil,' indicating weak/no evidence. After severance motion granted, Weil's case was separated and presumably dropped (prosecution focused on Catherine Douglas case). Weil suffered public arrest with other defendants, courtroom exposure, and murder accusation despite lack of evidence. Strongly implied innocent based on prosecution's own admission of no evidence.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete with full courtroom scenes and resolution
episode_summary.summary: Jerome Turner is murdered by his cousin Michael Reed over a dispute about cooperating with Hema Labs regarding DNA testing of Jerome's son Jaden. While Joseph Hernandez (the PI hired by Hema) is investigated as a suspect, the evidence shows Michael Reed committed the murder out of anger that Jerome was accepting payment from the company that had exploited their family for decades. No one is proven or strongly implied innocent - Michael Reed is the actual perpetrator, and Hernandez, while morally culpable for his tactics, did not commit the murder. The case resolves with a plea deal and settlement for the Robinson family.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through verdict. Final dialogue from Marielle Di Napoli cut off mid-sentence.
episode_summary.summary: David Di Napoli is murdered in his home, stabbed in a dog cage. Wife Marielle is initially suspected and arrested. She's publicly accused, tried in media coverage, and eventually exonerated when evidence points to Josh Felner, who murdered David to protect an asset-hiding divorce scheme. Marielle suffered arrest, public exposure, trial preparation before charges dismissed. Episode also involves wrongful suspicion of Marty Ashcroft briefly.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BERNARD: 'Two murdered husbands? Come on.' Detectives pressure her repeatedly about killing both husbands, use accusatory tone throughout interrogations.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: JUDGE: 'Case number 46894. People v. Marielle Di Napoli. Charge is Murder in the Second Degree.' [...] CUTTER: 'Case dismissed.' She was formally arrested, arraigned in open court, and prosecuted before being exonerated when evidence proved Josh Felner was the killer.
person_rows.notes: Marielle was arrested and prosecuted in open court for murdering her husband David. The arraignment was public ('Case number 46894'), she had defense counsel, and was treated as a murder defendant. She was later proven innocent when Josh Felner was convicted of the murder. Her public exposure included courtroom proceedings, likely media coverage (reality TV crew was at the scene), and community knowledge. She suffered arrest, legal process, and reputation damage before exoneration. Prosecution dropped charges after Ashcroft's testimony and evidence pointed to Felner. While Marielle had unconventional personal life, she was genuinely innocent of the murder accusation.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: BERNARD: 'This would be a really good time for you to explain some things.' LUPO: 'The lab will tell us tomorrow if it's [David's blood], unless you'd like to tell us now.' Detectives use possession of comics and blood evidence to pressure confession.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: MARIELLE: 'He killed David! You bastard! [...] He killed my soul!' BERNARD: 'Drop that now.' [...] ASHCROFT: 'Where's that crazy bitch? You arrest her? She tried to kill me.' BERNARD: 'We'll deal with her later. Now we're dealing with you.' [...] Search warrant executed, found comics with blood. Ashcroft gives testimony implicating Felner, is not charged.
person_rows.notes: Ashcroft was briefly suspected when Marielle attacked him and he was found with David's comic books containing blood. Detectives executed search warrant and interrogated him as suspect. However, exposure appears limited to police/prosecution - no indication of public/workplace/family exposure. He was pressured to explain but ultimately became witness against Felner. Including because he was publicly accused by Marielle ('You killed David!') in front of police, and subjected to search warrant and coercive interrogation. Severity 2 for police confrontation and search, but no arrest or broader public exposure documented.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript shows detectives investigating a credible school shooting/bombing threat from a blogger called 'Moot.' Investigation leads to identifying Rick Benson, a teacher who was falsely accused of molesting a student.
episode_summary.summary: Rick Benson, a 9th grade English teacher with only 3 years experience, was accused of molesting a student after the student urinated in a classroom trashcan and Benson tried to stop him. The accusation was fabricated. Benson spent 8 months in a 'rubber room' (Temporary Reassignment Center) during proceedings. The complaint was eventually dismissed, but the Department of Education told him he'd never get tenure, effectively ending his teaching career. His girlfriend left him during the accusations. The experience drove him to plan a school shooting/bombing, which was thwarted by police before any deaths occurred.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: MS. SCOTT: 'During a class, a student asked permission to use the bathroom... Rick told the student to wait. So this kid, he was 16, he goes to the front of the class and pees in the trashcan. Rick tried to stop him. The kid accused him of molesting him. The Department started proceedings against Rick. He spent eight months in the rubber rooms. They dismissed the complaint but they told him he'd never get tenure. They might as well have fired him.'
person_rows.notes: Student fabricated molestation accusation after being stopped from urinating in classroom trashcan. Benson was reassigned to 'rubber room' for 8 months. Although complaint was dismissed, Department of Education told him he would never receive tenure, effectively ending his teaching career. His girlfriend also left him due to the accusation. The trauma led him to plan a school attack (stopped by police). This represents total career destruction and life-altering harm from a false accusation, even though the formal complaint was eventually dismissed.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: KOZLOWSKI: 'I did a little demonstration by passing the current over the arms of my students. Left a little redness on the skin. Next thing I know, there's a complaint filed that I branded crosses. I was suspended, pending an arbitration... After four months' suspension, I quit New York and got a job here. Half the salary, twice the commute, but at least I'm teaching.'
person_rows.notes: Science teacher accused of 'branding crosses' on students' arms when he was actually conducting a standard Tesla coil demonstration that left temporary redness. Was suspended for 4 months pending arbitration. After resolution, he quit NYC schools and took a job in Suffolk County at half the salary with twice the commute. Career significantly damaged by false characterization of standard science demonstration.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BENDEL: 'I was conducting a computer reading program, and this one student was on a game site. I told him, come on, you know better, get back to reading. He had his hair gelled straight up, and I swatted it, like this. I barely touched it.' [Later] 'Thirty years of teaching down the drain. Thirty years I never laid a hand on a kid.'
person_rows.notes: Teacher with 30 years of service accused of assault for barely touching a student's gelled-up hair when redirecting him from a game site back to reading. Ended up in rubber room. Describes his entire 30-year career as being 'down the drain.' Life-altering consequence for minimal physical contact that was not assault.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: TEACHER: 'I called him a little bastard, and a few other choice words.' BERNARD: 'Why would you do that?' TEACHER: 'Because he punched me and tried to take my eye out with a pair of scissors.'
person_rows.notes: Teacher accused of using abusive language on a student, but only did so after the student physically assaulted him and tried to take his eye out with scissors. Teacher was defending himself. Assignment to rubber room represents punishment for responding to violent assault. The teacher's use of language was reactive to being attacked, making the 'abusive language' charge a mischaracterization of self-defense response.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: MS. SCOTT: 'I'm an English teacher. I told him if he didn't use proper grammar, people will think he's stupid even if he isn't stupid. Of course, what the student heard is, Ms. Scott called me stupid. That got me a reprimand that'll be in my file till the end of time.'
person_rows.notes: English teacher gave educational advice about proper grammar usage, explaining that poor grammar makes people appear stupid even if they aren't. Student misunderstood/mischaracterized this as being called stupid directly. Received permanent reprimand in personnel file. While less severe than others, permanent record damage for teaching moment represents public workplace exposure and lasting career harm.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript appears complete through closing arguments. Episode focuses on Nicole Bell shooting Henry King, a celebrity accused of 40+ rapes who was released after appellate court vacated conviction due to prosecutor's immunity promise.
episode_summary.summary: Henry King, a celebrity released from prison after rape conviction was overturned, is murdered. Nicole Bell, one of his rape victims, is arrested and confesses to the killing. The episode centers on whether her actions constitute murder or justifiable homicide. No innocent people are falsely accused in this episode - the investigation correctly identifies Nicole Bell as the shooter, and she confesses. The moral/legal conflict is whether a rape victim killing her rapist should be prosecuted, not whether an innocent person was wrongly accused.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Kyle Morrison is murdered in Central Park. Nina Ellis, his fiancée and CEO of Hythena (a medical testing company), is arrested and tried for his murder. During trial, Nina claims she was a victim of domestic abuse by Kyle and killed him in self-defense. The prosecution proves Nina fabricated the abuse claims - her broken arm came from falling off a horse during polo practice, not from Kyle's violence. Derek Seaver, the former CTO who was initially suspected and nearly framed by Nina, is proven innocent when investigators discover Nina called in a false tip to implicate him.

person_rows.quote_or_scene: BERNARD: 'She tried to frame a former employee for the murder. She actually called the Scarsdale PD, said she saw some guy in a blue Tesla drop a gun in the sewer.' [...] Discussion of finding gun in sewer on Saratoga Drive, a block from where Derek Seaver lives, matching his blue Tesla.
person_rows.notes: Nina Ellis attempted to frame Derek Seaver by calling police with a false tip about seeing someone in a blue Tesla (matching Seaver's car) dump a gun in a sewer near his home. Seaver was investigated but cleared when detectives realized Nina was the actual killer. The framing attempt was part of Nina's cover-up. Seaver had quit the company after discovering the fraudulent test results, so Nina had motive to eliminate him as a witness and frame him for Kyle's murder.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Amanda Larson, a social media influencer, goes missing in NYC. Joseph Denzig is initially arrested as detectives find Amanda's driver's license in his closet and his blood at the crime scene. He's publicly arrested and accused of involvement but is later cleared when he explains he was burglarizing her van and witnessed someone else abduct Amanda. The real perpetrator, Daniel Garrett, is subsequently identified and convicted. Denzig faces arrest, interrogation, and public exposure for crimes he didn't commit (kidnapping/murder), though he did commit burglary.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: We don't have time for 20 questions. Where's the girl? [...] Tell us where she is, or I swear to God!
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Detective finds Amanda's driver's license in Denzig's closet: 'Are you familiar with a woman named Amanda Larson?' 'Never heard of her.' 'That's strange, 'cause I just found her driver's license in your closet. Turn around. Turn around! Let's go.' Later: 'Your blood is all over Amanda Larson's van.' Denzig explains: 'I used a hammer to smash the window on her van. When I reached in and unlocked the door, I sliced my wrist.' He witnessed the actual abduction: 'Right before I broke in, I saw her coming down the street. She walked up to the van, but a guy came out of nowhere and started yelling at her. [...] he grabbed her and dragged her away.'
person_rows.notes: Denzig is innocent of kidnapping/murder (the accusations leveled at him with threats during interrogation) but guilty of burglary. He was arrested on-screen, threatened during interrogation, and his arrest would be part of the public record in a high-profile missing persons case covered by media/social media. The detectives' aggressive interrogation ('I swear to God!') and immediate arrest constitute public exposure and legal consequences. He is cleared when his testimony leads to the real perpetrator. Partially involved in that he committed burglary, but the harm from being accused of kidnapping/murder far outweighs his actual crime.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Judge David Keating is murdered in a parking garage. Investigation reveals Lucy McDaniel, a world-class tennis player, killed him after he refused to allow her to marry her boyfriend. The case exposes that Lucy was under an exploitative guardianship controlled by her father and the judge. Greg Wallace and Jesse Weisman ('Dr. Weisman') were initially suspected but cleared. Wallace was publicly confronted and investigated; Weisman was present at the crime scene and threatened the judge but had been paid. Both experienced consequences from being falsely suspected.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: What I think is you found out where Keating lived, learned his schedule, and went there to finish what you started on the courthouse steps.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Wallace admits he followed the judge to a hotel after confronting him on courthouse steps. Detectives confront him with cell phone evidence placing him near the crime scene. He explains he was with his son violating visitation order. Receipts confirm alibi.
person_rows.notes: Wallace had genuine grievance against judge over custody ruling and threatened him publicly. Was accused based on witness (Clara Newhall) seeing confrontation, his motive, and cell phone placing him near scene. Cleared when movie/ice cream receipts confirmed he was with son during murder. Detectives used coercive interrogation tactics.

person_rows.police_conduct_quote: You were at the garage where Judge Keating was killed. That's you. No question about it. You also threatened the guy.
person_rows.quote_or_scene: Defense attorney cross-examines Weisman in court, bringing up his threatening text ('Need to get paid by tomorrow or this ain't gonna end well'), his presence at crime scene, his assault convictions. Attorney argues: 'He's the only person in this damn courtroom who had the motive, means, and opportunity to actually kill Judge Keating.' Weisman testifies he was paid in full and held door open for woman (Lucy) entering garage.
person_rows.notes: Weisman (a bookie/loan shark) was owed $100K by the judge and sent threatening text day before murder. He met judge at garage to collect payment and was leaving just as Lucy arrived. His prior assault convictions made him appear violent. Defense publicly accused him during trial to create reasonable doubt. He was proven innocent because he received full payment and left before murder occurred, plus his description of the woman he saw matched Lucy.

episode_summary.source_notes: Transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Derek Hoyt, a family man planning to run for Congress, is pushed in front of a bus and killed. The murder is committed by Manny Lopez, a follower of right-wing media mogul Jordan Reed. Reed had orchestrated a smear campaign against Hoyt, posting fake photos depicting him as a pedophile and posting inflammatory lies to incite his followers. Lopez believed the lies and killed Hoyt thinking he was protecting children. Reed is ultimately convicted of Murder Two under a depraved indifference theory. While multiple people were accused during the investigation (the business rival, etc.), none were publicly exposed or materially harmed by false accusations - they were quickly cleared through standard questioning. The case centers on incitement to violence rather than wrongful accusation.

episode_summary.source_notes: Full transcript provided
episode_summary.summary: Head of School Henry Gleason is murdered at Davenport prep school. Cooper Young, a scholarship student, becomes initial suspect after being found running from scene with blood on jacket. He is publicly arrested and held before his lawyer intervenes.
